  5. The software routine ______ diagnostic checks during startup.

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write five additional present simple sentences describing the function of a system or subsystem.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Passive Voice in Technical Description
docs.text:
Rewrite the sentences using the passive voice where appropriate.

  1. The technician secures the module with four mounting screws.
  2. The test team performed the calibration before the environmental trial.
  3. The maintenance staff replaced the damaged connector.
  4. The design team integrated an additional protection layer.
  5. The operator initiates the verification sequence from the main panel.

docs.title: Review Focus
docs.text: The learner should understand that the passive voice is often used when the action matters more than the actor, but it should not be overused.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Shall, Must, Should, May
docs.text:
Choose the most suitable modal verb.

  1. The unit ______ withstand vibration levels defined in the specification.
  2. The enclosure ______ be opened while the power supply is connected.
  3. The technician ______ verify alignment before final tightening.
  4. Alternative materials ______ be used subject to approval.
  5. The event log ______ be retained for subsequent analysis.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text: Explain why each modal verb is appropriate in its sentence.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Conditional Logic in Procedures
docs.text:
Complete the procedural statements.

  1. If the system __ (detect) an internal fault, the alarm sequence ____ (start) automatically.
  2. If the connector ______ (be) not fully seated, communication instability may occur.
  3. If abnormal vibration __ (persist), the unit should ____ (remove) from service.
  4. If the test result __ (fall) outside the specified range, the sample shall ____ (retest).

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write two additional conditional sentences suitable for a maintenance or safety procedure.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Relative Clauses for Precision
docs.text:
Combine the sentences into one more precise sentence using a relative clause.

  1. The cable was replaced. The cable showed visible insulation damage.
  2. The module is mounted on the rear panel. The module controls the acquisition sequence.
  3. The procedure was revised. The procedure previously allowed incomplete documentation.
  4. The test article failed during vibration exposure. The test article had passed the electrical check.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: Relative clauses are used to reduce fragmentation and improve technical precision.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Sequencing and Process Grammar
docs.text: Reorder the steps and rewrite them as a coherent process paragraph: - perform the final inspection; - disconnect the power source; - open the access panel; - replace the damaged connector; - verify the interface alignment; - restore power and conduct a function check.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Use sequencing language such as: - first, - prior to, - once completed, - subsequently, - after verification, - finally.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Error Correction
docs.text:
Correct the grammatical and stylistic problems in the paragraph below.

The unit have been installed on the left support bracket and are connected to a 24 V source. If abnormal noise will occur, the operator must to stop the system and should checks the mounting points. The damaged parts was replaced and the operation restore after the inspection.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Rewrite the paragraph in correct professional English.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Nominal vs Verbal Style
docs.text:
Rewrite the sentences in a more natural technical style.

  1. The performance of an inspection of the cable routing was carried out.
  2. The execution of calibration was completed before testing.
  3. A verification of the alignment of the bracket was performed.
  4. The implementation of the update improved stability.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The learner should avoid excessive nominalization where a more direct verb structure improves clarity.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Requirement Rewriting
docs.text:
Rewrite the following informal instructions as formal requirement statements.

  1. The battery has to last at least ten hours.
  2. You should protect the cable from abrasion.
  3. It is allowed to use another material if approved.
  4. Don’t use the unit outside the specified temperature range.

docs.title: Suggested Output Style
docs.text: Use a controlled specification tone.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Grammar for Documentation Reuse
docs.text: Write a short paragraph of 90 to 120 words describing a subsystem. Your paragraph must include: - at least two present simple sentences; - one passive construction; - one requirement sentence using shall or must; - one conditional sentence; - one relative clause.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: The paragraph should be suitable for later reuse in: - a module page; - a system-description template; - a review-playbook example.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Maintenance and troubleshooting communication requires clarity, sequence control, and evidence-based reasoning. The engineer or technician must explain what was observed, what was checked, what was found, what action was taken, and what remains open. This page develops the language needed to communicate faults and maintenance actions professionally.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Symptom, Cause, Action
docs.text: For each item below, identify: - the symptom; - the possible cause; - a plausible corrective action.

docs.title: Items
docs.text:
1. intermittent loss of communication;
  2. abnormal wear on cable guide;
  3. repeated alignment difficulty during installation;
  4. elevated surface temperature on enclosure;
  5. delayed actuator response at upper temperature limit.

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write one short diagnostic sentence for each case.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Sequencing a Troubleshooting Narrative
docs.text: Reorder the following elements into a logical troubleshooting paragraph: - communication restored after re-securing connector; - intermittent data loss observed; - initial power check confirmed nominal supply; - loose connector identified at interface panel; - no abnormal temperature increase detected.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: Write a coherent fault narrative of 90 to 130 words.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Maintenance Log Entry
docs.text: Write a maintenance log entry using the information below: - date not required; - scheduled inspection; - cable support bracket showed wear; - bracket replaced; - alignment verified; - function check passed; - next inspection recommended after 100 operating hours.

docs.title: Tone
docs.text: Concise, traceable, professional.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Troubleshooting Questions
docs.text: For each situation, write three troubleshooting questions that a maintenance engineer should ask.

docs.title: Situations
docs.text:
1. actuator response slower than expected;
  2. event log not synchronizing with workstation;
  3. abnormal vibration detected during operation;
  4. display interface intermittently blank.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The questions should be diagnostic, not generic.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Root Cause Discussion
docs.text: Write a short root cause discussion based on the following: - repeated loosening of connector after vibration exposure; - connector itself not defective; - retention method likely insufficient; - redesign of retention bracket recommended.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Write 100 to 140 words using analytical language.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Corrective vs Preventive Action
docs.text: Distinguish between corrective and preventive action for the following cases: 1. loose connector found during testing; 2. wear observed on cable routing support; 3. incomplete installation record identified; 4. repeated misalignment during assembly.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: For each case, write: - one corrective action; - one preventive action.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Maintenance Procedure Writing
docs.text: Write a short maintenance procedure for replacing a damaged connector.

docs.title: Required Steps
docs.text:
• isolate power;
  • access panel removal;
  • connector removal;
  • inspection of mating surfaces;
  • installation of replacement connector;
  • alignment check;
  • function verification;
  • documentation update.

docs.title: Output Style
docs.text: Use controlled procedural English.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Troubleshooting Table
docs.text:
Create a troubleshooting table with four columns: - symptom; - probable cause; - diagnostic action; - corrective action.

Populate the table with at least five realistic entries.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Review a Weak Maintenance Note
docs.text:
Improve the note below.

The unit had a problem and we checked it. A connector was not okay, so we fixed it. Then it worked again.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. identify weak wording;
  2. rewrite as a professional maintenance note;
  3. add one recommendation for future inspection.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Applied Maintenance Case
docs.text: Write a short case study of 180 to 250 words describing a maintenance intervention involving: - detected anomaly; - inspection process; - identified cause; - corrective action; - verification result; - recommendation for future monitoring.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: The final text should be strong enough to be reused later as: - a module example; - a model answer; - a troubleshooting template example.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Engineering professionals often need to communicate orally in situations that require precision, concision, and controlled terminology. Typical situations include briefings, design reviews, handovers, maintenance updates, test summaries, and technical coordination meetings.

The purpose of this page is not to train casual conversation. It is to train spoken professional English for technical communication.

docs.title: Task 1 — System Briefing
docs.text: Prepare a two-minute briefing on one of the following: - monitoring unit; - power distribution unit; - sensor module; - actuator assembly.

docs.title: Required Content
docs.text:
• overall purpose;
  • main functions;
  • interfaces;
  • one limitation or operational consideration;
  • one maintenance consideration.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The briefing should be clear, structured, and terminology-controlled.

docs.title: Task 2 — Test Update
docs.text: You are asked to update the team after an environmental test.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• unit remained operational;
  • response time increased at upper temperature limit;
  • no critical failure;
  • minor wear observed on cable bracket;
  • endurance testing still required.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Deliver a spoken update of 60 to 90 seconds.

docs.title: Suggested Structure
docs.text:
• test objective;
  • main result;
  • observed issue;
  • recommended next step.

docs.title: Task 3 — Fault Explanation
docs.text: Explain the following fault case orally: - intermittent communication loss; - initial checks performed; - loose connector identified; - connector re-secured; - normal communication restored.

docs.title: Additional Requirement
docs.text: End with one preventive recommendation.

docs.title: Task 4 — Meeting Contribution
docs.text: Imagine you are in a design review meeting. You need to comment on the revised bracket design.

docs.title: Known Points
docs.text:
• improved maintainability;
  • simplified cable routing;
  • larger enclosure depth;
  • tighter alignment requirement during installation.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: State your evaluation in 5 to 7 sentences.

docs.title: Task 5 — Asking for Clarification
docs.text: Prepare three oral clarification questions for each topic: 1. unclear revision status of a document; 2. missing test limit in procedure; 3. ambiguous interface requirement; 4. unexplained anomaly in report.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: Questions should be precise and professional.

docs.title: Task 6 — Maintenance Handover
docs.text: Simulate a maintenance handover at the end of a shift.

docs.title: Situation
docs.text:
• bracket replaced;
  • alignment verified;
  • function test passed;
  • follow-up inspection still required after 100 hours.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Deliver a concise spoken handover of about one minute.

docs.title: Task 7 — Safety Brief
docs.text: Prepare a short safety briefing before maintenance activity involving enclosure access.

docs.title: Mandatory Points
docs.text:
• power isolation;
  • residual hazard awareness;
  • verification before intervention;
  • documentation update after completion.

docs.title: Task 8 — Civil vs Defence Register Awareness
docs.text: Explain the same system in two oral registers: - version A for a general industrial engineering audience; - version B for a defence-oriented programme review.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text: Identify how terminology changes while maintaining professional neutrality.

docs.title: Task 9 — Oral Summary from Notes
docs.text: Convert the notes below into a short oral summary: - revised interface layout; - reduced routing complexity; - no major issue in baseline validation; - detailed fatigue assessment pending.

docs.title: Target
docs.text: 30 to 45 seconds.

docs.title: Task 10 — Repository-Ready Speaking Script
docs.text: Write a spoken script that could later be stored in the repository as a model briefing. Choose one topic: - test status update; - maintenance handover; - system overview; - anomaly explanation; - safety opening statement.

docs.title: Length
docs.text: 150 to 220 words.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: The script should be suitable for: - oral rehearsal; - teaching demonstration; - adaptation into a briefing template.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The objective of this page is to develop the learner's ability to read technical documents efficiently and accurately. In professional engineering contexts, reading rarely means reading everything line by line. It usually means identifying the purpose of the document, extracting key information, detecting constraints, understanding risks, and distinguishing between primary and secondary information.

The tasks below are designed to train this operational reading competence.

docs.title: Reading Strategy for Technical English
docs.text:
Before starting the exercises, the learner should apply the following strategy:

  1. Identify the probable document type.
  2. Detect the communicative purpose.
  3. Locate keywords linked to performance, safety, maintenance, or requirements.
  4. Separate facts from recommendations.
  5. Extract the information that would matter in a real engineering workflow.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Identifying Document Purpose
docs.text:
Read the short excerpt below and answer the questions.

The monitoring unit is intended for indoor installation in controlled industrial environments. The enclosure provides IP54 protection against dust ingress and limited water exposure. The unit shall be mounted on a vertical support surface and connected to a regulated 24 V DC power source. Installation in high-vibration environments is not recommended unless additional damping measures are implemented.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. What is the main purpose of the text?
  2. What type of document is this most likely taken from?
  3. Which three installation constraints are explicitly stated?
  4. Which requirement is mandatory and which statement is advisory?

docs.title: Reviewer Notes
docs.text: The learner should identify that the paragraph is not a product advertisement. It is a controlled technical description combining environmental constraints, installation requirements, and recommendations.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Extracting Technical Constraints
docs.text:
Read the excerpt and complete the table.

The actuator assembly was tested at ambient temperatures ranging from -20°C to +55°C. Full stroke performance was achieved throughout the range, although response time increased by approximately 8 percent at the upper limit. The unit remained operational after exposure to repeated vibration cycles; however, the cable retention bracket showed visible wear after extended testing.

docs.title: Extract the following:
docs.text:
• tested temperature range;
  • parameter that changed during testing;
  • amount of change;
  • subsystem that showed degradation;
  • whether the system remained operational.

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write a two-sentence summary that distinguishes clearly between performance variation and functional failure.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Warning, Caution, or Note?
docs.text:
Read the three statements and classify each as a Warning, Caution, or Note.

  1. Disconnect the power supply before opening the enclosure to avoid the risk of electric shock.
  2. Do not exceed the recommended torque value when securing the mounting screws, as thread damage may occur.
  3. The status indicator may remain illuminated for up to five seconds after shutdown.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text: For each statement: - classify it; - explain why; - rewrite it in a more formal technical style if necessary.

docs.title: Review Focus
docs.text: This exercise develops understanding of severity, consequence, and documentation tone.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Reading for System Function
docs.text:
Read the excerpt and answer the questions.

The sensor module detects thermal deviations within the protected compartment and transmits real-time status data to the control interface. If the measured value exceeds the predefined threshold, the system generates an alert and stores an event record for subsequent analysis. Under normal operating conditions, the event log is automatically synchronized with the supervisory workstation at regular intervals.

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
1. What are the main functions of the sensor module?
  2. What happens when the threshold is exceeded?
  3. Which process occurs under normal operating conditions?
  4. Which words indicate automation?

docs.title: Follow-Up Task
docs.text: Write a three-sentence functional summary of the system.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Reading a Fault Narrative
docs.text:
Read the fault narrative below.

During scheduled inspection, maintenance personnel observed intermittent communication loss between the remote terminal and the central processing unit. Initial inspection confirmed that the power supply remained stable and that no abnormal temperature increase was present. Further investigation identified a partially loosened connector at the interface panel. After re-securing the connector and performing a communication check, normal operation was restored.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. What was the observed symptom?
  2. Which possible causes were excluded?
  3. What was the root cause?
  4. What corrective action was taken?
  5. Which sentence confirms recovery?

docs.title: Review Focus
docs.text: The learner should identify the sequence: symptom → checks → finding → corrective action → restored condition

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Reading a Requirement Statement
docs.text:
Read the statements below.

  1. The unit shall operate continuously for a minimum of 12 hours without external recharge.
  2. The display should remain readable under direct ambient light.
  3. The housing may be manufactured from aluminium alloy or equivalent material.
  4. The interface cable must be protected from mechanical abrasion during routine handling.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text: For each sentence: - identify whether the statement expresses a requirement, recommendation, permission, or obligation; - explain the difference between shall, should, may, and must in this context; - rewrite the four statements as part of a formal specification section.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Reading for Comparison
docs.text:
Read the short comparison below.

Compared with the baseline configuration, the revised assembly offers improved sealing performance, reduced cable routing complexity, and lower maintenance time. The revised version, however, requires a more precise alignment procedure during installation.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. List the three advantages.
  2. State the main trade-off.
  3. Rewrite the paragraph as a short engineering evaluation note.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Critical Reading and Reviewer Mindset
docs.text:
Read the paragraph below and identify weaknesses.

The device is very good and works well in many situations. It is robust and does not usually fail. The installation is not too difficult if the technician knows what to do. The results were satisfactory.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Identify all vague or weak expressions.
  2. Explain why the paragraph is unsuitable for professional technical documentation.
  3. Rewrite it using more precise engineering language.

docs.title: Suggested Review Questions
docs.text:
• What does “very good” mean technically?
  • What does “many situations” refer to?
  • What measurable evidence supports “robust”?
  • What makes the results “satisfactory”?

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Converting Reading into Documentation
docs.text: Choose one of the excerpts on this page and transform it into one of the following: - a system overview paragraph; - a maintenance note; - a requirement statement; - a risk note; - a short glossary entry.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Your text should be: - technically coherent; - professionally worded; - between 80 and 150 words; - suitable for inclusion in the MkDocs repository.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Reading Under Time Pressure
docs.text:
Imagine you have only two minutes to read the following paragraph before a design review meeting.

The updated power distribution unit incorporates an internal overcurrent protection stage and a revised terminal arrangement intended to simplify field servicing. The revised arrangement reduces cable crossover and improves accessibility; however, it increases the required enclosure depth by 12 mm. Validation testing confirmed nominal operation under standard load conditions, but extended testing under peak load remains pending.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text: In less than 60 words, prepare a briefing note covering: - the design improvement; - the main trade-off; - the current validation status.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The learner should prioritize information and avoid full-sentence copying.

docs.title: Self-Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before finalizing your answers, verify the following:

  • I identified the purpose of the text correctly.
  • I extracted relevant technical information, not peripheral wording.
  • I distinguished between requirement, recommendation, note, and warning.
  • I avoided vague reformulation.
  • My summary reflects the original technical meaning.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Engineering professionals are expected to write reports that are clear, structured, and evidence-based. A good report does not simply record events. It presents observations, findings, causes, actions, and recommendations in a form that supports traceability and decision-making.

This page trains the learner to write concise but professionally credible report content.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Observation to Report Sentence
docs.text:
Convert the observations below into report-style sentences.

  1. During the test the bracket moved slightly.
  2. The power stayed stable.
  3. One connector was not fully tightened.
  4. The unit still worked.
  5. The team changed the part.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Use a formal technical tone and avoid conversational wording.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Mini Test Report
docs.text: Use the notes below to write a mini test report.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• test article: actuator assembly;
  • test type: temperature exposure;
  • range: -20°C to +55°C;
  • result: operational throughout range;
  • observation: response time increased by 8% at upper limit;
  • conclusion: acceptable performance, further endurance testing recommended.

docs.title: Suggested Structure
docs.text:
• Objective
  • Procedure Summary
  • Results
  • Conclusion

Target length: 150 to 220 words.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Fault Report
docs.text: Write a fault report from the following information: - symptom: intermittent communication loss; - environment: vibration test; - initial checks: power stable, no thermal anomaly; - finding: loose connector; - corrective action: connector secured and rechecked; - outcome: communication restored.

docs.title: Additional Requirement
docs.text: Include a recommendation for future builds.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Non-Conformance Note
docs.text: A protective enclosure was found to have a measured wall thickness below the documented value.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Write a short non-conformance note including: - deviation identified; - potential implication; - immediate action; - required follow-up.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Executive Summary Practice
docs.text:
Summarize the following situation in no more than 90 words:

  • revised design improves maintainability;
  • installation requires more precise alignment;
  • no critical issues found during baseline validation;
  • long-duration testing remains incomplete;
  • recommendation to proceed with controlled trial deployment.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The summary should be concise, balanced, and decision-oriented.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Report Improvement Task
docs.text:
Improve the report paragraph below.

The test was generally okay and the unit mostly worked. There were some small problems but nothing serious happened. The team looked at the issue and after some changes everything was acceptable.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Identify all weak expressions.
  2. Rewrite the paragraph as a professional test report excerpt.
  3. Explain how your wording improves traceability.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Root Cause Reporting
docs.text: Write a short paragraph explaining the likely root cause of a repeated mounting issue. Use the prompts below: - repeated misalignment during installation; - tolerance stack-up suspected; - visual inspection alone insufficient; - alignment fixture recommended; - no evidence of material failure.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: Your text should resemble the “Analysis” section of a technical report.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Corrective and Preventive Action Entry
docs.text: Draft a CAPA-style entry using the following: - issue: abnormal wear on cable guide; - corrective action: replace affected guide; - preventive action: revise routing path and add inspection point; - verification: follow-up inspection after 50 operating hours.

docs.title: Target
docs.text: Write 100 to 140 words.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Report Section Headings
docs.text: Prepare a heading structure for a report on a vibration test campaign. Include at least: - scope; - equipment under test; - setup; - observations; - anomalies; - conclusion; - recommendations.

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Under each heading, write one example sentence.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Full Applied Report Task
docs.text: Choose one of the following topics and write a short report of 220 to 300 words: - thermal monitoring unit validation; - communication interface anomaly; - maintenance access review; - enclosure sealing assessment.

docs.title: Mandatory Elements
docs.text:
• document purpose;
  • technical context;
  • key findings;
  • action taken or recommended;
  • formal and consistent tone.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Safety-related communication in engineering must be clear, controlled, and proportionate. It should identify hazards, state consequences, define required action, and avoid both understatement and unnecessary alarmism. This page develops the language needed for warnings, cautions, risk statements, and incident communication.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Hazard, Risk, or Control?
docs.text: Classify each item below as: - hazard; - risk; - control measure; - consequence.

docs.title: Items
docs.text:
1. exposed energized conductors
  2. electric shock during enclosure opening
  3. isolate power before maintenance
  4. injury to maintenance personnel
  5. inadequate cable retention
  6. abrasion during routine handling
  7. protective sleeve installation

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Explain the relationship between hazard, risk, and control measure in one short paragraph.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Warning vs Caution
docs.text:
Rewrite the following statements as either a Warning or a Caution, depending on severity.

  1. Opening the enclosure while powered may expose personnel to live conductors.
  2. Excessive torque may damage the mounting threads.
  3. Improper cable routing may lead to progressive abrasion.
  4. Contact with hot external surfaces may occur after extended operation.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The learner must match the message level to the consequence.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Safety Note Improvement
docs.text:
Improve the note below.

Be careful when using the system because some parts may be dangerous.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. explain why the sentence is weak;
  2. rewrite it as a technically credible safety statement;
  3. specify the hazard and consequence.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Risk Statement Writing
docs.text: Write a short risk statement for each situation: 1. use of the unit outside the defined temperature range; 2. incomplete connector engagement; 3. missing documentation after maintenance; 4. incorrect lifting of heavy enclosure; 5. bypassing inspection step before release.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Each statement should include: - condition; - possible consequence; - implied or explicit control.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Incident Summary
docs.text: Write an incident summary based on the following: - technician opened enclosure before verifying isolation; - no injury occurred; - procedural non-compliance identified; - immediate briefing conducted; - additional checklist step introduced.

docs.title: Tone
docs.text: Formal, factual, improvement-oriented.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Safety Communication for Operators
docs.text:
Write a short operator-facing safety instruction related to: - thermal hazard; - moving part hazard; - electrical isolation; - environmental limitation.

Choose one topic and write 80 to 120 words.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Stronger Safety Language
docs.text: Improve the following expressions: 1. use carefully 2. avoid problems 3. hot part 4. dangerous cable 5. bad condition

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Replace each with wording more suitable for formal safety documentation.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Safety Review Exercise
docs.text:
Read the sentence and identify missing information.

Do not install the unit incorrectly.

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
• What makes this instruction weak?
  • What information is missing?
  • How would you rewrite it in a useful professional form?

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Safety and Documentation Link
docs.text: Write a short paragraph explaining why safety communication must be linked to documentation control, revision status, and training.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The answer should go beyond generic statements and reflect engineering governance.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Repository-Ready Safety Box
docs.text: Write a documentation-ready safety box that could later be inserted into a module or template page. Choose one of the following: - power isolation; - hot surface hazard; - vibration-related loosening; - improper lifting; - cable abrasion risk.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: Use a heading such as Warning, Caution, or Safety Note and write 70 to 120 words.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Describing a system in technical English is a core engineering skill. A good description does not merely list components. It explains function, architecture, relationships, constraints, and operational relevance. This page focuses on structured technical description suitable for design reviews, internal documentation, product overviews, maintenance references, and training material.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Component Description
docs.text:
Describe the following component in 80 to 120 words:

Component: cable retention bracket Known elements: aluminium alloy, mounted on side panel, prevents cable movement, supports maintenance access, subject to vibration exposure.

docs.title: Output Requirements
docs.text: Your paragraph should mention: - material; - mounting location; - function; - operational relevance; - possible stress or limitation.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Subsystem Description
docs.text: Write a structured description of a sensor module using the prompts below: - detects thermal variations; - connected to control interface; - provides real-time monitoring; - triggers alert above threshold; - installed inside protective enclosure.

docs.title: Output Requirements
docs.text: Organize your description into: 1. function; 2. main interfaces; 3. operating logic; 4. operational value.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Weak vs Strong Description
docs.text:
Read the weak description below.

This unit is very useful and is installed in the system. It does an important job and helps the operator. It is strong and works well in normal conditions.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Identify at least five weaknesses.
  2. Rewrite the paragraph in a professional technical style.
  3. Explain how your revision improves precision.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — System Overview from Notes
docs.text:
Turn the notes below into a coherent system overview paragraph.

  • portable monitoring unit;
  • battery-powered;
  • used in field inspection activities;
  • records temperature and vibration data;
  • transmits data wirelessly to supervisory interface;
  • designed for temporary deployment;
  • enclosure resistant to dust and splash exposure.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The output should sound like a real engineering overview, not a list converted into sentences.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Describing Interfaces
docs.text: Write a short paragraph describing the interface relationships in the following configuration: - sensor module to control unit; - control unit to display interface; - display interface to operator; - event log to supervisory workstation.

docs.title: Required Language
docs.text: Use at least four of the following: - is connected to - exchanges data with - provides input to - receives information from - transmits status data to - interfaces with

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Description with Constraints
docs.text: Describe an actuator assembly using the following constraints: - must operate from -20°C to +55°C; - installed on vibration-prone support frame; - requires alignment during installation; - accessible for scheduled maintenance; - protected by external housing.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: Write a paragraph that combines description and engineering constraints naturally.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Comparative Description
docs.text: Compare baseline design and revised design using the notes below.

docs.title: Baseline Design
docs.text:
• easier to manufacture;
  • more complex cable routing;
  • longer maintenance time.

docs.title: Revised Design
docs.text:
• improved internal layout;
  • reduced maintenance time;
  • slightly larger enclosure depth;
  • more precise installation alignment required.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Write a comparative note of 120 to 150 words suitable for a design review.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Description for Non-Specialist Stakeholders
docs.text: Write a concise explanation of a power distribution unit for a stakeholder who is not a specialist engineer but must understand its purpose in the system.

docs.title: Constraint
docs.text: Maintain professional language, but reduce unnecessary complexity. The output should remain technically accurate.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Diagram-to-Text Conversion
docs.text:
Imagine a diagram showing: - power source; - control unit; - two sensor modules; - display interface; - alarm output.

Write the text that could accompany the diagram in a technical manual.

docs.title: Output Requirements
docs.text: Mention: - the flow of information; - the flow of power; - the alert logic; - the role of the operator.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Repository-Ready Description
docs.text: Prepare a final technical description page fragment for later inclusion in the documentation repository. Choose one of the following topics: - monitoring unit; - communication interface; - actuator assembly; - maintenance workstation.

docs.title: Required Structure
docs.text:
Use the following headings: - Overview - Main Functions - Interfaces - Operational Considerations - Maintenance Notes

Write approximately 180 to 250 words.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Technical translation is not a simple word-for-word transfer from Italian into English. It requires attention to terminology, register, structure, function, and audience. In engineering documentation, a literal translation may remain grammatically acceptable but still sound unprofessional or operationally weak.

This page focuses on translation and adaptation from Italian into technical English and, where relevant, on explaining why certain literal solutions should be avoided.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — From Italian to Technical English
docs.text:
Translate the following sentence into professional technical English:

Il modulo di controllo è installato all'interno dell'involucro protettivo e gestisce la trasmissione dei dati verso l'interfaccia supervisore.

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write a second version that sounds more formal and documentation-oriented.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Avoiding Literal Translation
docs.text:
Translate the sentence below, but avoid literal translation traps.

Durante il collaudo è stato riscontrato un leggero aumento del tempo di risposta alle temperature più elevate.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text: Explain why a literal translation of collaudo or è stato riscontrato may not always produce the best professional result.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Procedure Translation
docs.text:
Translate the following procedural note:

Prima di aprire il pannello di accesso, scollegare l'alimentazione e verificare l'assenza di tensione residua.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Use controlled procedural English suitable for a technical manual.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Requirement Translation
docs.text:
Translate the following into specification language:

Il sistema deve rimanere operativo per almeno 12 ore in assenza di ricarica esterna.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The output should sound like a requirement statement, not an instructional sentence.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Maintenance Narrative Translation
docs.text:
Translate and improve the following text:

Durante l'ispezione programmata è stato osservato un allentamento parziale del connettore. Dopo il serraggio e il controllo funzionale, il sistema è tornato in condizioni normali di funzionamento.

docs.title: Additional Task
docs.text: Explain the difference between a simple translation and a report-ready translation.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — False Friends Awareness
docs.text:
Translate the sentence below and identify at least two possible false-friend risks.

Il tecnico ha eventualmente sostituito il componente non conforme dopo un ulteriore controllo visivo.

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text: Pay attention to words such as eventualmente, non conforme, and ulteriore.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Register Adaptation
docs.text:
Translate the message below in two ways: - version A for an internal technical report; - version B for a procedural instruction.

È necessario prestare particolare attenzione al corretto instradamento dei cavi per evitare abrasioni durante il funzionamento.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text: Explain how the function of the text changes the translation.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Improving an Imperfect Translation
docs.text:
Read the English sentence below and improve it.

During the test it has been found a small increase of answer time at the higher temperatures.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. identify the problems;
  2. rewrite the sentence in proper technical English;
  3. explain why your version is better.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Translation for Repository Reuse
docs.text:
Translate the following excerpt and make it ready for inclusion in the MkDocs repository.

Il nuovo supporto riduce la complessità del cablaggio e migliora l'accessibilità in manutenzione, ma richiede un allineamento più accurato durante l'installazione.

docs.title: Output Requirement
docs.text: Write 50 to 80 words and make the tone suitable for a design review or system description page.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Applied Translation Task
docs.text:
Translate the short paragraph below into professional technical English.

L'unità è destinata all'uso in ambienti industriali controllati. L'installazione in aree soggette a vibrazioni elevate non è raccomandata se non sono previste misure aggiuntive di smorzamento. Le verifiche preliminari devono essere completate prima della messa in servizio.

docs.title: Final Review Criteria
docs.text:
• terminology accuracy;
  • professional register;
  • clarity of constraints;
  • natural English structure;
  • suitability for technical documentation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Technical English depends heavily on lexical precision. In engineering communication, an incorrect or imprecise word can create confusion, reduce credibility, or produce operational ambiguity. The goal of this page is to help the learner build controlled, reusable, and context-aware technical vocabulary.

The exercises below focus not only on knowing a term, but on understanding: - what it means; - where it is used; - what other terms it is commonly associated with; - what wording should be avoided.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Matching Terms to Definitions
docs.text: Match each term to the most appropriate definition.

docs.title: Terms
docs.text:
1. enclosure
  2. interface
  3. bracket
  4. threshold
  5. wear
  6. redundancy
  7. alignment
  8. compliance

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text: A. the condition of meeting a requirement, rule, or specification B. the point at which a reaction, change, or function is triggered C. progressive damage caused by use, friction, or exposure D. a structural support used for mounting or securing a part E. a protective outer casing or housing F. the arrangement that allows two systems or parts to connect or interact G. the correct relative positioning of components H. the duplication of functions or elements to improve reliability

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write one sentence for each term in a realistic engineering context.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Classifying Vocabulary by Function
docs.text: Place the following terms in the correct category: - inspect - withstand - connector - enclosure - reliable - verify - degradation - interface - compliant - calibrate - fault - robust

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
• nouns for components
  • nouns for conditions or states
  • verbs for technical actions
  • adjectives for evaluation and description

docs.title: Reflection Task
docs.text: Explain why knowing the grammatical category of a technical term helps writing quality.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Stronger Technical Alternatives
docs.text:
Replace the weak wording with a more professional technical term or expression.

  1. The part broke.
  2. The system works well.
  3. The cable moved.
  4. The device got hot.
  5. The result was bad.
  6. The component is very strong.
  7. The technician looked at the unit.
  8. The signal disappeared.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Weak: The part broke. Possible stronger wording: The bracket fractured under load. / The component failed during testing.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: More than one answer may be acceptable, provided the wording is more precise and technically plausible.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Collocation Awareness
docs.text:
Complete the collocations with the most suitable word.

  1. perform a ______ inspection
  2. meet the ______ criteria
  3. restore normal ______
  4. detect a ______ condition
  5. withstand mechanical ______
  6. maintain structural ______
  7. issue a ______ report
  8. ensure proper ______

docs.title: Possible words
docs.text: operation, alignment, acceptance, vibration, scheduled, integrity, fault, technical

docs.title: Follow-Up
docs.text: Write a short paragraph using at least four of the completed collocations.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Near-Synonym Control
docs.text: Explain the difference between the following pairs: 1. fault / failure 2. reliable / robust 3. test / inspection 4. issue / anomaly 5. operate / function 6. repair / replace

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text: Do not translate only. Explain the likely difference in engineering use.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Vocabulary in Context
docs.text:
Complete the paragraph with suitable technical vocabulary.

During routine __, the maintenance team identified abnormal _ on the cable support bracket. Although the unit remained fully , the affected part was removed and replaced as a precautionary measure. A follow-up confirmed correct installation and restored ___.

docs.title: Suggested term bank
docs.text: alignment, operational, wear, inspection, function, interface, verification, compliant

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Civil vs Defence Vocabulary Awareness
docs.text:
For each pair below, explain whether the terms are equivalent, partially overlapping, or context-dependent.

  1. equipment / asset
  2. user / operator
  3. failure / loss of capability
  4. maintenance / sustainment
  5. component / item
  6. testing / validation

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Write a short note explaining why terminology may change depending on sector, audience, and document purpose.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Adjective Quality Control
docs.text:
Choose the better adjective for each sentence and justify your choice.

  1. The enclosure is strong / impact-resistant.
  2. The interface is good / reliable.
  3. The design is simple / maintainable.
  4. The result is positive / compliant.
  5. The system is modern / interoperable.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The learner should prefer adjectives that carry technical meaning over adjectives that are merely positive.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Build a Mini Glossary
docs.text:
Create a mini glossary of ten terms related to one of the topics below: - maintenance; - safety; - systems integration; - testing; - documentation control.

For each term, include: - the term; - part of speech; - short definition; - one example sentence.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Vocabulary for Reuse
docs.text: Choose five terms from this page and turn them into a reusable phrasebank section for the repository. Your output should include: - the term; - a common collocation; - an example sentence; - a note on typical misuse.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: This exercise is designed to produce content that can later be moved to: - a glossary page; - a language-tools page; - a quality-assurance note.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This page develops general technical writing competence beyond single-sentence grammar. The focus is on structured, clear, and professionally credible written communication in engineering contexts. The learner is expected to move from fragmented statements to controlled technical paragraphs and short documents.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Clarifying Ambiguous Writing
docs.text:
Rewrite the paragraph below to make it clearer and more professional.

The system had some issues during testing, but overall it was fine. A few parts were not perfect and the team made changes. After that, everything was acceptable and the system could be used.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Identify ambiguous expressions.
  2. Replace them with more precise wording.
  3. Produce a revised paragraph of 80 to 100 words.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Writing a Technical Summary
docs.text: Write a summary of 100 to 130 words based on the notes below: - environmental test completed; - unit remained operational; - response time increased at high temperature; - cable bracket showed wear; - no critical failure observed; - further long-duration testing recommended.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: A good summary prioritizes findings and does not read like a disconnected list.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Writing from a Review Perspective
docs.text:
You are reviewing a draft that says:

The installation is easy and the unit is reliable.

docs.title: Task
docs.text: Rewrite the sentence in three stronger ways: 1. as a technical overview; 2. as a maintenance-oriented note; 3. as a design-review comment.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Controlled Paragraph Writing
docs.text:
Write a paragraph on the topic below:

Topic: why clear documentation is essential in engineering environments.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• 120 to 160 words;
  • formal tone;
  • at least three technical collocations;
  • one sentence about safety or traceability;
  • one sentence about maintenance or operations.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — Writing with Evidence
docs.text:
Transform the weak claims below into evidence-based technical statements.

  1. The design is better.
  2. The revised unit is more efficient.
  3. The enclosure is stronger.
  4. The maintenance process is simpler.
  5. The test results were good.

docs.title: Guidance
docs.text: Use measurable or at least functionally meaningful wording.

docs.title: Exercise 6 — Short Technical Note
docs.text:
Write a short technical note for internal documentation on the following situation:

  • connector loosened during vibration test;
  • communication intermittency observed;
  • connector re-secured;
  • subsequent check passed;
  • recommendation: inspect mounting retention during next build.

docs.title: Output Length
docs.text: 90 to 130 words.

docs.title: Exercise 7 — Writing for Reuse
docs.text: Write a paragraph that could later be reused in one of the following repository contexts: - glossary usage note; - module example; - template example; - quality-assurance example.

docs.title: Topic Options
docs.text:
• operational limitation;
  • maintenance access;
  • signal integrity;
  • requirement traceability.

docs.title: Exercise 8 — Revision and Improvement
docs.text:
Take the paragraph below and revise it in two stages.

The unit is designed to be used in several environments and it has a structure that helps protect it. It also works with different parts and it gives information to the operator.

docs.title: Stage 1
docs.text: Improve grammar and vocabulary.

docs.title: Stage 2
docs.text: Improve professional tone, structure, and technical density.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text: Explain what changed between Stage 1 and Stage 2.

docs.title: Exercise 9 — Engineering Style Transformation
docs.text: Rewrite the following informal statements as formal technical prose: - We checked the unit and it was okay. - The cable was in a bad position. - The test gave nice results. - The technician fixed the problem quickly.

docs.title: Reviewer Focus
docs.text: The challenge is not only grammar, but professional register.

docs.title: Exercise 10 — Repository-Ready Writing Task
docs.text: Prepare a polished text of 180 to 220 words on one of the following topics: - benefits of preventive maintenance; - role of specifications in engineering quality; - value of clear interface documentation; - importance of controlled vocabulary in technical English.

docs.title: Output Goal
docs.text: The text should be strong enough to be reused later in the site as: - a module example; - a side note; - a quality-assurance reference text.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This rubric is designed to support fair and transparent evaluation of the final project.

The project should not be judged only on grammar or vocabulary accuracy. A strong final project should also demonstrate: - documentation logic; - technical usefulness; - structural coherence; - terminological control; - publication readiness.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
The final project is evaluated across six areas:

  1. scope and coherence;
  2. technical English quality;
  3. terminology control;
  4. documentation structure;
  5. applied value and usability;
  6. publication readiness.

docs.title: Performance Levels
docs.text: Level General Meaning Excellent strong and consistent performance; very limited revision needed Good solid performance; minor weaknesses present Satisfactory acceptable but uneven performance; further revision needed Weak insufficient control; major improvement required

docs.title: 1. Scope and Coherence
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent The project has a clear purpose, a defined audience, and a coherent structure. Pages work together as one documentation product. Good The purpose is generally clear and most pages support it. Minor inconsistencies are present, but the repository remains credible. Satisfactory The purpose is partly clear, but scope control is uneven. Some pages feel disconnected, repetitive, or underdeveloped. Weak The repository lacks a stable objective. Content feels fragmented, generic, or loosely assembled.

docs.title: 2. Technical English Quality
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent Language is clear, professional, and well controlled. Grammar and sentence structure support technical communication effectively. Good Language is generally appropriate and understandable. Minor grammar or style issues are present, but usability remains good. Satisfactory Language is understandable but uneven. Weaknesses in grammar, phrasing, or register reduce professional credibility. Weak Language is frequently vague, inaccurate, informal, or structurally weak. The text does not reliably support technical communication.

docs.title: 3. Terminology Control
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent Terminology is accurate, consistent, and contextually appropriate. Key terms are used with discipline and stability. Good Most terminology is relevant and correctly used. Minor inconsistencies do not significantly affect overall quality. Satisfactory Terminology is partly controlled, but usage is sometimes generic, repetitive, or imprecise. Weak Terminology is inconsistent, weak, or overly general. The register does not feel professionally credible.

docs.title: 4. Documentation Structure
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent Pages are logically organised, easy to navigate, and internally well structured. Headings and links support the reader effectively. Good The repository is readable and mostly well structured. Some pages could be improved, but the overall hierarchy works. Satisfactory Basic structure is present, but organisation is uneven. Some headings, sections, or page flows are unclear. Weak The repository is poorly structured. Navigation, headings, and hierarchy do not support effective use.

docs.title: 5. Applied Value and Usability
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent The project contains clearly useful content such as templates, phrasebanks, examples, exercises, or guidance pages that can be applied directly. Good The project contains useful applied material, although some parts remain more descriptive than practical. Satisfactory Some useful content is present, but application value is uneven or limited. Weak The repository provides little practical value. Content remains abstract, generic, or non-operational.

docs.title: 6. Publication Readiness
docs.text: Level Descriptor Excellent The project is ready for publication with minimal or no further revision. Formatting, naming, and Markdown use are well controlled. Good The project is close to publication readiness. Only minor adjustments are needed. Satisfactory The repository is functional, but still needs visible revision before publication. Weak The repository is not ready for publication. Major work is still required.

docs.title: Suggested Weighting
docs.text: Area Weight Scope and coherence 15% Technical English quality 25% Terminology control 15% Documentation structure 15% Applied value and usability 15% Publication readiness 15%

docs.title: Reviewer Comment Prompts
docs.text:
Reviewers are encouraged to comment on at least these points:

  • strongest aspect of the project;
  • main issue affecting professional credibility;
  • terminology recommendation;
  • structural recommendation;
  • priority action before publication.

docs.title: Self-Assessment Questions
docs.text:
Before final submission, the learner should ask:

  • Does the repository feel coherent?
  • Does it contain reusable professional content?
  • Are the key terms stable across pages?
  • Is the English controlled enough for technical readers?
  • Would another reader understand how to use this site?

docs.title: Final Interpretation
docs.text:
A strong final project is not only linguistically acceptable.

It should also be: - coherent; - useful; - reviewable; - documentation-oriented; - ready for structured publication.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Project Overview
  • Project Guidelines
  • Revision Checklist
  • Publishing Guide
  • Student Portfolio

docs.title: What You Are Expected to Build
docs.text:
The final project should be developed as a coherent documentation repository.

The goal is not to produce a large quantity of pages. The goal is to build a small but credible MkDocs site that shows your ability to use B2-level technical English in a structured, professional, and reviewable way.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
Your project should demonstrate that you can:

  • organise information across multiple pages;
  • write in clear and controlled technical English;
  • use terminology consistently;
  • support the reader through examples and structured content;
  • build a site that is navigable, reusable, and publication-ready.

docs.title: Mandatory Requirements
docs.text:
Your project should include:

  • a clear homepage or introduction page;
  • a page explaining project scope;
  • several substantial content pages;
  • at least one glossary component;
  • at least one template or model document;
  • at least one applied or exercise-oriented page;
  • a portfolio or reflection page.

docs.title: Language Requirements
docs.text:
Your writing should:

  • remain clear and professional;
  • avoid uncontrolled informal language;
  • distinguish between description, requirement, recommendation, and observation;
  • use terminology consistently;
  • avoid obvious translation-like phrasing;
  • show control of technical grammar and document-oriented wording.

docs.title: Editorial Requirements
docs.text:
Your pages should:

  • use clear and consistent titles;
  • apply headings logically;
  • avoid empty or weak sections;
  • present information in a reusable way;
  • include internal links where useful;
  • maintain a stable structure across similar pages.

docs.title: Technical Repository Requirements
docs.text:
Your repository should:

  • use a clear docs/ structure;
  • include a working mkdocs.yml;
  • follow stable naming conventions;
  • use Markdown consistently;
  • be ready for local preview and GitHub-based publication.

docs.title: Recommended Content Model
docs.text: A strong project usually includes five layers:

docs.title: 1. Introduction Layer
docs.text: Explains what the repository is, who it is for, and how it should be used.

docs.title: 2. Knowledge Layer
docs.text: Provides definitions, concepts, explanations, and technical reference content.

docs.title: 3. Application Layer
docs.text: Provides exercises, templates, models, or practical scenarios.

docs.title: 4. Review Layer
docs.text: Provides checklists, quality guidance, or revision support.

docs.title: 5. Portfolio Layer
docs.text: Explains what was developed, improved, and learned during the project.

docs.title: Choosing a Good Topic
docs.text:
Choose a topic that is: - specific enough to remain coherent; - broad enough to support several useful pages; - clearly connected to the course objectives.

Suitable themes may include:

  • technical English for maintenance teams;
  • terminology and phraseology for engineering trainees;
  • documentation for a generic monitoring system;
  • safety and compliance communication;
  • civil vs defence-oriented terminology;
  • troubleshooting and reporting language;
  • system description and test-report writing.

Avoid topics that are too broad, too vague, or disconnected from the course focus.

docs.title: What Makes a Page Strong
docs.text:
A strong page usually includes:

  • a clear purpose;
  • a defined scope;
  • useful headings;
  • technical terminology used in context;
  • practical examples;
  • meaningful links to related pages.

A weak page usually contains:

  • generic filler text;
  • repeated ideas;
  • vague claims;
  • unclear structure;
  • little practical value.

docs.title: Writing Guidelines
docs.text: When drafting, apply these principles:

docs.title: Write for use
docs.text: The page should help the reader understand, compare, apply, or produce technical language.

docs.title: Prefer precision over decoration
docs.text: Professional documentation should be informative, not ornamental.

docs.title: Keep paragraphs purposeful
docs.text: Each paragraph should have a clear function.

docs.title: Control terminology
docs.text: Technical terms should be used consistently and in the correct context.

docs.title: Help the reader
docs.text: Structure content so that information can be found quickly.

docs.title: Skills the Project Should Show
docs.text:
Try to include evidence of the following:

  • system description;
  • process explanation;
  • technical comparison;
  • requirement-style writing;
  • report-style writing;
  • revision and improvement;
  • glossary development;
  • professional tone control.

docs.title: Final Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before considering the project ready, verify that:

  • all key pages contain meaningful content;
  • internal links work correctly;
  • terminology is coherent across sections;
  • headings match the real content;
  • obvious grammar and wording issues have been corrected;
  • the repository reads like documentation, not like draft notes.

docs.title: Recommended Review Sequence
docs.text:
A practical review order is:

  1. review project scope;
  2. review page map;
  3. review terminology consistency;
  4. review language quality;
  5. review navigation and cross-links;
  6. review formatting and Markdown consistency;
  7. review publication readiness.

docs.title: Final Advice
docs.text:
Treat the final project as a real engineering communication artefact.

Even if the topic is educational, the repository should show: - structure; - clarity; - consistency; - reader awareness; - documentary discipline.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Project Overview
  • Evaluation Rubric
  • Revision Checklist
  • Publishing Guide
  • Student Portfolio

docs.title: What the Final Project Is
docs.text:
The final project is the main consolidation task of the course.

Its purpose is to show that the learner can use B2-level technical English in a structured, professional, and documentation-oriented way.

Instead of producing a single essay or presentation, the learner builds a small technical documentation site using MkDocs and GitHub.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
The final project should demonstrate that the learner can:

  • organise technical information clearly;
  • write in controlled professional English;
  • maintain terminological consistency across pages;
  • distinguish between different document functions;
  • build a small but coherent documentation repository.

docs.title: Project Format
docs.text:
The final output should function as a mini documentation site, not as a collection of disconnected exercises.

Possible project scenarios include:

  • a technical training documentation set;
  • an internal engineering knowledge base;
  • a system introduction and support handbook;
  • a maintenance and troubleshooting guide;
  • a safety and compliance documentation set;
  • a civil vs defence terminology reference;
  • a dual-use engineering communication repository.

The scenario should remain realistic, clear, and professionally neutral.

docs.title: What a Good Project Should Show
docs.text: A good final project should show five things.

docs.title: 1. Documentation Logic
docs.text:
The repository should be structured as documentation, not as free-form prose.

Pages should have: - a clear purpose; - a predictable structure; - a direct relationship to reader needs.

docs.title: 2. Technical Language Control
docs.text:
The English should reflect a usable B2 technical level.

This means: - generally controlled grammar; - relevant terminology; - clear sentence structure; - limited vagueness; - consistent professional tone.

docs.title: 3. Internal Coherence
docs.text:
The repository should behave like one project.

This includes: - consistent terminology; - stable naming conventions; - coherent page structure; - useful internal links.

docs.title: 4. Usability
docs.text: The site should help the reader do something practical, such as: - understand a system; - review a process; - interpret a requirement; - use a template; - compare technical terminology.

docs.title: 5. Publication Readiness
docs.text: The repository should be organised in a way that is suitable for MkDocs publication and GitHub-based versioning.

docs.title: Recommended Scope
docs.text:
The final project does not need to be very large, but it should be coherent and complete enough to show planning, revision, and sustained writing effort.

A balanced project usually includes:

  • overview pages;
  • technical description pages;
  • glossary content;
  • one or more support pages;
  • templates or model documents;
  • practice or portfolio material.

docs.title: Minimum Suggested Deliverables
docs.text:
A solid minimum project may include:

  • 1 homepage or overview page;
  • 3 to 5 substantial content pages;
  • 1 glossary section with at least 20 useful terms;
  • 1 language-support or phrasebank page;
  • 2 practical templates;
  • 1 exercise or guided practice page;
  • 1 portfolio or reflection page.

A stronger project will usually include better internal linking, more realistic examples, and more consistent editorial control.

docs.title: Define the Reader Before Writing
docs.text:
Before drafting the project, define the main reader.

Typical readers may include: - engineering trainees; - junior maintenance technicians; - documentation learners; - project support staff; - system-integration trainees; - professionals moving from Italian technical writing to English-language documentation.

The site should be written for a reader who needs clarity, structure, and practical usefulness.

docs.title: What Counts as Evidence of Learning
docs.text:
The final project should provide visible evidence in these areas:

  • terminology control
  • functional writing competence
  • documentation structure
  • revision and improvement
  • digital documentation literacy

In practical terms, the project should show that the learner can write, revise, organise, and publish technical content in a controlled way.

docs.title: Simple Workflow
docs.text:
A practical workflow is:

  1. define the project objective;
  2. identify the target reader;
  3. choose the main content categories;
  4. create the page map;
  5. draft the core pages;
  6. add glossary and support material;
  7. include templates or applied pages;
  8. review language quality;
  9. review structural coherence;
  10. prepare the site for publication.

docs.title: Common Risks
docs.text:
Common project weaknesses include:

  • pages that are too generic;
  • inconsistent terminology;
  • poor distinction between lesson notes and documentation;
  • repetition across pages;
  • weak internal linking;
  • vague wording without technical support;
  • titles and headings that do not match the content clearly.

These issues should be addressed during revision.

docs.title: Final Advice
docs.text:
The best final projects are not always the largest.

The strongest projects are usually the ones that are: - focused; - consistent; - well structured; - clear in purpose; - easy to review and navigate.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Project Guidelines
  • Evaluation Rubric
  • Revision Checklist
  • Publishing Guide
  • Student Portfolio

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This page explains how to turn the final project into a publishable MkDocs site managed through GitHub.

The focus is on a workflow that is: - clear; - reliable; - easy to maintain; - suitable for an educational technical documentation repository.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
Before publishing, make sure the project is:

  • complete enough to be credible;
  • readable in local preview;
  • structured correctly under docs/;
  • configured properly in mkdocs.yml;
  • ready for version control and release through GitHub.

docs.title: Recommended Repository Structure
docs.text:
A typical repository should contain at least:

```text project-root/ ├── docs/ │ ├── index.md │ ├── modules/ │ ├── glossaries/ │ ├── language-tools/ │ ├── exercises/ │ ├── templates/ │ └── final-project/ ├── mkdocs.yml ├── README.md ├── requirements.txt └── .gitignore

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this checklist to review the final project before submission or publication.

The goal is to verify that the repository is: - coherent; - readable; - technically credible; - structurally consistent; - ready for publication.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
Review the project across six areas:

  1. project-level coherence;
  2. page-level quality;
  3. language quality;
  4. terminology control;
  5. documentation usability;
  6. publication readiness.

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• The project has a clear objective.
  • The target reader is identifiable.
  • The repository feels like one documentation product.
  • The main sections support the same purpose.
  • The scope is realistic and controlled.
  • Titles, sections, and examples match the declared topic.

docs.title: Warning Signs
docs.text:
• Some pages feel unrelated.
  • The tone changes too much from page to page.
  • Section names and actual content do not match.
  • The repository mixes notes, exercises, and documentation without clear structure.

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• The title reflects the real content.
  • The opening section explains the page purpose.
  • Headings are meaningful.
  • Paragraphs have a clear function.
  • The page is not empty, repetitive, or generic.
  • Examples are relevant and readable.
  • The page adds real value to the repository.

docs.title: Warning Signs
docs.text:
• The page contains filler language.
  • The same idea appears several times in slightly different wording.
  • The content is too generic to be useful.
  • Headings exist but do not help navigation.

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• Sentences are grammatically controlled.
  • The tone is professional rather than conversational.
  • Vague adjectives are limited or explained.
  • Active and passive voice are used intentionally.
  • Long sentences remain readable.
  • Punctuation supports clarity.
  • Italian-influenced constructions have been corrected.

docs.title: Typical Problems to Remove
docs.text:
• the system is composed by → use the system consists of
  • it allows to monitor → use it allows users to monitor or it enables monitoring
  • in according to → use in accordance with
  • very important without explanation
  • good performance without measurable or contextual support

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• Key terms are used consistently.
  • Abbreviations are introduced clearly.
  • Similar terms are not treated as if they were identical.
  • Glossary entries match actual page usage.
  • The same component or process is not renamed without reason.

docs.title: Review Questions
docs.text:
• Do I use fault, failure, issue, and anomaly accurately?
  • Do I distinguish requirement from recommendation?
  • Do I use system, subsystem, module, and component consistently?
  • Do I keep the same naming convention across pages?

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• The reader can find information quickly.
  • Section relationships are clear.
  • Templates, examples, and phrasebanks are actually reusable.
  • Theory and application are clearly separated.
  • Internal links support navigation.

docs.title: Review Questions
docs.text:
• Are important pages linked from other relevant pages?
  • Does the homepage explain how to use the site?
  • Are templates really reusable?
  • Are examples representative and useful?

docs.title: Check
docs.text:
• mkdocs.yml matches the real repository structure.
  • File names are stable and consistent.
  • Navigation entries match real files.
  • The site renders correctly in local preview.
  • There are no visible placeholder notes.
  • Markdown formatting is clean.
  • README or release notes match the current repository state.

docs.title: Fast Final Review
docs.text:
A practical final review can be done in this order:

  1. read the homepage;
  2. read the navigation from top to bottom;
  3. open five representative pages from different sections;
  4. review one glossary page, one module page, one exercise page, one template page, and one final-project page;
  5. check whether the repository still feels coherent as a whole.

docs.title: Sign-Off Questions
docs.text:
The project is ready only if you can answer yes to these questions:

  • Does the site behave like a documentation product?
  • Would a new reader understand its purpose?
  • Is the English suitable for technical readers?
  • Is the terminology stable enough to support trust?
  • Is the project ready to be shown publicly?

docs.title: Final Note
docs.text:
Revision is not a cosmetic stage.

In technical communication, revision is part of the product itself. A well-revised repository is easier to read, easier to trust, and easier to maintain.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This page documents the learner’s contribution, development, and revision process during the final project.

It does not replace the technical content of the repository. Its purpose is to show: - what was built; - what was improved; - which challenges emerged; - what the learner can now do more confidently in Technical English.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
A good portfolio page should explain:

  • the project theme and purpose;
  • the learner’s main contribution;
  • the main writing or terminology challenges;
  • the most important improvements made through revision;
  • the key skills developed;
  • possible next steps.

docs.title: 1. Project Summary
docs.text: Briefly explain the project theme and purpose in two or three sentences.

docs.title: 2. My Contribution
docs.text:
State which sections or pages you developed directly.

Typical examples include: - modules; - glossaries; - language tools; - exercises; - templates; - final-project pages.

docs.title: 3. Main Challenges
docs.text:
Describe the main difficulties you encountered.

Possible areas include: - terminology control; - avoiding translation from Italian structures; - distinguishing description from requirement language; - maintaining consistency across pages; - writing for a real reader instead of only for assessment.

docs.title: 4. What Improved Through Revision
docs.text:
Explain what changed between early drafts and later versions.

For example: - stronger openings; - clearer headings; - more precise terminology; - less vague wording; - better cross-linking; - more stable tone and structure.

docs.title: 5. Skills Developed
docs.text:
State which abilities improved through the project.

Examples: - writing technical explanations; - building glossary entries; - drafting reusable templates; - revising English for clarity; - structuring Markdown documentation; - preparing a repository for publication.

docs.title: 6. Next Steps
docs.text:
Conclude with realistic future improvements.

Examples: - expanding selected sections; - improving examples; - refining consistency; - adapting the repository for a more specific engineering context.

docs.title: Writing Advice
docs.text:
Keep the portfolio reflective, but still professional.

Avoid vague comments such as: - I liked this project a lot - It was difficult but interesting - I learned many new things

Prefer statements such as: - One of the main difficulties was maintaining terminological consistency across glossary and module pages. - Several pages were revised to improve heading logic and reduce repetition. - The project improved my ability to distinguish descriptive language from requirement language.

docs.title: Useful Reflection Questions
docs.text:
Use these questions to help draft the page:

  • Which part of the repository best represents my current level of Technical English?
  • Which page required the most revision, and why?
  • Which terminology area did I control well?
  • Which area still needs improvement?
  • What did I learn about writing for users instead of only for evaluation?
  • What would I improve in a future version?

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text: Brief introduction to the repository.

docs.title: My Contribution
docs.text: Main sections and pages developed or revised.

docs.title: Key Challenges
docs.text: Main linguistic and editorial difficulties.

docs.title: Revision and Improvement
docs.text: Most important changes made during review.

docs.title: Skills Developed
docs.text: Main technical-English and documentation skills gained.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Realistic future improvements.

docs.title: Final Note
docs.text:
A strong portfolio page makes development visible.

It shows not only what the learner produced, but also how the work improved through revision, structure, and greater control of technical English.

docs.title: What This Section Contains
docs.text:
This section provides the core terminology support for the course.

The glossaries are designed to help learners: - understand technical terms more accurately; - use vocabulary more consistently; - avoid vague or overly general wording; - build stronger technical writing and speaking habits.

The goal is not to collect as many words as possible. The goal is to support clear, controlled, and reusable technical English.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
The glossary section includes:

  • core vocabulary used across engineering contexts;
  • discipline-oriented terminology for specific technical areas;
  • context-specific terminology for regulated, institutional, and defence-oriented settings.

docs.title: How to Use the Glossaries
docs.text:
A practical way to use each glossary page is:

  1. read the definition;
  2. study the example sentence;
  3. check the usage note;
  4. compare the term with related or similar terms;
  5. reuse it in your own sentence or document.

docs.title: What Each Entry Typically Includes
docs.text:
Most glossary entries include:

  • Definition to explain the meaning of the term clearly;

  • Example to show how the term is used in context;

  • Usage note to explain where the term is especially useful;

  • Learner note or contrast note to help avoid common mistakes;

  • Related terms to support vocabulary expansion and consistency.

docs.title: Core Technical Vocabulary
docs.text:
• General Engineering Glossary
