  • Useful Technical Verbs
  • Technical Adjectives and Descriptors

docs.title: Discipline-Oriented Glossaries
docs.text:
• Mechanical Terms
  • Electrical and Electronic Terms
  • Systems Engineering Terms
  • Maintenance Terms
  • Safety and Risk Terms
  • Compliance and Standards Terms

docs.title: Context and Documentation Support
docs.text:
• Defence and Dual-Use Terms
  • Procurement and Document Control Terms

docs.title: Recommended Use with Other Sections
docs.text:
The glossaries work best when used together with:

  • Modules
  • Language Tools
  • Exercises
  • Templates and Models

docs.title: Practical Advice
docs.text:
Do not memorise isolated words.

A better method is to learn: - the term; - a typical collocation; - a sentence pattern; - the kind of document where the term is commonly used.

For example, it is more useful to learn: - comply with a requirement - detect a fault condition - issue a revised document

than to memorise only: - comply - detect - issue

docs.title: Final Note
docs.text:
A strong glossary is not the longest glossary.

A strong glossary is one that helps the learner choose the right term more quickly, more consistently, and with less ambiguity.

docs.title: Compliance and Standards Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary supports requirement writing, specification review, standards-based documentation, and formal statements about conformity. It is especially useful for learners who need to produce language that is disciplined, auditable, and suitable for controlled engineering documents.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/12-requirements-specifications-and-compliance.md, templates/specification-template.md, templates/test-report-template.md, and quality-assurance/style-and-editorial-guide.md.

docs.title: shall
docs.text:
Definition. Shall is the preferred modal verb for stating a mandatory requirement in many formal technical and contractual contexts.

Example. The enclosure shall maintain ingress protection under the environmental conditions defined in the specification.

Usage note. Use shall when the sentence expresses a formal requirement that must be verified or otherwise assessed for compliance.

Contrast / learner note. Do not use shall for casual future meaning. In controlled documents it normally signals obligation.

Related terms. requirement, compliance, verification, mandatory statement

docs.title: should
docs.text:
Definition. Should expresses recommendation, preferred practice, or strong advice without the full force of a mandatory requirement.

Example. Fasteners should be rechecked after the initial vibration test to confirm retention integrity.

Usage note. Useful in guidance notes, best-practice sections, and advisory language.

Contrast / learner note. If a statement is mandatory, should is too weak. Use shall or another formally defined mandatory expression.

Related terms. recommendation, guidance, preferred practice, may

docs.title: may
docs.text:
Definition. May expresses permission, allowance, or acceptable possibility within a defined framework.

Example. The inspection may be performed using equivalent calibrated equipment, provided that measurement uncertainty remains acceptable.

Usage note. Use may when the document intentionally allows flexibility or alternative methods.

Contrast / learner note. Do not use may when the intended meaning is capability. In that case can may be clearer, depending on the style guide.

Related terms. allowed, optional, permitted, can

docs.title: comply with
docs.text:
Definition. To comply with something means to meet the requirements of a standard, specification, regulation, contractual clause, or internal rule.

Example. The revised design complies with the updated sealing requirement and the applicable environmental standard.

Usage note. This phrase is central in declarations of conformity, review notes, and test conclusions.

Contrast / learner note. Be careful with weak phrasing such as is in line with. In formal compliance writing, complies with is stronger and more explicit.

Related terms. conformity, requirement, verification evidence, in accordance with

docs.title: in accordance with
docs.text:
Definition. In accordance with indicates that an action, method, or result follows a stated standard, instruction, procedure, or governing reference.

Example. Testing was performed in accordance with the approved procedure and the current issue of the relevant standard.

Usage note. Useful in reports, procedures, and audit-ready language when stating how an activity was conducted.

Contrast / learner note. This phrase often describes process alignment, while complies with often describes outcome or requirement satisfaction. In practice they may appear together.

Related terms. according to, procedure, standard, compliance

docs.title: acceptance criteria
docs.text:
Definition. Acceptance criteria are the defined conditions or performance thresholds that determine whether an item, result, or activity is acceptable.

Example. Acceptance criteria required stable communication with no data loss during the full environmental exposure period.

Usage note. Common in test plans, verification reports, supplier evaluation, and inspection records.

Contrast / learner note. Acceptance criteria should be measurable or at least assessable. Vague criteria produce weak verification evidence.

Related terms. pass/fail, requirement, verification, inspection

docs.title: deviation
docs.text:
Definition. A deviation is a departure from an approved requirement, process, drawing, plan, or expected condition.

Example. A documented deviation was raised because the approved cable clamp was temporarily unavailable.

Usage note. Use this term when a controlled difference from the baseline or requirement must be recorded and justified.

Contrast / learner note. A deviation may be authorised. A nonconformity usually indicates failure to meet a requirement and may not be pre-authorised.

Related terms. waiver, nonconformity, change request, concession

docs.title: waiver
docs.text:
Definition. A waiver is formal authorisation to accept a known departure from a requirement under stated conditions.

Example. A temporary waiver was granted pending implementation of the revised mounting arrangement.

Usage note. Appropriate in controlled technical programmes where acceptance of a deviation must be traceable.

Contrast / learner note. A waiver is a formal decision, not an informal agreement or undocumented tolerance.

Related terms. deviation, concession, approval authority, residual risk

docs.title: standard
docs.text:
Definition. A standard is an established document or reference that defines rules, requirements, methods, terminology, or characteristics for common and repeated use.

Example. Material selection was reviewed against the applicable corrosion-resistance standard.

Usage note. Use this word carefully and, where possible, cite the exact standard rather than referring to standards in general.

Contrast / learner note. A company procedure is not automatically a standard, even if it is mandatory internally.

Related terms. specification, procedure, regulation, code

docs.title: regulation
docs.text:
Definition. A regulation is a rule or set of rules issued by a competent authority and carrying formal legal or mandatory force within its scope.

Example. The export review considered the applicable regulation before release of the controlled technical dataset.

Usage note. Particularly useful in compliance summaries, audit notes, and institutional communication.

Contrast / learner note. A standard may be voluntary or adopted contractually; a regulation usually has authority-based mandatory status.

Related terms. authority, compliance, legal requirement, control

docs.title: certification
docs.text:
Definition. Certification is the formal process or result by which compliance with defined requirements is confirmed by a recognised body or authorised organisation.

Example. Certification evidence was updated following the enclosure redesign and repeat environmental testing.

Usage note. Useful in compliance communication, supplier qualification, and programme readiness discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Certification is not the same as test completion. Test data may support certification, but the formal status depends on the recognised process.

Related terms. approval, conformity assessment, qualification, evidence

docs.title: conformity
docs.text:
Definition. Conformity is the state of meeting specified requirements, standards, or approved criteria.

Example. Conformity of the revised assembly was demonstrated through inspection, documentation review, and functional testing.

Usage note. Common in formal reports, audit language, and quality statements.

Contrast / learner note. Conformity is the condition achieved; compliance is often the action or status of meeting the requirement. In many contexts they overlap, but both should be used carefully.

Related terms. compliance, verification, acceptance, nonconformity

docs.title: Requirement-writing caution
docs.text: In controlled documents, modal verbs are not stylistic decoration. They define obligation level. A repository that mixes shall, should, may, can, and simple present without discipline becomes difficult to verify and difficult to review.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Defence and Dual-Use Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary introduces professional terminology often encountered in engineering environments that support defence, security, resilience, surveillance, support, or dual-use technology programmes. The emphasis remains descriptive, institutional, and documentation-oriented. The aim is to support neutral professional English rather than sensitive operational detail.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/16-defence-oriented-technical-english.md, civil-vs-defence/terminology-comparison.md, civil-vs-defence/documentation-differences.md, and templates/briefing-template.md.

docs.title: platform
docs.text:
Definition. A platform is the physical or functional base that carries, supports, or hosts systems, subsystems, payloads, or mission-related equipment.

Example. The sensor package was adapted for integration on a lightweight mobile platform with strict mass limits.

Usage note. Use this term in architecture summaries, capability descriptions, and high-level system documentation.

Contrast / learner note. A platform is broader than a vehicle or structure alone because it often implies an integration context.

Related terms. host system, payload, interface, mission profile

docs.title: payload
docs.text:
Definition. A payload is the mission-related or function-specific equipment carried by a platform in order to perform a defined role.

Example. The electro-optical payload required vibration isolation to preserve image stability during transit and operation.

Usage note. This term is useful in system descriptions where the carried capability is distinct from the platform itself.

Contrast / learner note. Payload is not a synonym for cargo in technical programme language. It usually refers to function-bearing equipment.

Related terms. platform, sensor package, mission equipment, integration

docs.title: mission profile
docs.text:
Definition. A mission profile is the structured description of expected operating phases, conditions, durations, and task demands relevant to a system or capability.

Example. Battery sizing was reviewed against the updated mission profile, which included longer surveillance periods and extended standby time.

Usage note. Use this term when performance, endurance, environmental conditions, or support concepts depend on real use patterns.

Contrast / learner note. A mission profile is broader than a test scenario because it reflects intended use, not only a laboratory event.

Related terms. operational concept, duty cycle, endurance, deployment

docs.title: readiness
docs.text:
Definition. Readiness is the state of being prepared for intended use, deployment, or task execution, considering technical condition, support status, and personnel factors where relevant.

Example. Reduced spare-part availability affected fleet readiness despite acceptable hardware reliability.

Usage note. Useful in supportability, maintenance, logistics, and programme review discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Readiness is not identical to reliability. A system may be technically reliable but not ready if documentation, spares, or trained personnel are missing.

Related terms. availability, supportability, sustainment, serviceability

docs.title: interoperability
docs.text:
Definition. Interoperability is the ability of systems, teams, or organisations to operate together effectively through compatible interfaces, procedures, data formats, and expectations.

Example. Interoperability requirements drove the selection of standardised communication interfaces and message structures.

Usage note. Common in system-of-systems work, joint programmes, integration documents, and institutional reviews.

Contrast / learner note. Compatibility is often narrower and more technical; interoperability may include procedural and organisational dimensions.

Related terms. interface, integration, standardisation, coordination

docs.title: surveillance
docs.text:
Definition. Surveillance is the systematic observation, monitoring, or collection of information in support of situational awareness, protection, or analysis.

Example. The surveillance subsystem was optimised for persistent monitoring under variable lighting conditions.

Usage note. Use this term in capability descriptions and programme documentation where observation or monitoring functions are central.

Contrast / learner note. In a technical repository, focus on function and system behaviour rather than sensitive operational details.

Related terms. monitoring, sensing, situational awareness, observation

docs.title: sustainment
docs.text:
Definition. Sustainment is the set of activities and resources required to maintain a capability in service across its operational life.

Example. Early design choices improved sustainment by reducing connector variety and simplifying inspection access.

Usage note. Useful in lifecycle planning, support concepts, defence-oriented maintenance language, and programme affordability reviews.

Contrast / learner note. Sustainment is broader than maintenance because it includes logistics, training, spares, documentation, and long-term support arrangements.

Related terms. supportability, lifecycle, readiness, maintenance

docs.title: deployment
docs.text:
Definition. Deployment is the movement or controlled introduction of personnel, systems, or capabilities into operational use or an active field environment.

Example. Environmental sealing was reviewed before deployment in coastal conditions.

Usage note. This term often appears in planning notes, support readiness reviews, and capability summaries.

Contrast / learner note. In software contexts deployment may mean rollout of a build; in defence-oriented contexts it may refer to field introduction or tasking readiness. Context should clarify the meaning.

Related terms. fielding, rollout, operational use, readiness

docs.title: command and control
docs.text:
Definition. Command and control refers to the processes and systems used to direct, coordinate, communicate with, and supervise assets or activities.

Example. The system architecture included a secure interface to the command and control environment for status reporting and task updates.

Usage note. Use this term descriptively and institutionally, especially when discussing information flow and system integration.

Contrast / learner note. Avoid unnecessary abbreviation unless the audience is already familiar with the term or a glossary entry has introduced it.

Related terms. coordination, communication, supervision, interface

docs.title: dual-use
docs.text:
Definition. Dual-use describes technology, components, software, or knowledge that can support both civil and defence or security-related applications.

Example. The imaging module is considered dual-use because it supports both infrastructure inspection and resilience-oriented monitoring tasks.

Usage note. Very useful when discussing export review, market positioning, programme context, and institutional communication.

Contrast / learner note. Dual-use does not automatically imply restricted use, but it often increases the need for careful compliance and release review.

Related terms. controlled technology, export review, civil application, defence application

docs.title: supportability
docs.text:
Definition. Supportability is the degree to which a system design enables effective maintenance, logistics, training, documentation, and availability outcomes.

Example. Modular connector architecture improved supportability by reducing replacement time and simplifying stock management.

Usage note. Important in design trade-offs, lifecycle reviews, and maintainability-focused documentation.

Contrast / learner note. Supportability is broader than maintainability. A maintainable design may still be weak in supply chain or documentation support.

Related terms. maintainability, sustainment, readiness, spare part strategy

docs.title: hardening
docs.text:
Definition. Hardening is the process of increasing resistance to environmental stress, interference, shock, tampering, or other adverse conditions depending on context.

Example. Additional enclosure hardening was introduced to improve environmental resilience during transport and storage.

Usage note. Use cautiously and with context. The meaning may be mechanical, electronic, environmental, or cyber-related.

Contrast / learner note. Because the term is broad, the document should specify what kind of hardening is intended and against which condition.

Related terms. protection, resilience, ruggedisation, shielding

docs.title: Tone guidance for this glossary
docs.text: When using defence-oriented terminology in a training repository, prefer neutral, engineering-centred language. Describe architecture, interfaces, support, readiness, and documentation logic. Avoid unnecessary operational detail. This keeps the language professional, export-aware, and appropriate for educational use.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Electrical and Electronic Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary supports technical communication involving electrical power, signal transmission, electronic assemblies, instrumentation, and control hardware. The selected terms are broad enough for B2 learners but specific enough to improve the quality of engineering descriptions, troubleshooting notes, and test documentation.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/06-describing-systems-and-architectures.md, modules/08-performance-testing-and-evaluation.md, modules/14-troubleshooting-and-fault-analysis.md, and templates/test-report-template.md.

docs.title: voltage
docs.text:
Definition. Voltage is the electrical potential difference between two points and is the driving condition that enables current to flow in a circuit.

Example. Input voltage remained within the specified limits throughout the endurance test.

Usage note. Use voltage when documenting electrical limits, power conditions, test setup, and interface behaviour.

Contrast / learner note. Do not confuse voltage with current. Voltage is the potential difference; current is the flow of electric charge.

Related terms. current, power supply, overvoltage, input range

docs.title: current
docs.text:
Definition. Current is the flow of electric charge through a conductor or circuit path, usually measured in amperes.

Example. Current draw increased significantly during startup but stabilised once the control loop reached nominal operation.

Usage note. Useful in performance reports, troubleshooting, power budgeting, and safety notes.

Contrast / learner note. Learners sometimes write consumption where current draw is needed. Consumption is broader and less precise.

Related terms. voltage, load, power, protection

docs.title: resistance
docs.text:
Definition. Resistance is the opposition that a material or circuit element presents to the flow of electric current.

Example. Connector resistance rose after repeated environmental exposure, indicating possible contact degradation.

Usage note. Use this term when discussing electrical integrity, continuity checks, sensor elements, and fault diagnosis.

Contrast / learner note. In mechanical contexts, resistance may refer to opposition more generally, but in electrical documents it should be used with discipline and usually with measurable context.

Related terms. continuity, impedance, conductor, contact quality

docs.title: power supply
docs.text:
Definition. A power supply is the source or subsystem that provides electrical energy at the required voltage and current for a device or assembly.

Example. The power supply shall maintain stable output during transient load conditions.

Usage note. Essential in hardware descriptions, system architecture pages, and troubleshooting records.

Contrast / learner note. A power supply can be an external source or an internal subsystem; the document should clarify the architecture.

Related terms. input voltage, output regulation, load, protection

docs.title: signal
docs.text:
Definition. A signal is an electrical, electronic, or digital representation of information used for control, communication, monitoring, or measurement.

Example. The diagnostic signal remained intermittent until the connector interface was cleaned and re-seated.

Usage note. The term is highly flexible but should be qualified where useful: analogue signal, digital signal, control signal, feedback signal, or status signal.

Contrast / learner note. Avoid writing data when the text is really about the physical or logical signal path.

Related terms. interface, transmission, bandwidth, interference

docs.title: circuit
docs.text:
Definition. A circuit is the complete path or functional arrangement through which electric current flows or through which an electronic function is implemented.

Example. The protection circuit isolated the affected branch before the fault propagated to the main controller.

Usage note. Appropriate in design notes, fault analysis, electrical schematics, and hardware descriptions.

Contrast / learner note. A circuit is not the same as a system. It is usually one electrical or electronic part of a broader architecture.

Related terms. board, branch, connector, protection

docs.title: connector
docs.text:
Definition. A connector is a device that enables electrical, signal, or combined connections between cables, modules, boards, or external equipment.

Example. Inspection showed minor contamination on the connector contacts, which likely contributed to intermittent communication loss.

Usage note. Very common in assembly descriptions, maintenance findings, and interface control discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Connector issues are often described too vaguely. Where possible, state whether the problem concerns pin damage, retention, contamination, corrosion, or seating.

Related terms. interface, cable, contact resistance, continuity

docs.title: sensor
docs.text:
Definition. A sensor is a device that detects a physical condition or parameter and converts it into a signal suitable for monitoring or control.

Example. The temperature sensor shall provide stable output across the specified operating range.

Usage note. Use this term when describing instrumentation, feedback loops, monitoring systems, and test setups.

Contrast / learner note. A sensor detects; an actuator causes or controls action. Learners should keep the distinction clear.

Related terms. actuator, signal, calibration, monitoring

docs.title: actuator
docs.text:
Definition. An actuator is a device that converts electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or other input into physical motion or controlled action.

Example. The actuator response time exceeded the specified threshold during low-temperature testing.

Usage note. Common in mechanical-electrical integration, control systems, and functional testing.

Contrast / learner note. Do not describe an actuator only as a motor unless the system architecture actually reduces to that element. The actuator may include transmission and control functions.

Related terms. motor, control signal, response time, mechanism

docs.title: grounding
docs.text:
Definition. Grounding is the intentional electrical connection to a reference potential or protective earth for safety, stability, or signal integrity purposes.

Example. Improper grounding increased susceptibility to noise on the measurement channel.

Usage note. Important in safety discussions, EMC considerations, test setup descriptions, and installation instructions.

Contrast / learner note. Grounding problems may appear as signal instability, communication errors, or unexplained measurement drift.

Related terms. shielding, interference, protective earth, reference potential

docs.title: bandwidth
docs.text:
Definition. Bandwidth is the range of frequencies or data transfer capacity that a system, channel, or signal path can support effectively.

Example. The interface bandwidth was sufficient for nominal operation but became a limiting factor during high-rate data capture.

Usage note. Useful in communication, instrumentation, and performance discussions.

Contrast / learner note. In some documents bandwidth refers to data throughput in a broad sense; in others it has a more specific frequency-domain meaning. Context matters.

Related terms. throughput, signal quality, latency, communication channel

docs.title: interference
docs.text:
Definition. Interference is the unwanted effect of one electrical, electromagnetic, or signal source on another, causing degradation, noise, or instability.

Example. Electromagnetic interference was suspected after the sensor output showed repeatable disturbances near the transmitter housing.

Usage note. Use this term in test reports, troubleshooting notes, installation guidance, and compatibility discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Interference should be distinguished from random noise when the disturbance has a traceable source or coupling mechanism.

Related terms. noise, shielding, grounding, compatibility

docs.title: Typical electrical reporting patterns
docs.text:
• Input voltage remained within the specified range throughout the test.
  • The signal path was interrupted at the connector interface.
  • The sensor output showed increased noise under high-interference conditions.
  • Corrective action focused on grounding, shielding, and connector integrity.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: General Engineering Glossary
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This page covers cross-disciplinary terms that appear repeatedly in technical descriptions, requirements, reports, design reviews, and engineering discussions. The vocabulary here is deliberately broad: it helps learners describe systems before they specialise into mechanical, electrical, maintenance, compliance, or defence-oriented language.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Especially useful with modules/01-introduction-to-technical-english.md, modules/02-engineering-vocabulary-foundations.md, modules/05-describing-components-and-assemblies.md, and modules/06-describing-systems-and-architectures.md.

docs.title: component
docs.text:
Definition. A component is an individual part of a system or assembly that performs a specific function and can usually be identified, described, replaced, or tested as a distinct element.

Example. The power conditioning component was replaced after the inspection revealed unstable output under high thermal load.

Usage note. Use this term when you need a precise alternative to generic words such as part or piece. It is highly appropriate in technical descriptions, maintenance records, and configuration lists.

Contrast / learner note. Do not treat component, subsystem, and module as automatic synonyms. A component is usually smaller and more specific than a subsystem.

Related terms. assembly, module, subsystem, item

docs.title: assembly
docs.text:
Definition. An assembly is a set of components joined together to perform a combined function or to form a larger technical unit.

Example. The actuator assembly consists of the motor, gearbox, mounting bracket, and electrical connector.

Usage note. This term is common in mechanical documentation, installation instructions, illustrated parts lists, and manufacturing notes.

Contrast / learner note. An assembly is usually something physically put together. A system may include software, interfaces, procedures, and human interaction in addition to physical hardware.

Related terms. component, subassembly, integration, installation

docs.title: interface
docs.text:
Definition. An interface is the defined point or mechanism through which two elements interact, exchange signals, transfer energy, or connect mechanically.

Example. The interface between the control unit and the sensor package shall remain stable during continuous operation.

Usage note. Use interface in systems engineering, electrical descriptions, architecture summaries, and integration discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Avoid vague sentences such as the units are connected somehow. A good technical sentence identifies the interface and, when necessary, the type of interface.

Related terms. connection, connector, protocol, integration

docs.title: specification
docs.text:
Definition. A specification is a formal statement of technical characteristics, limits, functions, materials, dimensions, or performance expectations that a product, process, or service must satisfy.

Example. The environmental specification defines the temperature, vibration, and humidity limits for normal operation and storage.

Usage note. This term is central in design, procurement, test planning, compliance work, and customer communication.

Contrast / learner note. A specification describes what is required or permitted. A report describes what was observed or completed.

Related terms. requirement, standard, acceptance criteria, tolerance

docs.title: requirement
docs.text:
Definition. A requirement is a mandatory statement that defines what a system, subsystem, or process shall do or what condition it shall satisfy.

Example. One system requirement states that the monitoring unit shall record event data for a minimum of thirty days.

Usage note. Use this word with discipline. In professional writing, a requirement is not just a wish or preference; it implies obligation and traceability.

Contrast / learner note. A requirement is mandatory. A recommendation or design preference is not automatically a requirement.

Related terms. shall, constraint, compliance, verification

docs.title: configuration
docs.text:
Definition. Configuration refers to the documented arrangement of functional and physical characteristics of a system or item at a given point in time.

Example. The test was performed using the baseline configuration defined in the current integration release.

Usage note. This term is important in testing, document control, maintenance, and change management.

Contrast / learner note. Do not confuse configuration with setup in a casual sense. Configuration usually implies controlled and recorded technical status.

Related terms. baseline, version, change, configuration item

docs.title: integration
docs.text:
Definition. Integration is the process of combining components, modules, or subsystems so that they operate together as an intended whole.

Example. The integration phase revealed a timing mismatch between the communication unit and the supervisory controller.

Usage note. Use this term in programme planning, test reports, system descriptions, and project reviews.

Contrast / learner note. Integration is not the same as installation. Installation may be one step within a broader integration activity.

Related terms. interface, compatibility, validation, deployment

docs.title: reliability
docs.text:
Definition. Reliability is the probability or demonstrated ability of a system or item to perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.

Example. Reliability data showed that the revised connector design reduced intermittent signal loss during vibration exposure.

Usage note. This term is essential in performance discussions, qualification evidence, maintenance strategy, and lifecycle planning.

Contrast / learner note. Reliability is different from availability. A system may be reliable in design but unavailable if logistics or maintenance support are inadequate.

Related terms. availability, durability, failure rate, maintainability

docs.title: performance
docs.text:
Definition. Performance refers to how effectively a system, component, or process achieves its intended function when assessed against defined criteria.

Example. The performance of the monitoring software remained within the specified response time across the full operating range.

Usage note. The word is common but should be supported by measurable criteria whenever possible.

Contrast / learner note. Avoid empty statements such as good performance. State what parameter is being discussed: accuracy, efficiency, response time, throughput, endurance, or stability.

Related terms. efficiency, capability, accuracy, response time

docs.title: operating conditions
docs.text:
Definition. Operating conditions are the environmental, electrical, mechanical, and procedural circumstances under which a system is expected to function.

Example. The unit was tested under representative operating conditions, including high humidity and continuous duty cycle.

Usage note. This phrase is especially useful when linking requirements, test methods, and design constraints.

Contrast / learner note. Do not confuse operating conditions with storage conditions or transport conditions unless the document explicitly compares them.

Related terms. environmental limits, duty cycle, operating range, service conditions

docs.title: verification
docs.text:
Definition. Verification is the process of confirming that specified requirements have been met, usually through inspection, analysis, test, or demonstration.

Example. Requirement verification was completed through bench testing and document review.

Usage note. Use this term in formal project language, especially where evidence must be linked to a requirement.

Contrast / learner note. Verification asks whether the item was built correctly against the specification. Validation asks whether the correct solution was chosen for the intended use.

Related terms. validation, acceptance, requirement, traceability

docs.title: deployment
docs.text:
Definition. Deployment is the controlled introduction of a system, software build, capability, or technical solution into operational or field use.

Example. Deployment of the updated software package was postponed until the interface issue had been closed.

Usage note. This term is common in software, systems, operational planning, and support documentation.

Contrast / learner note. Deployment is broader than release or installation. It often includes coordination, configuration control, user readiness, and support arrangements.

Related terms. release, installation, rollout, operational use

docs.title: Preferred collocations on this page
docs.text:
• meet a requirement
  • define an interface
  • verify compliance
  • control the configuration
  • assess performance under representative operating conditions
  • complete subsystem integration before deployment

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Maintenance Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary supports communication about inspection, serviceability, failure response, corrective action, and technical support planning. It is particularly useful for learners who need to write maintenance logs, troubleshooting records, inspection notes, or service recommendations.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/13-maintenance-english.md, modules/14-troubleshooting-and-fault-analysis.md, templates/maintenance-log-template.md, and templates/troubleshooting-report-template.md.

docs.title: inspection
docs.text:
Definition. Inspection is the structured examination of an item, assembly, or process in order to identify condition, conformity, wear, damage, or other relevant findings.

Example. Visual inspection identified contamination around the connector interface and minor deformation of the protective cover.

Usage note. Use inspection when the activity aims to observe and assess condition, whether visually, dimensionally, or through instruments.

Contrast / learner note. Inspection is not the same as test. A test usually evaluates function or performance; an inspection may occur without active operation.

Related terms. condition check, examination, conformity assessment, serviceability

docs.title: serviceability
docs.text:
Definition. Serviceability is the condition of being fit for continued use or ready to perform the intended function safely and effectively.

Example. The component remained serviceable despite superficial surface marks because no loss of function or structural integrity was detected.

Usage note. This term is valuable in maintenance findings, release-to-service decisions, and technical evaluations after inspection.

Contrast / learner note. Serviceability does not always mean perfect condition. It means acceptable condition for continued use within defined limits.

Related terms. airworthy or fit-for-service concepts, inspection result, acceptance, release

docs.title: overhaul
docs.text:
Definition. An overhaul is a major maintenance action in which an item is disassembled, inspected, restored, repaired, or replaced to recover required performance or service life.

Example. The gearbox was removed for overhaul after repeated vibration excursions exceeded maintenance thresholds.

Usage note. Use this term when the maintenance activity is extensive and planned as a substantial restoration action.

Contrast / learner note. Overhaul is much broader than routine servicing or minor replacement.

Related terms. repair, refurbishment, maintenance interval, disassembly

docs.title: replacement
docs.text:
Definition. Replacement is the removal of an existing item and the installation of another item in order to restore function, condition, or compliance.

Example. Sensor replacement resolved the intermittent output issue observed during calibration.

Usage note. Common in maintenance logs, corrective actions, and parts management.

Contrast / learner note. A replacement may be temporary, permanent, like-for-like, or upgraded. If that distinction matters, state it explicitly.

Related terms. spare part, corrective action, installation, serviceability

docs.title: scheduled maintenance
docs.text:
Definition. Scheduled maintenance is maintenance performed at predetermined intervals or according to predefined usage criteria.

Example. Scheduled maintenance includes filter inspection every six months and connector integrity checks at each major service interval.

Usage note. Useful in support planning, maintenance manuals, and lifecycle strategy discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Scheduled maintenance is proactive. Unscheduled maintenance is triggered by faults, damage, or unexpected findings.

Related terms. preventive maintenance, interval, inspection plan, support strategy

docs.title: unscheduled maintenance
docs.text:
Definition. Unscheduled maintenance is maintenance initiated by failure, degradation, unexpected behaviour, or inspection findings outside the normal planned interval.

Example. Unscheduled maintenance was required after the unit reported repeated communication faults during field operation.

Usage note. Appropriate in incident follow-up, support statistics, and reliability discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Frequent unscheduled maintenance may indicate design weakness, inadequate operating procedures, or environmental mismatch.

Related terms. corrective maintenance, downtime, failure, troubleshooting

docs.title: preventive maintenance
docs.text:
Definition. Preventive maintenance is maintenance intended to reduce the probability of failure or performance degradation before a fault becomes evident.

Example. Preventive maintenance actions include lubrication, seal inspection, and verification of fastening torque.

Usage note. Use this term when explaining planned actions designed to preserve serviceability.

Contrast / learner note. Preventive maintenance aims to avoid faults. Corrective maintenance responds after a problem has already emerged.

Related terms. scheduled maintenance, reliability, service interval, condition monitoring

docs.title: corrective action
docs.text:
Definition. Corrective action is the action taken to eliminate the cause of a detected nonconformity, fault, or undesirable condition in order to prevent recurrence.

Example. Corrective action included replacement of the damaged connector and revision of the cable routing instruction.

Usage note. This term is stronger than a simple repair because it emphasises recurrence prevention as well as immediate recovery.

Contrast / learner note. A temporary fix is not always corrective action if the underlying cause remains unaddressed.

Related terms. root cause, containment, preventive action, nonconformity

docs.title: downtime
docs.text:
Definition. Downtime is the period during which a system or item is not available for its intended use due to maintenance, failure, or other inhibiting conditions.

Example. Connector rework reduced downtime compared with full harness replacement.

Usage note. Useful in maintenance planning, support metrics, and operational impact summaries.

Contrast / learner note. Downtime measures loss of availability, not necessarily severity of technical damage.

Related terms. availability, maintenance burden, turnaround time, supportability

docs.title: spare part
docs.text:
Definition. A spare part is a replacement item held in stock or available through supply channels for maintenance purposes.

Example. Lead time for the spare part affected the expected restoration date for the affected unit.

Usage note. Important in logistics, support planning, repair decisions, and readiness discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Not every replacement item is immediately a stocked spare part. Availability and supply arrangements matter.

Related terms. lead time, replacement, stock level, supportability

docs.title: failure mode
docs.text:
Definition. A failure mode is the specific manner in which a component, subsystem, or process can fail or stop meeting its intended function.

Example. One identified failure mode involved intermittent signal drop caused by reduced contact pressure at the connector.

Usage note. Use this term in reliability analysis, troubleshooting, design review, and corrective action planning.

Contrast / learner note. A failure mode is the manner of failure; the root cause explains why it occurred.

Related terms. root cause, fault symptom, degradation mechanism, mitigation

docs.title: troubleshooting
docs.text:
Definition. Troubleshooting is the structured process of identifying, isolating, and resolving the cause of a technical problem.

Example. Troubleshooting began with power checks, continued with connector inspection, and concluded with replacement of the damaged interface board.

Usage note. This term is central in maintenance communication and diagnostic workflows.

Contrast / learner note. Good troubleshooting is evidence-based. Avoid presenting assumptions as confirmed causes.

Related terms. fault isolation, symptom, corrective action, verification test

docs.title: Preferred maintenance reporting language
docs.text:
• Inspection revealed no abnormal wear.
  • The item remains serviceable pending routine replacement at the next scheduled interval.
  • Unscheduled maintenance was initiated following repeated fault indications.
  • Corrective action addressed both the damaged component and the contributing installation practice.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Mechanical Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary collects terms frequently used when describing structures, moving parts, loads, tolerances, alignment, and mechanical behaviour. It is particularly useful for maintenance documentation, component description, manufacturing notes, and technical reporting involving physical assemblies.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/05-describing-components-and-assemblies.md, modules/08-performance-testing-and-evaluation.md, modules/13-maintenance-english.md, and templates/system-description-template.md.

docs.title: load
docs.text:
Definition. Load is the force or combination of forces applied to a component, structure, or system during operation, handling, or testing.

Example. The mounting frame shall withstand the specified static and dynamic load without permanent deformation.

Usage note. Always clarify the type of load when relevant: static, dynamic, axial, radial, thermal, or shock-related.

Contrast / learner note. Learners often write pressure when they really mean load. Pressure is force per unit area; load is the broader engineering concept.

Related terms. stress, strain, torque, fatigue

docs.title: torque
docs.text:
Definition. Torque is the turning effect produced by a force acting at a distance from an axis or pivot.

Example. The fastening procedure specifies the required torque to prevent loosening during vibration exposure.

Usage note. Common in installation instructions, maintenance tasks, and motor or transmission descriptions.

Contrast / learner note. Do not replace torque with force. They are related but not equivalent.

Related terms. rotation, shaft, fastener, alignment

docs.title: shaft
docs.text:
Definition. A shaft is a rotating mechanical element used to transmit motion, torque, or power between connected parts.

Example. Inspection of the drive shaft revealed surface wear near the coupling interface.

Usage note. Use this term in descriptions of motors, gearboxes, actuators, pumps, and rotating equipment.

Contrast / learner note. A shaft transmits rotation. A rod may transfer force or provide structural support but is not necessarily rotational.

Related terms. bearing, coupling, rotation, alignment

docs.title: bracket
docs.text:
Definition. A bracket is a structural support element used to hold, secure, or position a component relative to another structure.

Example. The sensor bracket was redesigned to reduce vibration-induced displacement during field trials.

Usage note. Useful in assembly descriptions, installation notes, and design modifications.

Contrast / learner note. A bracket is not just any support. In technical descriptions it usually implies a shaped mounting element with a defined fixing function.

Related terms. mounting, fastener, support, housing

docs.title: tolerance
docs.text:
Definition. Tolerance is the permissible variation in a dimension, parameter, or characteristic from its nominal value.

Example. Machining tolerance on the mating surface shall be maintained to ensure correct sealing performance.

Usage note. This term is essential in manufacturing, inspection, assembly fit, and specification documents.

Contrast / learner note. Tolerance is not the same as error. A tolerance is an allowed range; an error is an undesired deviation from a true or target value.

Related terms. clearance, fit, dimensional control, acceptance criteria

docs.title: fatigue
docs.text:
Definition. Fatigue is progressive material damage caused by repeated or cyclic loading, often leading to crack initiation and eventual failure.

Example. The revised design reduces fatigue risk at the bracket interface by lowering local stress concentration.

Usage note. Appropriate in durability discussions, test reports, design reviews, and failure analysis.

Contrast / learner note. Fatigue is not simply wear. Wear concerns material loss from contact and motion, while fatigue concerns repeated stress cycles.

Related terms. stress concentration, crack, endurance, failure mode

docs.title: wear
docs.text:
Definition. Wear is the gradual loss or degradation of material at contacting surfaces due to friction, motion, contamination, or repeated use.

Example. Excessive wear was observed on the guide surface after prolonged operation in a contaminated environment.

Usage note. Common in maintenance findings, inspection reports, and lifecycle evaluations.

Contrast / learner note. Wear may exist without immediate failure, but it can still reduce efficiency, accuracy, or service life.

Related terms. abrasion, friction, lubrication, serviceability

docs.title: stiffness
docs.text:
Definition. Stiffness is the resistance of a component or structure to elastic deformation under applied load.

Example. Additional reinforcement increased bracket stiffness and reduced unwanted movement during transport vibration.

Usage note. Use this word in structural behaviour discussions and comparisons between design options.

Contrast / learner note. Stiffness is different from strength. A component may be strong enough not to fail but still insufficiently stiff for alignment or precision requirements.

Related terms. deflection, strength, rigidity, deformation

docs.title: alignment
docs.text:
Definition. Alignment is the correct relative positioning of parts so that motion, load transfer, sealing, or interface behaviour remains within acceptable limits.

Example. Misalignment of the coupling caused abnormal vibration and accelerated bearing wear.

Usage note. Important in rotating equipment, mechanical interfaces, installation tasks, and troubleshooting reports.

Contrast / learner note. Alignment problems often create secondary symptoms such as noise, overheating, or uneven wear.

Related terms. fit, interface, tolerance, inspection

docs.title: seal
docs.text:
Definition. A seal is a feature or component designed to prevent leakage, contamination ingress, or unwanted transfer between adjacent areas.

Example. The seal must remain effective throughout the specified temperature range and pressure cycle.

Usage note. Useful in fluid systems, enclosures, environmental protection discussions, and maintenance notes.

Contrast / learner note. A seal can refer to a discrete component or to the achieved sealing function. Context should make this clear.

Related terms. gasket, housing, leakage, enclosure

docs.title: housing
docs.text:
Definition. A housing is the external structure or enclosure that supports, protects, or contains internal components.

Example. The aluminium housing provides mechanical protection and acts as a thermal path for heat dissipation.

Usage note. Common in product descriptions, mechanical drawings, and design summaries.

Contrast / learner note. Housing is broader than cover. A cover may close an opening, while a housing often forms the main structural body.

Related terms. enclosure, bracket, mounting, protection

docs.title: fastener
docs.text:
Definition. A fastener is a mechanical element used to join or secure parts together, such as a bolt, screw, nut, rivet, or clamp.

Example. All fasteners in the external assembly shall be secured according to the approved torque procedure.

Usage note. Prefer this term when describing the joining function in a general way, especially in instructions and parts lists.

Contrast / learner note. Do not use screw when the exact element is unknown. Fastener is safer and more professional unless the specific type matters.

Related terms. torque, bracket, assembly, retention

docs.title: Typical mechanical sentence patterns
docs.text:
• The assembly shall withstand the applied load without permanent deformation.
  • Inspection revealed wear at the rotating interface.
  • Misalignment may increase vibration and reduce service life.
  • The housing supports the internal components and protects them from contamination.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.
