docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Procurement and Document Control Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary supports communication about technical procurement, supplier interaction, deliverables, revision logic, and controlled documentation. It is particularly useful for learners who work around engineering governance, configuration control, programme administration, or supplier-facing documentation.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/17-procurement-and-programme-language.md, modules/18-mkdocs-and-github-documentation-project.md, templates/technical-email-template.md, and quality-assurance/repository-governance.md.

docs.title: request for proposal
docs.text:
Definition. A request for proposal is a formal invitation asking suppliers or contractors to submit a technical and commercial proposal in response to stated needs and evaluation criteria.

Example. The request for proposal included mandatory interface requirements, environmental limits, and delivery milestones.

Usage note. In writing, it is common to introduce the full term once and then use the abbreviation only if the context supports it.

Contrast / learner note. A request for proposal is broader than a purchase order because it begins a competitive or evaluative procurement phase.

Related terms. statement of work, compliance matrix, supplier response, evaluation

docs.title: statement of work
docs.text:
Definition. A statement of work is the document section that defines the tasks, scope, outputs, responsibilities, and boundaries of the contracted effort.

Example. The statement of work required monthly progress reporting and delivery of a controlled interface document.

Usage note. Useful in procurement, subcontracting, and programme governance.

Contrast / learner note. A specification defines what the technical solution must satisfy; a statement of work defines what effort shall be performed.

Related terms. scope, deliverable, milestone, contractual requirement

docs.title: deliverable
docs.text:
Definition. A deliverable is a document, item, dataset, service, or completed output that must be provided as part of a project, contract, or agreed work package.

Example. The verification report is a contractual deliverable and must be issued in controlled form.

Usage note. Use this term when discussing obligations, planning, review status, and customer-facing outputs.

Contrast / learner note. A task is an activity; a deliverable is the output resulting from the activity.

Related terms. output, submission, milestone, acceptance

docs.title: milestone
docs.text:
Definition. A milestone is a defined project event or achievement used to monitor progress, sequence decisions, or trigger subsequent work.

Example. Completion of the integration readiness review was treated as a milestone before field acceptance testing.

Usage note. Useful in schedule discussions, progress updates, and programme dashboards.

Contrast / learner note. A milestone is not the same as a deadline, although milestones often have scheduled dates.

Related terms. schedule, review gate, phase completion, deliverable

docs.title: revision
docs.text:
Definition. A revision is a formally identified version change to a controlled document, drawing, or dataset resulting from approved modification.

Example. Revision B incorporated the updated connector specification and corrected the inspection interval table.

Usage note. This term is essential in document control, change notes, and repository governance.

Contrast / learner note. Do not confuse revision with informal editing. A revision normally implies formal traceability.

Related terms. issue, version, change record, baseline

docs.title: issue
docs.text:
Definition. Issue can refer either to a formal published version of a document or to a problem requiring resolution, depending on context.

Example. The latest document issue was released after closure of the review comments.

Usage note. Because the word has two meanings, good technical writing should make the intended meaning obvious.

Contrast / learner note. When referring to a problem, pair the word with context such as technical issue or open issue. When referring to document status, pair it with document issue or a revision identifier.

Related terms. revision, release, problem, action item

docs.title: controlled copy
docs.text:
Definition. A controlled copy is a document instance that is subject to formal update, distribution, and revision control procedures.

Example. Only the controlled copy in the repository shall be used for release and audit purposes.

Usage note. Useful in quality systems, formal document control, and training guidance.

Contrast / learner note. A downloaded local file may become uncontrolled unless the system explicitly maintains its status.

Related terms. document control, distribution, revision, master record

docs.title: document register
docs.text:
Definition. A document register is the structured list or database that records controlled documents, their status, identifiers, owners, and revision information.

Example. The document register was updated to reflect withdrawal of the obsolete test procedure.

Usage note. Appropriate in programme governance, quality assurance, and repository administration.

Contrast / learner note. A folder listing is not automatically a document register unless it records controlled metadata and status.

Related terms. index, controlled copy, revision history, ownership

docs.title: version
docs.text:
Definition. Version is the identified state of a document, software item, dataset, or configuration at a given point in its development or release history.

Example. The analysis was repeated using software version 3.4.2 and the current approved hardware baseline.

Usage note. This term is broad and widely useful, but the repository should define how versions relate to revisions, releases, and baseline states.

Contrast / learner note. In some organisations revision is used for documents and version for software; consistency matters more than preference.

Related terms. revision, release, baseline, change history

docs.title: approval authority
docs.text:
Definition. An approval authority is the person or function formally empowered to accept, approve, release, or authorise a document, change, waiver, or deliverable.

Example. Final approval authority remained with the project technical lead following completion of the review cycle.

Usage note. Useful in controlled workflows and governance language.

Contrast / learner note. Reviewing a document does not automatically mean holding approval authority.

Related terms. authorisation, sign-off, review, release

docs.title: lead time
docs.text:
Definition. Lead time is the elapsed time between initiation of a request or order and the availability, delivery, or completion of the requested output.

Example. Long lead time for the replacement interface board influenced the temporary support plan.

Usage note. Important in procurement, logistics, maintenance support, and schedule risk discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Lead time is different from processing time or transit time, although these may contribute to total lead time.

Related terms. schedule risk, availability, spare part, procurement planning

docs.title: compliance matrix
docs.text:
Definition. A compliance matrix is a structured table showing how a proposal, design, or deliverable addresses each stated requirement or evaluation criterion.

Example. The supplier response included a compliance matrix linking each requirement to evidence, assumptions, and planned verification.

Usage note. Very useful in proposal evaluation, requirement review, and structured technical assessment.

Contrast / learner note. A matrix is valuable only if statements are evidence-based rather than declarative and unsupported.

Related terms. traceability, requirement, evidence, evaluation

docs.title: Repository governance language to prefer
docs.text:
• issue a revised document
  • record the current status in the document register
  • use the controlled copy as the reference source
  • submit the deliverable by the agreed milestone
  • identify the approval authority before release

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Safety and Risk Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary supports professional communication about hazards, exposure, protective measures, and incident-related reasoning. The goal is to help learners write safety-oriented English that is precise, calm, and non-dramatic, which is a key feature of competent engineering communication.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/05-safety-and-compliance.md, modules/15-safety-risk-and-incident-communication.md, templates/incident-report-template.md, and quality-assurance/repository-review-playbook.md.

docs.title: hazard
docs.text:
Definition. A hazard is a source, situation, or condition with the potential to cause harm, damage, or other adverse consequences.

Example. Exposed conductive surfaces created an electrical hazard during maintenance access.

Usage note. Use hazard to identify the source of possible harm, not the probability that harm will occur.

Contrast / learner note. Hazard and risk are not the same. The hazard is the source of danger; the risk combines likelihood and severity.

Related terms. risk, exposure, control measure, unsafe condition

docs.title: risk
docs.text:
Definition. Risk is the combination of the likelihood of occurrence of harm and the severity of the consequences if the harmful event occurs.

Example. The residual risk was considered acceptable after additional guarding and lockout measures were introduced.

Usage note. Risk language should normally be supported by an assessment method, rating logic, or qualitative judgement framework.

Contrast / learner note. Avoid using risk as a loose synonym for problem. In safety writing, the term has a structured meaning.

Related terms. hazard, severity, likelihood, mitigation

docs.title: mitigation
docs.text:
Definition. Mitigation is the reduction of risk through design change, procedural control, protective measure, training, or other intervention.

Example. Risk mitigation included relocation of the interface, clearer labelling, and a revised inspection step.

Usage note. Use this term in safety notes, design reviews, and incident follow-up actions.

Contrast / learner note. Mitigation reduces risk but may not eliminate the hazard completely.

Related terms. control measure, protective action, residual risk, prevention

docs.title: exposure
docs.text:
Definition. Exposure is the state of being subjected to a hazard, environmental condition, or harmful influence.

Example. Prolonged exposure to contamination increased the probability of connector degradation.

Usage note. The term is useful in both safety and reliability discussions, especially when environment matters.

Contrast / learner note. Exposure describes contact or presence in relation to a hazard; it does not by itself describe the final consequence.

Related terms. hazard, duration, susceptibility, operating environment

docs.title: severity
docs.text:
Definition. Severity is the measure of how serious the consequences of a hazardous event or failure could be.

Example. Although the likelihood was low, the severity of uncontrolled thermal escalation justified immediate design review.

Usage note. Usually appears with likelihood when discussing risk assessment.

Contrast / learner note. Severity concerns consequence magnitude, not frequency.

Related terms. likelihood, consequence, risk rating, criticality

docs.title: likelihood
docs.text:
Definition. Likelihood is the probability or relative chance that a hazardous event, failure, or unwanted condition will occur.

Example. Improved cable retention reduced the likelihood of accidental disconnection during maintenance handling.

Usage note. Useful in qualitative and quantitative risk language.

Contrast / learner note. Likelihood is not the same as exposure duration, although exposure can influence likelihood.

Related terms. probability, frequency, severity, occurrence

docs.title: safe state
docs.text:
Definition. A safe state is the condition into which a system moves or is placed to minimise harm following a fault, abnormal condition, or emergency action.

Example. On loss of communication, the controller transitions to a predefined safe state and inhibits further motion commands.

Usage note. Use this term in control logic, safety functions, hazard analyses, and emergency procedures.

Contrast / learner note. A safe state is not always a full shutdown. It is the condition judged safest for the specific system and context.

Related terms. fail-safe behaviour, emergency stop, inhibition, protective logic

docs.title: protective measure
docs.text:
Definition. A protective measure is any design feature, device, barrier, warning, or procedural control intended to reduce exposure or consequence.

Example. The protective measure selected for the interface zone combined physical shielding with access restrictions.

Usage note. Useful in formal safety writing because it stays neutral and technically broad.

Contrast / learner note. Prefer protective measure to vague wording such as something was added for safety.

Related terms. mitigation, barrier, control, safeguard

docs.title: incident
docs.text:
Definition. An incident is an unplanned event that resulted in, or had the potential to result in, harm, damage, interruption, or reportable abnormality.

Example. The incident caused temporary system unavailability but no damage to adjacent equipment.

Usage note. This term is common in safety reporting, operational summaries, and investigation notes.

Contrast / learner note. An incident may involve consequences; a near miss is usually an event that could have led to harm but did not.

Related terms. event, near miss, nonconformity, occurrence

docs.title: near miss
docs.text:
Definition. A near miss is an event that did not result in harm or damage but had the potential to do so under slightly different circumstances.

Example. The dropped fastener was classified as a near miss because no component damage occurred and the foreign object was recovered immediately.

Usage note. Near-miss reporting is important because it reveals vulnerabilities before more serious outcomes occur.

Contrast / learner note. Do not downplay near misses. They often provide high-value learning with low-cost consequences.

Related terms. incident, hazard, learning action, prevention

docs.title: emergency shutdown
docs.text:
Definition. Emergency shutdown is the rapid and deliberate stopping of a system or process to reduce danger or prevent escalation during an unsafe condition.

Example. Emergency shutdown was initiated after abnormal temperature rise exceeded the defined intervention threshold.

Usage note. Use this phrase in procedures, safety analyses, and event descriptions where controlled stopping is part of the safety strategy.

Contrast / learner note. Not every stop is an emergency shutdown. The term implies a defined protective response to a hazardous or abnormal condition.

Related terms. safe state, protective logic, emergency procedure, isolation

docs.title: residual risk
docs.text:
Definition. Residual risk is the remaining level of risk after planned control measures and mitigations have been applied.

Example. Residual risk remained acceptable provided that the inspection interval was respected and trained personnel performed the task.

Usage note. This term is useful when explaining why design and procedural controls do not eliminate all risk but reduce it to an acceptable level.

Contrast / learner note. Residual risk should be explicitly acknowledged rather than hidden by overconfident language.

Related terms. mitigation, acceptance, control measure, safety case

docs.title: Tone guidance for safety writing
docs.text: Safety writing should remain factual, controlled, and evidence-based. Strong safety communication does not rely on dramatic vocabulary. Instead, it uses structured statements such as the hazard was identified, the risk was reduced, exposure was limited, and the residual risk was accepted subject to defined controls.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Systems Engineering Terms
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary introduces terminology used when engineers move from single components to integrated capability, lifecycle planning, interfaces, and requirement-driven development. The entries are particularly useful in architecture pages, programme reviews, traceability discussions, and integration reports.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/06-describing-systems-and-architectures.md, modules/12-requirements-specifications-and-compliance.md, modules/16-defence-oriented-technical-english.md, and modules/18-mkdocs-and-github-documentation-project.md.

docs.title: stakeholder
docs.text:
Definition. A stakeholder is an individual, team, authority, customer, operator, or organisation with an interest in the system, its performance, its compliance status, or its lifecycle outcomes.

Example. Stakeholder expectations were reviewed before the interface requirements were frozen.

Usage note. Use this term in project planning, requirement elicitation, validation discussions, and governance material.

Contrast / learner note. A stakeholder is not only the end user. Technical authorities, maintainers, procurement teams, and compliance bodies may all be stakeholders.

Related terms. user, operator, authority, sponsor

docs.title: constraint
docs.text:
Definition. A constraint is a limiting condition that restricts design freedom, implementation options, or operational use.

Example. Mass and power constraints required the team to reassess the sensor configuration.

Usage note. Use constraint when a design choice is bounded by factors such as size, cost, schedule, standards, or existing interfaces.

Contrast / learner note. A constraint limits what can be done; a requirement states what shall be done.

Related terms. requirement, limitation, trade-off, boundary

docs.title: trade-off
docs.text:
Definition. A trade-off is the balanced consideration of competing design objectives in which improvement in one area may lead to compromise in another.

Example. The trade-off analysis compared reduced weight against lower structural stiffness and shorter expected service life.

Usage note. Common in design reviews, architecture papers, and decision records.

Contrast / learner note. A trade-off is not a random compromise. It should be justified against criteria, evidence, and stakeholder priorities.

Related terms. constraint, optimisation, design decision, performance

docs.title: baseline
docs.text:
Definition. A baseline is an approved reference state of requirements, design, configuration, or documentation against which future changes are controlled.

Example. The test campaign was executed against the current hardware baseline and the approved software release.

Usage note. Use this term in configuration management, verification planning, and project governance.

Contrast / learner note. A draft is not a baseline until formally approved or controlled for a defined purpose.

Related terms. configuration, release, revision, change control

docs.title: validation
docs.text:
Definition. Validation is the process of confirming that the selected system or solution satisfies the intended user need and operational purpose.

Example. Operational trials supported validation of the monitoring workflow in a representative user context.

Usage note. Use validation when the question concerns suitability for intended use rather than simple requirement compliance.

Contrast / learner note. Verification asks whether the system meets the specification; validation asks whether it solves the right problem in practice.

Related terms. verification, user need, acceptance, operational suitability

docs.title: traceability
docs.text:
Definition. Traceability is the ability to link requirements, design elements, implementation choices, test evidence, issues, and changes across the lifecycle.

Example. Requirement traceability was maintained through a controlled matrix linking each statement to its verification method and evidence source.

Usage note. Very valuable in regulated projects, systems engineering, quality assurance, and document repositories.

Contrast / learner note. Traceability is not achieved by storing documents only. The links between artefacts must be explicit and maintainable.

Related terms. matrix, requirement, verification, evidence

docs.title: configuration item
docs.text:
Definition. A configuration item is a product, document, software element, or other controlled entity selected for formal configuration management.

Example. Each critical software package was treated as a separate configuration item for release and rollback control.

Usage note. Use this term in configuration plans, review records, and repository governance documentation.

Contrast / learner note. Not every file is automatically a configuration item. The organisation decides what requires formal control.

Related terms. baseline, revision, controlled document, change request

docs.title: lifecycle
docs.text:
Definition. Lifecycle refers to the full sequence of phases through which a system or item passes, from concept and design to operation, support, upgrade, and retirement.

Example. Lifecycle considerations influenced the selection of a more maintainable enclosure design.

Usage note. The term is useful when discussing supportability, sustainability, maintenance burden, and long-term cost.

Contrast / learner note. Lifecycle is broader than project schedule. It includes the operational and support phases after delivery.

Related terms. supportability, sustainment, maintenance, retirement

docs.title: system-of-systems
docs.text:
Definition. A system-of-systems is a larger integrated capability composed of multiple independent systems that interact to provide combined operational or functional benefit.

Example. Interface control became more challenging because the solution formed part of a wider system-of-systems environment.

Usage note. Useful in defence, transport, infrastructure, and complex digital environments.

Contrast / learner note. Do not overuse this term. It is appropriate only when the constituent systems retain some degree of independence.

Related terms. integration, interoperability, architecture, external dependency

docs.title: functional allocation
docs.text:
Definition. Functional allocation is the assignment of specific functions or responsibilities to particular subsystems, components, software elements, or human operators.

Example. Functional allocation confirmed that health monitoring would be performed locally while data archiving would remain a supervisory function.

Usage note. Use this term in architecture design, responsibility mapping, and requirement decomposition.

Contrast / learner note. Allocation is about where a function resides. Implementation details may still be unresolved.

Related terms. architecture, requirement decomposition, subsystem responsibility, interface

docs.title: interface control
docs.text:
Definition. Interface control is the disciplined management of information that defines how two system elements connect, communicate, or interact.

Example. Late changes to interface control data introduced avoidable integration risk.

Usage note. Important in multi-team environments and whenever hardware, software, or organisational boundaries must be coordinated.

Contrast / learner note. An interface is the technical boundary itself; interface control is the management activity that keeps the boundary stable and understood.

Related terms. interface, integration, baseline, configuration management

docs.title: operational concept
docs.text:
Definition. An operational concept is a structured description of how a system is expected to be used, supported, and integrated in its intended environment.

Example. The validation workshop identified several gaps between the draft operational concept and the actual maintenance workflow.

Usage note. Useful in early design, validation planning, user documentation, and high-level project communication.

Contrast / learner note. An operational concept is broader than a procedure because it explains intended use, context, and interaction, not only sequence steps.

Related terms. use case, mission profile, user need, validation

docs.title: Typical systems-engineering sentence patterns
docs.text:
• The design must satisfy the stated requirement while respecting mass and power constraints.
  • Traceability shall be maintained from requirement to verification evidence.
  • The selected baseline will remain frozen until completion of the integration review.
  • Validation activities should reflect the operational concept rather than laboratory convenience alone.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Technical Adjectives and Descriptors
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary focuses on adjectives and descriptive terms that strengthen technical writing by making it more precise and less conversational. Many B2 learners know the underlying concepts but still rely on weak wording such as good, strong, bad, or useful. The descriptors below provide more professional alternatives.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with language-tools/phrasebank-for-description.md, modules/09-principles-of-technical-writing.md, templates/system-description-template.md, and quality-assurance/style-and-editorial-guide.md.

docs.title: robust
docs.text:
Definition. Robust describes a design, process, or system that continues to function effectively under variable, demanding, or imperfect conditions.

Example. The revised connector retention concept proved more robust under repeated handling and vibration exposure.

Usage note. Useful in design comparison, review commentary, and performance summaries.

Contrast / learner note. Robust does not mean heavy or oversized. It refers to dependable performance under challenge.

Related terms. resilient, reliable, durable, tolerant

docs.title: reliable
docs.text:
Definition. Reliable describes something that performs its intended function consistently over time and under stated conditions.

Example. The architecture is reliable provided that power quality remains within the specified limits.

Usage note. A strong adjective for reports and recommendations, but it should ideally be supported by evidence.

Contrast / learner note. Reliable is not the same as available or maintainable, although the concepts are related.

Related terms. dependable, stable, consistent, maintainable

docs.title: redundant
docs.text:
Definition. Redundant describes an additional element or path included so that the function can continue if one element fails or becomes unavailable.

Example. The communication chain includes a redundant data path to preserve status reporting in the event of a single-point fault.

Usage note. Use this term in system architecture, safety-related design reasoning, and reliability discussions.

Contrast / learner note. In everyday English, redundant can mean unnecessary. In engineering, it often means intentionally duplicated for resilience.

Related terms. backup, fail-safe, resilience, availability

docs.title: scalable
docs.text:
Definition. Scalable describes a design or architecture that can be expanded, adapted, or increased in capacity without major redesign.

Example. The software architecture is scalable enough to support additional sensing channels with limited reconfiguration effort.

Usage note. Common in digital systems, platforms, data processing, and modular product families.

Contrast / learner note. Scalable does not necessarily mean currently large. It means capable of efficient growth.

Related terms. modular, extensible, configurable, adaptable

docs.title: modular
docs.text:
Definition. Modular describes a design composed of separate functional units that can be combined, replaced, or upgraded with limited disruption to the whole system.

Example. A modular enclosure concept simplified maintenance access and reduced replacement time for failed units.

Usage note. Useful in architecture discussions, supportability arguments, and product-family descriptions.

Contrast / learner note. Modular design supports change, but it does not automatically guarantee standardisation or interoperability.

Related terms. scalable, replaceable, supportable, configurable

docs.title: compliant
docs.text:
Definition. Compliant describes an item, design, process, or document that satisfies stated requirements, rules, standards, or criteria.

Example. The revised report is compliant with the current document template and review checklist.

Usage note. A useful adjective when the subject has already been assessed against an explicit reference set.

Contrast / learner note. Do not use compliant without implying or stating the reference against which compliance was judged.

Related terms. conformant, acceptable, verified, approved

docs.title: portable
docs.text:
Definition. Portable describes equipment or a solution that can be moved, carried, or relocated without excessive difficulty and without unacceptable setup burden.

Example. The diagnostic tool is portable enough for use in both workshop and field-support environments.

Usage note. Useful in product descriptions and support discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Portable is not the same as handheld. Portable equipment may still require transport cases or setup support.

Related terms. lightweight, deployable, field-capable, mobile

docs.title: maintainable
docs.text:
Definition. Maintainable describes a system or design that can be inspected, serviced, repaired, or restored efficiently and safely.

Example. The revised cable routing is more maintainable because the inspection points remain accessible without major disassembly.

Usage note. Very valuable in design reviews and supportability arguments.

Contrast / learner note. Maintainable concerns ease of maintenance, not only maintenance frequency.

Related terms. supportable, accessible, serviceable, lifecycle-efficient

docs.title: interoperable
docs.text:
Definition. Interoperable describes systems or elements that can work together effectively through compatible data, procedures, and interfaces.

Example. The selected message format is interoperable with the wider monitoring environment and existing archive tools.

Usage note. Common in systems engineering and multi-party environments.

Contrast / learner note. Interoperable is broader than electrically compatible or physically connectable.

Related terms. compatible, integrated, coordinated, standardised

docs.title: configurable
docs.text:
Definition. Configurable describes a system or product that can be adjusted or tailored through defined settings or controlled options.

Example. The alarm thresholds are configurable within an approved range to support different operating profiles.

Usage note. Useful in software, instrumentation, and adaptable hardware platforms.

Contrast / learner note. Configurable does not mean uncontrolled or user-modifiable in any way. Good technical writing should indicate limits and permissions.

Related terms. adjustable, selectable, parameterised, scalable

docs.title: lightweight
docs.text:
Definition. Lightweight describes a component, structure, or solution with relatively low mass, often as a design objective linked to transportability or integration limits.

Example. A lightweight bracket was selected to reduce total system mass without compromising stiffness.

Usage note. Use this term carefully and preferably with context such as structural constraints, portability, or platform limits.

Contrast / learner note. Lightweight should not imply fragility unless evidence supports that concern.

Related terms. portable, low-mass, compact, efficient

docs.title: ruggedised
docs.text:
Definition. Ruggedised describes equipment deliberately adapted or designed to tolerate harsh environmental or handling conditions.

Example. The field laptop uses a ruggedised enclosure to improve durability during transport and outdoor operation.

Usage note. Common in support, field-use, and environmental qualification contexts.

Contrast / learner note. Because the term is broad, good writing should indicate what conditions the item is ruggedised against.

Related terms. robust, hardened, protected, resilient

docs.title: Weak versus strong description
docs.text: Weak description often relies on vague adjectives such as good, strong, bad, or suitable. Strong technical description uses controlled adjectives connected to a reason or condition, for example maintainable because access points remain visible or robust under repeated vibration and handling.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: Useful Technical Verbs
docs.text:
Scope of this glossary

This glossary focuses on verbs that make technical writing more precise, more formal, and more useful in professional contexts. Weak technical English often depends on a narrow set of everyday verbs such as do, make, put, fix, or check. The verbs below help learners describe engineering action more accurately.

docs.title: How to read and reuse the entries
docs.text: Each entry is written for active professional use. The definition gives conceptual precision, the example shows sentence-level application, and the usage note explains where the term is especially valuable in engineering communication. Where helpful, a contrast note is added to prevent common learner mistakes.

docs.title: Best fit inside this course
docs.text: • Best aligned with modules/02-engineering-vocabulary-foundations.md, modules/09-principles-of-technical-writing.md, modules/10-technical-emails-and-professional-messages.md, and language-tools/phrasebank-for-reporting.md.

docs.title: assemble
docs.text:
Definition. To assemble means to put components together in the required order or arrangement to form an assembly or completed unit.

Example. The sensor bracket shall be assembled in accordance with the approved installation sequence.

Usage note. Prefer this verb to casual alternatives such as put together in technical descriptions and instructions.

Contrast / learner note. Assembly concerns creation of the unit. Installation concerns placing the unit into its operating location.

Related terms. install, fit, integrate, mount

docs.title: integrate
docs.text:
Definition. To integrate means to combine elements so that they operate together effectively as part of a broader system or architecture.

Example. The new monitoring module was integrated with the existing supervisory controller after interface verification.

Usage note. Useful in system design, software-hardware interaction, and programme planning.

Contrast / learner note. Integrate is broader than connect. Two elements may be connected physically but not fully integrated functionally.

Related terms. interface, connect, validate, deploy

docs.title: deploy
docs.text:
Definition. To deploy means to introduce, release, or place a system, capability, or software build into intended use or an operational environment.

Example. The revised configuration will be deployed only after closure of the outstanding compliance comments.

Usage note. Useful in software, fielding, operational rollout, and support communication.

Contrast / learner note. Deploy is not always the same as install. Deployment often includes coordination, readiness, and controlled release.

Related terms. release, field, introduce, activate

docs.title: monitor
docs.text:
Definition. To monitor means to observe, track, or supervise a condition, parameter, or activity over time.

Example. The control software continuously monitors temperature, input voltage, and communication integrity.

Usage note. A key verb for instrumentation, system operation, safety, and reporting.

Contrast / learner note. Monitor implies sustained or repeated observation, not a single check.

Related terms. observe, detect, supervise, track

docs.title: detect
docs.text:
Definition. To detect means to identify the presence of a condition, event, signal, or anomaly through observation, measurement, or system logic.

Example. The diagnostic routine detects loss of communication before the fault propagates to the logging layer.

Usage note. A stronger verb than see or find when the document concerns sensing, alarm logic, or evidence-based discovery.

Contrast / learner note. Detection indicates recognition. Diagnosis or interpretation may still be required afterwards.

Related terms. sense, identify, alarm, indicate

docs.title: withstand
docs.text:
Definition. To withstand means to remain functional or structurally acceptable when exposed to a load, condition, or stress.

Example. The external enclosure shall withstand repeated handling and the specified transport shock profile.

Usage note. Very useful in requirement statements and qualification summaries.

Contrast / learner note. Withstand concerns resistance to an applied condition. It is not the same as simply survive in a casual sense.

Related terms. tolerate, resist, endure, maintain integrity

docs.title: verify
docs.text:
Definition. To verify means to confirm, through objective means, that a requirement or stated condition has been met.

Example. The test sequence was designed to verify correct transition to the predefined safe state.

Usage note. Essential in requirement-driven engineering and formal reporting.

Contrast / learner note. Do not use verify when you only mean believe or expect. Verification requires evidence.

Related terms. confirm, inspect, validate, demonstrate

docs.title: validate
docs.text:
Definition. To validate means to confirm that a solution is suitable for its intended use, often through representative application or user-oriented assessment.

Example. Field trials were conducted to validate the maintenance workflow under realistic constraints.

Usage note. Important in systems engineering, user acceptance, and operational evaluation.

Contrast / learner note. Validation is not interchangeable with verification. Each answers a different question.

Related terms. verify, assess suitability, user acceptance, representative use

docs.title: troubleshoot
docs.text:
Definition. To troubleshoot means to investigate a fault systematically in order to isolate the cause and restore correct function.

Example. The maintenance team troubleshot the communication issue by checking power, grounding, and connector integrity in sequence.

Usage note. Very common in maintenance and technical support contexts.

Contrast / learner note. Troubleshoot is an active process. It should not be used for guessing without evidence.

Related terms. diagnose, isolate, correct, inspect

docs.title: calibrate
docs.text:
Definition. To calibrate means to compare, adjust, or confirm the performance of an instrument or measurement system against a known reference.

Example. The pressure transducer was calibrated before the qualification test campaign began.

Usage note. Appropriate in instrumentation, metrology, and pre-test preparation.

Contrast / learner note. Calibration is not the same as simple setup. It implies reference-based accuracy control.

Related terms. adjust, verify, measurement, instrument

docs.title: mitigate
docs.text:
Definition. To mitigate means to reduce the severity, likelihood, or impact of a risk, fault, or adverse condition.

Example. Additional shielding was introduced to mitigate interference on the measurement channel.

Usage note. This verb is especially useful in safety, risk, and design review language.

Contrast / learner note. Mitigate does not always remove the issue completely. It reduces the problem or its consequences.

Related terms. reduce, control, protect, contain

docs.title: upgrade
docs.text:
Definition. To upgrade means to improve, replace, or revise a system, component, or software item so that capability, compliance, supportability, or performance is enhanced.

Example. The legacy interface board was upgraded to improve maintainability and signal integrity.

Usage note. Useful in lifecycle planning, change proposals, and capability discussions.

Contrast / learner note. Upgrade should imply improvement against a defined baseline, not just change for its own sake.

Related terms. replace, revise, enhance, modernise

docs.title: Verb discipline in technical writing
docs.text: Strong writing often depends on selecting the right verb. Compare the team did a check with the team inspected the interface, or the system has a problem with the controller detected a communication fault. The second form is shorter, clearer, and more professional.

docs.title: Writing patterns worth reusing
docs.text:
The value of a glossary increases when the learner converts terminology into reusable sentence patterns. After studying the entries above, build short patterns such as:

  • The system shall ... under the specified operating conditions.
  • The inspection confirmed that ...
  • The interface between X and Y is responsible for ...
  • A deviation was recorded because ...
  • The revised document includes ...

docs.title: Suggested micro-practice
docs.text:
1. Select five entries from this page and write one sentence for each in a different document genre.
  2. Rewrite one weak sentence by replacing generic vocabulary with glossary terms.
  3. Add one contrast pair to your personal notes, for example verification vs validation or incident vs near miss.
  4. Reuse at least three entries in a module assignment before moving to the next glossary.

docs.title: Cross-reference inside the repository
docs.text: For applied use, connect this page to the relevant modules, phrasebanks, templates, and exercises. Terminology becomes valuable only when it is reused consistently across the documentation set.

docs.title: What This Section Contains
docs.text:
This section is the reusable language toolbox of the course.

Unlike the modules, which follow a teaching sequence, the pages here are designed for quick consultation while reading, drafting, revising, or preparing technical communication.

Use this section when you need support with: - grammar for technical writing; - functional language for specific communication tasks; - linking words and text organisation; - phrasebanks for common engineering contexts; - common mistakes and false friends.

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text:
The Language Tools section includes three main resource types:

Resource Type Purpose Core references explain grammar, function, and text organisation Phrasebanks provide reusable wording for specific tasks Error-prevention pages help avoid recurring mistakes and transfer problems

docs.title: When to Use This Section
docs.text: Use these pages at different stages of the writing process.

docs.title: Before Drafting
docs.text: Consult the relevant phrasebank or functional-language page to choose the right tone and structure.

docs.title: During Drafting
docs.text: Use the grammar and linking pages to keep the text clear, controlled, and logically connected.

docs.title: During Revision
docs.text: Use the common-mistakes and false-friends pages to detect weak phrasing, interference, or inappropriate wording.

docs.title: Core References
docs.text:
These pages support technical writing at sentence and paragraph level.

  • Grammar Reference
  • Functional Language
  • Linking Words for Technical Writing

docs.title: Error Prevention
docs.text:
These pages help learners identify and correct frequent problems.

  • Common Mistakes for Italian Speakers
  • False Friends in Technical English

docs.title: Phrasebanks
docs.text:
These pages provide reusable language for common technical communication tasks.

  • Phrasebank for Description
  • Phrasebank for Reporting
  • Phrasebank for Meetings
  • Phrasebank for Email Writing
  • Phrasebank for Safety

docs.title: Writing a Technical Report
docs.text: Start with: - Phrasebank for Reporting - Grammar Reference - Linking Words for Technical Writing

docs.title: Drafting a Technical Email
docs.text: Start with: - Phrasebank for Email Writing - Functional Language

docs.title: Preparing for a Meeting or Review
docs.text: Start with: - Phrasebank for Meetings - Functional Language

docs.title: Writing Safety-Related Content
docs.text: Start with: - Phrasebank for Safety - Grammar Reference

docs.title: How This Section Supports the Course
docs.text:
The Language Tools pages are intended to work together with:

  • Modules
  • Glossaries
  • Exercises
  • Templates and Models

These tools are most useful when they are applied directly in a real task.

docs.title: Practical Advice
docs.text:
Do not try to memorise entire pages.

A better approach is to: 1. identify the communication task; 2. select the relevant expressions; 3. use them in a draft; 4. revise the draft for clarity and consistency.

docs.title: Final Note
docs.text:
This section is not a grammar appendix.

It is a working resource designed to help the learner produce technical English that is clearer, more controlled, and more reusable in professional documentation.

docs.title: Purpose of this page
docs.text:
Italian-speaking learners of technical English often possess strong technical reasoning and adequate specialist vocabulary, yet still produce writing that sounds translated rather than naturally professional. The problem is not usually lack of intelligence or lack of subject knowledge. It is often a question of transfer: Italian sentence structure, collocation patterns, and rhetorical habits are carried directly into English technical prose.

This page identifies the most recurrent patterns and shows how to correct them in a way that supports engineering clarity, not only grammatical accuracy.

docs.title: 1. Translating sentence rhythm directly from Italian
docs.text: Italian technical writing often tolerates longer sentence chains and more embedded explanation than English documentation normally prefers. English technical prose usually benefits from shorter clauses, clearer segmentation, and stronger sentence hierarchy.

docs.title: Weaker version
docs.text: The component, which is installed in the upper part of the frame and that has the function to support the connector, is very important for the stability of the system.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The component is installed in the upper part of the frame and supports the connector. Its primary role is to improve system stability.

docs.title: Correction principle
docs.text: Do not try to reproduce Italian sentence length. Write in smaller logical units.

docs.title: 2. Using generic adjectives instead of technical meaning
docs.text: Italian learners often rely on words such as good, bad, important, critical, or strong when a more precise technical descriptor would communicate more effectively.

docs.title: Weak
docs.text: The connector was in a bad condition.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: The connector showed local contamination and reduced retention.

docs.title: Stronger technical options
docs.text:
• damaged
  • deformed
  • misaligned
  • contaminated
  • serviceable
  • non-compliant
  • within tolerance
  • structurally intact

docs.title: 3. Overusing “must” where “shall” or “should” is more appropriate
docs.text:
Many Italian learners use must for all strong statements. In professional technical English, the choice between shall, should, and must often depends on document type.

  • shall: formal requirement
  • should: recommendation or good practice
  • must: strong obligation, often used in procedures or safety-critical statements

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The bracket shall withstand the specified load.
  • A follow-up inspection should be performed after transport.
  • Personnel must not energise the unit while the cover is removed.

docs.title: 4. Confusing “actual” and “attuale”
docs.text:
This is one of the most persistent false friends.

  • English actual = real / effettivo
  • Italian attuale usually corresponds to current in English

docs.title: Incorrect
docs.text: The actual configuration includes revision 3.

docs.title: Correct
docs.text: The current configuration includes revision 3.

docs.title: 5. Writing “make a control” instead of “perform a check”
docs.text: Many direct translations from Italian sound unnatural because they use a generic verb plus noun combination that does not exist in normal English technical collocation.

docs.title: Prefer these forms
docs.text:
• perform a check
  • conduct an inspection
  • carry out verification
  • issue a report
  • release a document
  • record a value
  • submit a revision

docs.title: 6. Using “control” when the real meaning is “check” or “inspect”
docs.text: In English technical contexts, control often means regulation, command, or active management, not simple verification.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The control unit regulates the output signal.
  • The operator checked the connector alignment.

docs.title: Common correction
docs.text: Italian controllare may correspond to: - check - inspect - verify - review - monitor - control only when actual regulation or command is involved

docs.title: 7. Translating “to support” too widely
docs.text: Italian writers sometimes use support for every kind of physical or functional help. In English technical writing, verb choice should reflect the exact type of support.

docs.title: Possible alternatives
docs.text:
• support for structural or organisational support
  • withstand for mechanical resistance or tolerance
  • enable for functional possibility
  • facilitate for process improvement
  • sustain in some specialised contexts

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• weak: The material supports high temperature.
  • better: The material withstands high temperature exposure.

docs.title: 8. Overusing “important”
docs.text: Important is not wrong, but it is often weak in technical prose.

docs.title: Weak
docs.text: This parameter is important for performance.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: This parameter is critical to thermal stability.

docs.title: Even better when possible
docs.text: This parameter directly affects thermal stability and response time.

docs.title: 9. Incorrect use of prepositions
docs.text: Prepositions are a persistent error area because direct transfer from Italian is very common.

docs.title: Frequent corrections
docs.text:
• compliant with, not compliant to
  • related to, not usually related with
  • responsible for, not responsible of
  • dependent on, not dependent from
  • consistent with, not consistent to
  • according to, not in according with

docs.title: 10. Using “eventually” for “eventualmente”
docs.text: In English, eventually means in the end or after some time. It does not usually mean if needed or possibly.

docs.title: Incorrect
docs.text: Eventually, an additional test can be done.

docs.title: Correct depending on meaning
docs.text:
• If necessary, an additional test can be performed.
  • A further test may be performed if required.

docs.title: 11. Translating “sensible” from “sensibile”
docs.text:
• English sensible = reasonable, practical
  • English sensitive = sensitive, responsive, delicate

docs.title: Example
docs.text: The sensor is highly sensitive to pressure variation.

docs.title: 12. Unnatural use of “function” and “have the function to”
docs.text: A very common transfer pattern is has the function to. This is grammatically understandable but stylistically weak.

docs.title: Prefer
docs.text:
• The function of the valve is to regulate flow.
  • The valve regulates flow.
  • The bracket is intended to support the assembly.

docs.title: 13. Overusing the passive with weak subject control
docs.text: Italian learners sometimes produce passive-heavy English where the actor remains unclear and the sentence becomes difficult to follow.

docs.title: Weak
docs.text: It was decided that the document was to be updated after it was reviewed.

docs.title: Better
docs.text:
The review team decided to update the document after completing the technical review.

Use passive voice, but do not use it as a substitute for structure.

docs.title: 14. Writing “is composed by” instead of “is composed of”
Correct forms
docs.text:
• The assembly is composed of three sections.
  • The system consists of three sections.
  • The unit includes a power stage and a control module.

docs.title: 15. False equivalence between “problem” and technical issue types
docs.text: Italian problema is broad. In English technical writing, more specific nouns are often better.

docs.title: Alternatives
docs.text:
• issue
  • fault
  • failure
  • deviation
  • non-conformity
  • anomaly
  • malfunction
  • constraint

docs.title: Example
docs.text: An intermittent communication fault was detected during the startup sequence.

docs.title: 16. Translating emphasis instead of evidence
docs.text: Italian technical speech may sometimes tolerate stronger evaluative emphasis. English engineering prose usually prefers evidence-based qualification.

docs.title: Weak
docs.text: The result was very good and absolutely acceptable.

docs.title: Better
docs.text: The result remained within the specified tolerance range and met the acceptance criteria.

docs.title: 17. Using “before to” instead of “before + -ing / noun / clause”
Incorrect
docs.text: Before to start the test, inspect the connector.

docs.title: Correct
docs.text:
• Before starting the test, inspect the connector.
  • Before the test begins, inspect the connector.
  • Before test execution, inspect the connector.

docs.title: 18. Using “informations” and other non-count forms incorrectly
docs.text: Many technical nouns that are plural in Italian remain uncountable in English.

docs.title: Common examples
docs.text:
• information not informations
  • equipment not equipments
  • advice not advices
  • evidence often uncountable in technical reporting

docs.title: 19. Weak paragraph logic
docs.text: A frequent B2-level issue is not individual grammar, but paragraph organisation. Learners mix observation, interpretation, action, and conclusion without signalling the shift.

docs.title: Better paragraph sequence
docs.text:
1. observation;
  2. supporting data;
  3. interpretation;
  4. action taken;
  5. residual concern or next step.

docs.title: 20. Revision checklist for Italian-speaking learners
docs.text:
Before releasing a text, ask:

  • Did I translate structure directly from Italian?
  • Is any sentence too long for its function?
  • Did I choose a vague adjective where a technical descriptor is available?
  • Are my modals aligned with the document type?
  • Are my prepositions natural in English?
  • Did I use actual, eventually, sensible, or control correctly?
  • Are observation and interpretation separated clearly?

docs.title: Final note
docs.text:
Do not translate professionalism literally

Professional technical English is not obtained by translating Italian word for word at a high level of formality. It is obtained by choosing the wording that makes the engineering meaning precise, reviewable, and proportionate.

docs.title: Purpose of this page
docs.text:
False friends are terms that look familiar to Italian-speaking learners because they resemble Italian words, but they do not carry the same meaning in professional English. In technical writing, false friends are particularly dangerous because the sentence may appear correct on the surface while still conveying the wrong meaning, the wrong level of certainty, or an unnatural register.

This page focuses on the false friends most likely to appear in engineering, reporting, maintenance, safety, compliance, and documentation contexts.

docs.title: 1. current vs attuale
docs.text:
• current = attuale
  • actual = reale / effettivo

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The current configuration includes revision 5.
  • The actual measured value was lower than expected.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Using actual when you mean current can make the sentence sound incorrect or subtly misleading.

docs.title: 2. actual vs attuale
docs.text: This is the reverse side of the previous error and deserves separate attention.

docs.title: Correct meanings
docs.text:
• actual = real, true, effective, measured, factual
  • current = present, in force now, presently applicable

docs.title: Example
docs.text: The actual torque value recorded during the test was 18.4 Nm, whereas the current specification sets the nominal value at 18 Nm.

docs.title: 3. eventual vs eventuale
docs.text:
• English eventual = final, ultimate, occurring at the end of a process
  • Italian eventuale often means possible or potential

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The eventual outcome of repeated contamination exposure may be seal degradation.
  • If necessary, an additional inspection may be performed.

docs.title: Learner note
docs.text: When you want to say eventuale in the Italian sense, you usually need possible, potential, if necessary, or if required.

docs.title: 4. eventually vs eventualmente
docs.text:
• English eventually = in the end / after some time
  • Italian eventualmente often means if needed / possibly

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The issue was eventually resolved after replacement of the interface module.
  • If required, a further review may be scheduled.

docs.title: 5. sensible vs sensibile
docs.text:
• sensible = reasonable, practical, well judged
  • sensitive = sensitive, delicate, responsive to change

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• A sensible revision strategy is to update the baseline in controlled stages.
  • The sensor is highly sensitive to thermal drift.

docs.title: 6. support vs sopportare
docs.text:
• support = supportare, sostenere, fornire supporto
  • withstand = sopportare nel senso di resistere a una condizione tecnica

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The bracket supports the lower assembly.
  • The material shall withstand elevated temperature exposure.

docs.title: Learner note
docs.text: When you mean mechanical or environmental resistance, withstand is often the better verb.

docs.title: 7. control vs controllare
docs.text: In English, control often implies active regulation or command, not only checking.

docs.title: Compare
docs.text:
• The control unit regulates power distribution.
  • The operator checked the fastener torque.
  • Quality reviewed the document before issue.

docs.title: Better alternatives for Italian controllare
docs.text:
• check
  • inspect
  • verify
  • review
  • monitor

docs.title: 8. argument vs argomento
docs.text:
• English argument often means line of reasoning, debate, or position
  • Italian argomento is often better translated as topic, subject, or issue

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The main topic of today’s review is connector maintainability.
  • The report presents a strong argument for retaining the current layout.

docs.title: 9. library vs libreria
docs.text:
• English library = biblioteca or software library
  • Italian libreria = bookshop, or shelving unit depending on context

docs.title: Technical example
docs.text: The software module relies on an external encryption library.

docs.title: 10. fabric vs fabbrica
docs.text:
• English fabric = textile material
  • Italian fabbrica = factory / plant

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The unit is manufactured at the main production plant.
  • Protective fabric covers were used during transport.

docs.title: 11. camera vs camera
docs.text:
• English camera = optical or video device
  • Italian camera = room in many non-technical contexts

docs.title: Example
docs.text: The inspection camera provided visual access to the internal cavity.

docs.title: 12. large vs largo
docs.text:
• English large = big / ampio
  • Italian largo may mean wide rather than big in many technical contexts

docs.title: Better distinction
docs.text:
• large enclosure = big overall
  • wide slot = largo in width dimension

docs.title: 13. realization vs realizzazione
docs.text: • English realization often means awareness or understanding, or in some contexts implementation, but it is not always the best translation of Italian realizzazione.

docs.title: Better alternatives depending on meaning
docs.text:
• implementation
  • manufacture
  • development
  • production
  • completion

docs.title: Example
docs.text: The implementation of the revised interface logic reduced configuration error risk.

docs.title: 14. eventuality vs eventualità
docs.text:
This pair can also mislead learners.

  • eventuality in English means a possible event, often in risk or planning contexts;
  • however, it is not always the most natural choice.

docs.title: Often better alternatives
docs.text:
• possibility
  • scenario
  • risk case
  • contingency

docs.title: 15. correspondent vs corrispondente
docs.text:
• English correspondent often means reporter or person who exchanges correspondence
  • Italian corrispondente in technical comparison often means corresponding

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The corresponding requirement is listed in Section 5.
  • The foreign correspondent filed a report from the site.

docs.title: 16. accurate vs accurato
docs.text:
• English accurate = precise, correct
  • Italian accurato may also mean careful or well executed

docs.title: Better distinction
docs.text:
• accurate measurement = measurement with high precision/correctness
  • careful review = revisione accurata nel senso di attenta

docs.title: 17. consistent vs consistente
docs.text:
• English consistent = coherent, compatible, stable in pattern
  • Italian consistente often means substantial or significant in quantity

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
• The results are consistent with the previous test campaign.
  • A substantial deviation was observed.

docs.title: 18. inspection vs ispezione / controllo
docs.text: This is not a pure false friend, but it causes confusion because inspection is more formal and specific than general check.

docs.title: Distinction
docs.text:
• check = quick verification
  • inspection = structured and often documented examination
  • review = documentary or analytical examination
  • audit = formal compliance or system-level evaluation

docs.title: 19. notation vs notazione / nota
docs.text: English notation is usually a symbolic or representational system, not a short written note.

docs.title: Better alternatives
docs.text:
• note
  • comment
  • entry
  • annotation

docs.title: 20. Final strategy for dealing with false friends
docs.text:
When a word looks "too easy" because it resembles Italian closely, pause and verify:

  • Does it carry the same meaning in English?
  • Is it natural in technical prose?
  • Does it match the function of the sentence?
  • Would a native technical reviewer actually use this word here?

docs.title: Quick-reference examples
docs.text:
• attuale → current, not automatically actual
  • eventuale → possible / potential / if required, not automatically eventual
  • sensibile → sensitive, not sensible
  • sopportare → often withstand, not always support
  • controllare → often check / inspect / verify / review, not always control

docs.title: Final note
docs.text:
Similarity is not reliability

In technical English, words that look familiar are often the most dangerous because they reduce the learner’s instinct to verify meaning. Good professional writing requires disciplined lexical checking, especially when a term appears deceptively transparent.

docs.title: Purpose of this page
docs.text:
This page groups English expressions by communicative function rather than by grammar category alone. In professional engineering communication, the writer does not start by asking, "Which tense should I use?" The writer usually starts by asking, "What exactly am I trying to do in this sentence?" Am I defining a system? Reporting a finding? Introducing a requirement? Requesting action? Limiting a conclusion? Recording a decision?

Functional language is useful because it helps the learner choose wording according to the task performed by the document. This is particularly important in engineering contexts, where a small change in wording may change the professional status of a sentence.

docs.title: 1. Defining and describing
docs.text: Use this language when introducing a component, subsystem, document section, process step, or technical objective.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The purpose of this section is to describe...
  • This subsystem is intended to...
  • The unit consists of...
  • The assembly is mounted on...
  • The main function of the module is to...
  • The interface provides access to...
  • The current configuration includes...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Definition language should be stable, neutral, and free from unnecessary evaluation. Avoid inserting opinion into a descriptive sentence unless evaluation is genuinely required.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: The module consists of a sealed enclosure, an interface connector, and an internal processing board. Its primary function is to collect status data and transmit it to the supervisory controller.

docs.title: 2. Stating purpose and design intent
docs.text: This function explains why a component, arrangement, or procedure exists.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The bracket is designed to provide...
  • This arrangement has been selected to improve...
  • The revision was introduced to reduce...
  • The update is intended to support...
  • The selected layout allows...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Purpose language should not be confused with performance claims. Saying that a system was designed to do something is not identical to proving that it has already done it successfully under all conditions.

docs.title: 3. Reporting observations
docs.text: Use this language to state what was actually seen, measured, or recorded.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• Minor surface wear was observed on...
  • No abnormal behaviour was detected during...
  • The measured value exceeded...
  • Visual inspection identified...
  • The observed condition remained within...
  • Intermittent signal loss was recorded at...
  • No visible damage was identified.

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Observation language should remain disciplined. Avoid adding explanation, recommendation, and speculation in the same sentence unless the structure is carefully controlled.

docs.title: 4. Interpreting findings with appropriate caution
docs.text: Technical writing often requires interpretation, but interpretation must be clearly distinguished from fact.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The available evidence suggests that...
  • The issue appears to be related to...
  • The current data does not support a definitive conclusion regarding...
  • This behaviour is consistent with...
  • The trend may indicate...
  • A contributing factor may have been...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Use these forms when the evidence is meaningful but not fully conclusive. They are particularly important in fault analysis and early-stage review documents.

docs.title: 5. Stating requirements
docs.text: This function is central to specifications, procedures, interface definitions, and compliance-oriented documentation.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The unit shall...
  • Verification shall be performed by...
  • The interface shall remain accessible for...
  • Operation shall not continue unless...
  • The document shall include...
  • Each change shall be recorded in...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Requirement language should be precise, enforceable, and testable. Avoid vague expressions such as should be good, must be adequate, or as soon as possible unless they are replaced by controlled criteria.

docs.title: 6. Recommending action
docs.text: Use this function when proposing a next step without formalising it as a requirement.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• Additional verification is recommended.
  • Further analysis should be conducted if...
  • A follow-up inspection is advised after...
  • The current draft should be updated to reflect...
  • A short alignment meeting is recommended before release.
  • The evidence should be reviewed against the current baseline.

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Recommendations are strong when they are justified and clearly scoped. Avoid writing recommendations that do not specify who should act or under what condition they become relevant.

docs.title: 7. Recording decisions and status
docs.text: Meeting minutes, review notes, and programme updates depend on reliable status language.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The team agreed to retain the current arrangement pending further verification.
  • No change was approved at this stage.
  • Action 03 remains open until dimensional confirmation is received.
  • The document is ready for internal review.
  • The proposed update has been provisionally accepted.
  • Release is currently on hold pending closure of review comments.

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Decision language should always preserve traceability. The reader should understand what was decided, by whom, under what condition, and whether the decision is final or provisional.

docs.title: 8. Requesting clarification or information
docs.text: This function is essential in meetings, emails, and review comments.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• Could you clarify whether this point affects the external interface?
  • Please confirm whether the revised wording is acceptable.
  • Do we have verified data for this assumption?
  • Please advise whether the change applies to all configurations or to the tested unit only.
  • Can you confirm the intended requirement level for this statement?

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Well-formed requests are specific and answerable. Weak requests often force the recipient to guess what kind of response is needed.

docs.title: 9. Expressing limitation and scope
docs.text: Mature technical documents state clearly what they do not cover.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• This note applies to the tested configuration only.
  • The current assessment excludes...
  • The conclusion remains provisional pending...
  • The present section does not address...
  • The available evidence is sufficient for preliminary review only.
  • This statement shall not be interpreted as...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Limitation language is not a sign of weakness. It is a sign of technical discipline. It prevents overclaiming and improves review credibility.

docs.title: 10. Linking action and consequence
docs.text: Many engineering texts must show the consequence of an omitted check, incorrect condition, or design decision.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• Failure to perform this check may result in...
  • This action reduces the risk of...
  • Delayed closure may affect...
  • The proposed change would improve... while increasing...
  • Continued operation in this condition may lead to...
  • The revision is expected to improve...

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Consequence language should remain proportional. Use may, can, or is likely to when the relationship is plausible but not fully deterministic.

docs.title: 11. Escalating concern professionally
docs.text: Professional escalation language must be firm without becoming emotional.

docs.title: Core patterns
docs.text:
• The issue requires formal review before release.
  • The current evidence is insufficient to support approval.
  • The deviation should be assessed against the applicable requirement set.
  • Further action is necessary prior to operational use.
  • The risk remains open until verification has been completed.

docs.title: Usage note
docs.text: Escalation language should identify the reason for concern and the next step needed, not only the existence of a concern.

docs.title: Weak sentence
docs.text: The component is important and there is a problem and we need to see what to do.

docs.title: Functionally controlled version
docs.text: The component supports the lower interface and is therefore critical to alignment stability. Local deformation was identified during inspection. Further dimensional verification is recommended before release to service.

docs.title: Weak sentence
docs.text: Please check this document because maybe there are some issues.

docs.title: Functionally controlled version
docs.text: Please review the attached revision and confirm whether the updated acceptance wording in Section 6 is suitable for internal issue. Particular attention is requested for the traceability statement and closure criteria.

docs.title: Review checklist
docs.text:
When revising a paragraph, ask the following questions:

  • What is the primary function of each sentence?
  • Are observation and interpretation separated clearly?
  • Is the level of obligation appropriate?
  • Does the wording match the document type?
  • Is the request, recommendation, or decision traceable?

docs.title: Final note
docs.text:
Functional language reduces ambiguity
