Focus areas: - components and assemblies; - systems and architectures; - processes and workflows; - performance, testing, and evaluation.

docs.title: Modules 09–12 — Documentary Writing
docs.text:
These modules strengthen structured professional writing.

Focus areas: - technical writing principles; - technical emails; - reports and summaries; - requirements and compliance language.

docs.title: Modules 13–15 — Operational Communication
docs.text:
These modules focus on communication in maintenance and risk-related contexts.

Focus areas: - maintenance language; - troubleshooting and fault analysis; - safety and incident communication.

docs.title: Modules 16–18 — Sector and Publication Layer
docs.text:
These modules extend the course toward specialised and publication-oriented output.

Focus areas: - defence-oriented and institutional technical language; - programme and procurement communication; - documentation architecture with MkDocs and GitHub.

docs.title: Before You Move to the Next Module
docs.text:
Before progressing, check whether you can:

  • understand the core terminology;
  • explain the main concepts in simple technical English;
  • complete the main task with reasonable control;
  • revise your output for clarity and structure.

docs.title: Useful Companion Sections
docs.text:
Use these sections alongside the modules:

  • Glossaries
  • Language Tools
  • Exercises
  • Templates and Models
  • Final Project

docs.title: Module 01 — Introduction to Technical English
docs.text:
Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

docs.title: Module at a glance
docs.text:
Attribute Value Module focus Introduction to Technical English Area Core Technical English Operational purpose Technical English is a professional operating language. Primary documentary output Orientation note and starter glossary entry set Priority companion resources General Engineering GlossaryGrammar ReferenceVocabulary Exercises

How to extract more value from this module

Complete the applied task first, then compare your draft against the related template, model answer, or playbook. This sequence produces better professional growth than reading every support page before writing.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Core Technical English

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Technical English is a professional operating language. It exists to make engineering actions intelligible, traceable, and reviewable. In practice, it supports specification, description, coordination, verification, and post-action analysis. The key idea of this module is that technical language should be judged by operational usefulness, not by decorative complexity.

In many engineering settings, weak language creates secondary technical risk. A vague statement can cause an incorrect assumption, an incomplete inspection, a delayed response, or an avoidable disagreement during review. This is why introductory control over technical English matters from the very beginning of the course.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• technical communication — the structured use of language to describe, instruct, report, or coordinate technical work. Example: Technical communication should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • controlled language — a deliberately limited and consistent way of writing used to reduce ambiguity. Example: Controlled language should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • traceability — the ability to connect a statement, action, or decision to a source, step, or revision history. Example: Traceability should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • specification — a document or statement that defines characteristics, limits, or expected performance. Example: Specification should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • procedure — a structured sequence of actions to be performed in a defined order. Example: Procedure should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • verification — the activity of checking whether a requirement or expectation has been satisfied. Example: Verification should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • clarity — the quality of being easy to understand without unnecessary interpretation. Example: Clarity should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • consistency — the stable use of the same terms, units, formatting, and logic across a text. Example: Consistency should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • audience — the reader or listener for whom the technical message is intended. Example: Audience should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • documentary purpose — the practical reason a document exists, such as instruction, evidence, or reference. Example: Documentary purpose should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• technical communication: typical collocation pattern — technical communication management, technical communication verification, or similar context-based use.
  • controlled language: typical collocation pattern — controlled language management, controlled language verification, or similar context-based use.
  • traceability: typical collocation pattern — traceability management, traceability verification, or similar context-based use.
  • specification: typical collocation pattern — specification management, specification verification, or similar context-based use.
  • procedure: typical collocation pattern — procedure management, procedure verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
The module focuses on definition patterns, functional statements, and the distinction between informal explanation and documentary wording. It introduces high-value structures such as X is intended to..., The purpose of this document is to..., and This section provides....

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• defining a technical concept
  • stating purpose and scope
  • distinguishing reader-oriented and action-oriented writing
  • introducing documentary hierarchy

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — introductory system note
docs.text: The purpose of this section is to provide a concise description of the monitoring unit and its documentary role within the maintenance package. The unit is not presented here from a design perspective. Instead, the objective is to identify the information required by the technician, reviewer, and project coordinator. The monitoring unit records selected operational parameters, stores event data, and supports post-event analysis. For documentation purposes, the most important characteristics are its interface points, status indications, and inspection requirements.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: This unit is important and it does many things for the system. You can use it to check what is happening and it helps with maintenance.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The unit supports condition monitoring by recording selected parameters and presenting basic status information to the operator. For maintenance purposes, it also provides event data that may assist fault confirmation and post-event review.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A junior team member is asked to write the opening paragraph of a maintenance chapter. The first draft is linguistically understandable but does not identify audience, scope, or documentary purpose. Your task is to rewrite the paragraph so that the reader immediately understands what the section covers, why it exists, and how the information should be used.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Draft a 220–300 word introductory page for a technical subsystem. Explain scope, intended audience, and the difference between descriptive information and action-oriented instructions.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create a documentation page called technical-english-principles.md containing definitions, two weak-vs-strong examples, and a short style note for future modules.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.title: Quality checklist for revision
docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.title: Common weaknesses observed at B2 level
docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A newly assigned junior engineer must join an international design-support team and contribute to short technical notes without overusing informal language.

docs.title: Documentary risk
docs.text: The main risk is that the engineer writes grammatically acceptable sentences that remain too generic, too conversational, or too vague for traceable documentation.

docs.title: Premium response strategy
docs.text:
Before drafting the answer, the learner should stabilise the documentary function of the text. In other words, the writer must decide whether the page is primarily descriptive, explanatory, prescriptive, diagnostic, or evaluative. This step is essential because engineering English becomes weak when one paragraph tries to serve several functions at once without signalling the shift.

A strong response should also separate three layers of meaning:

  1. technical fact, meaning what was observed, measured, configured, installed, checked, or recorded;
  2. engineering interpretation, meaning what those facts appear to indicate under the current evidence boundary;
  3. documentary consequence, meaning what the reader should now understand, verify, approve, revise, or do.

docs.title: Model answer logic
docs.text: A strong introductory answer should define scope, state function, identify the relevant object or process, and avoid promotional adjectives such as excellent, very good, or advanced unless those terms are supported by a measurable criterion.

docs.title: Premium model paragraph
docs.text:
In a premium-quality submission, the learner should normally produce a paragraph or section that can survive technical review with minimal rewriting. A useful model is the following:

The text should open by identifying the relevant object, process, document, or activity in unambiguous terms. It should then clarify the operational or documentary reason why the subject matters. Once the context is stable, the writer should move to controlled detail, using terms consistently and avoiding unnecessary synonym changes. The conclusion should not merely repeat the opening; instead, it should state status, implication, limitation, or recommended follow-up in a form that a reviewer can act on.

This model paragraph is deliberately generic so that it can be transferred across modules. The learner is encouraged to adapt it to the specific document type rather than memorise it mechanically.

docs.title: Teacher note and correction focus
docs.text: At this stage, reward control and discipline more than lexical showmanship. If the learner can produce short, stable, well-structured sentences, the foundation is strong even if the vocabulary range is not yet wide.

docs.title: Premium correction questions
docs.text:
When reviewing student work for this module, the teacher or reviewer should test the page against the following questions:

  • Does the learner understand the purpose of the text, or only its topic?
  • Are technical terms used consistently enough to support review and reuse?
  • Does the wording remain proportionate to the evidence available?
  • Is there a visible distinction between description, interpretation, and recommendation?
  • Could this text be inserted into the final repository with limited revision, or does it still read like classroom output?

Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

How to extract more value from this module

Complete the applied task first, then compare your draft against the related template, model answer, or playbook. This sequence produces better professional growth than reading every support page before writing.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Core Technical English

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Engineering vocabulary becomes useful only when it is organised by function, hierarchy, and documentary use. This module develops an active lexical base that supports description, explanation, and reporting across different technical domains.

Many learners know isolated words but cannot use them productively. They may recognise component and system but fail to show the structural relationship between them. Professional writing requires not only vocabulary size, but lexical architecture.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• component — a discrete part that contributes to the function of an assembly or system. Example: Component should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • assembly — a group of connected parts that operate together as a functional unit. Example: Assembly should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • subsystem — a major functional section within a larger system. Example: Subsystem should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • interface — a point at which systems, subsystems, or users interact. Example: Interface should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • housing — the protective body or enclosure of a component or device. Example: Housing should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • actuator — a device that converts control input into physical movement or action. Example: Actuator should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • sensor — a device that detects or measures a condition or parameter. Example: Sensor should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • constraint — a limit that influences design, operation, or maintenance. Example: Constraint should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • reliability — the probability or tendency of a system to perform as intended over time. Example: Reliability should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • serviceable — still suitable for operation or continued use. Example: Serviceable should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• component: typical collocation pattern — component management, component verification, or similar context-based use.
  • assembly: typical collocation pattern — assembly management, assembly verification, or similar context-based use.
  • subsystem: typical collocation pattern — subsystem management, subsystem verification, or similar context-based use.
  • interface: typical collocation pattern — interface management, interface verification, or similar context-based use.
  • housing: typical collocation pattern — housing management, housing verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
Language work focuses on compound nouns, noun stacks, classification language, and frequent verb–noun collocations such as perform a check, withstand vibration, monitor temperature, and maintain alignment.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• classifying items by function
  • describing hierarchy from part to system
  • using collocations instead of word-by-word translation
  • building glossary entries with usage notes

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — vocabulary in system hierarchy
docs.text: The communication subsystem consists of a control board, two interface modules, a power-conditioning stage, and the associated connectors. Each component performs a different function, but the subsystem is documented as a single replaceable unit for maintenance planning purposes. The distinction between component-level description and subsystem-level description is therefore operationally significant.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: The system has many pieces and all the pieces are important for operation.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The system is divided into several subsystems, each of which contains components with distinct electrical, structural, or interface functions.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A learner is asked to write a glossary page but includes only bilingual word pairs. The entries do not distinguish hierarchy, document use, or collocation. Redesign the glossary so that it supports future writing tasks rather than passive memorisation only.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Prepare ten glossary entries from one engineering field. Each entry must contain a definition, one example sentence, one collocation, and one usage warning for Italian-speaking learners.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

How to extract more value from this module

Complete the applied task first, then compare your draft against the related template, model answer, or playbook. This sequence produces better professional growth than reading every support page before writing.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Core Technical English

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Technical grammar is not an academic ornament. It determines obligation, scope, sequence, and evidential focus. This module studies grammar as a precision tool for engineering discourse.

In requirements, procedures, and reports, the difference between shall, should, must, and may is functionally important. The same is true for passive voice, conditionals, and relative clauses. Weak grammatical choices often produce ambiguity even when vocabulary is correct.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• passive voice — a grammatical structure in which the action or result is foregrounded rather than the actor. Example: Passive voice should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • modal verb — a verb used to express obligation, possibility, recommendation, or permission. Example: Modal verb should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • requirement language — the set of grammatical choices used to impose or define expected conditions. Example: Requirement language should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • conditional statement — a sentence that expresses what happens if a condition is met or not met. Example: Conditional statement should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • relative clause — a clause that adds precise identifying information to a noun. Example: Relative clause should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • sequence marker — a word or phrase used to show order between steps or events. Example: Sequence marker should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • limitation — a stated boundary on applicability, use, or interpretation. Example: Limitation should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • obligation — a mandatory condition or action. Example: Obligation should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • recommendation — an advised but not mandatory action. Example: Recommendation should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • tolerance — an allowable range of variation. Example: Tolerance should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• passive voice: typical collocation pattern — passive voice management, passive voice verification, or similar context-based use.
  • modal verb: typical collocation pattern — modal verb management, modal verb verification, or similar context-based use.
  • requirement language: typical collocation pattern — requirement language management, requirement language verification, or similar context-based use.
  • conditional statement: typical collocation pattern — conditional statement management, conditional statement verification, or similar context-based use.
  • relative clause: typical collocation pattern — relative clause management, relative clause verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
Key grammar points include passive structures for process description, modal verbs for obligation and recommendation, first-condition patterns for procedures and troubleshooting, and relative clauses for precise identification of parts, signals, or requirements.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• writing requirements that are mandatory but not overstated
  • reporting observations without unjustified certainty
  • expressing sequence and dependency
  • clarifying which element a condition applies to

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — modal control in a procedure
docs.text: The connector shall be inspected prior to installation. If contamination is detected, the surface shall be cleaned using an approved method before the unit is energised. The connector may be reused provided that no deformation, cracking, or pin damage is observed. Additional torque verification should be performed after reassembly.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: You have to inspect the connector and maybe clean it. It should not be bad and you can use it again if it is okay.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The connector shall be inspected prior to installation. Reuse is permitted only if no physical damage is observed and interface integrity remains within the specified acceptance criteria.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A draft requirement uses must, can, and should inconsistently. The engineering meaning is unclear and reviewers disagree about whether the statements are mandatory or advisory. Rephrase the requirement set using a stable modal logic and explain your choices.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.text: For V4-level work, the learner should not simply upload the page. The page should be reviewed as if it were part of a living engineering knowledge base. This means checking title precision, heading hierarchy, internal links, document scope, tone stability, and reuse value.

docs.title: Premium cross-reference and repository path

docs.title: Module 04 — Reading Technical Texts
docs.text:
Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

docs.title: Module at a glance
docs.text:
Attribute Value Module focus Reading Technical Texts Area Core Technical English Operational purpose Technical reading is selective, goal-oriented, and evidence-driven. Primary documentary output Annotated technical-reading record Priority companion resources Reading ExercisesPhrasebank for ReportingReport Models

How to extract more value from this module

Complete the applied task first, then compare your draft against the related template, model answer, or playbook. This sequence produces better professional growth than reading every support page before writing.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Core Technical English

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Technical reading is selective, goal-oriented, and evidence-driven. The reader often searches for a parameter, a condition, a warning, a limitation, or a procedural dependency rather than reading linearly for general interest.

In engineering practice, reading efficiency affects safety, maintenance quality, and review speed. Readers must often navigate dense documents under time pressure. They therefore need strategies for extracting only what is operationally relevant without losing context.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• scope — the coverage and limits of a document or section. Example: Scope should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • assumption — a condition accepted as true for the purpose of analysis or operation. Example: Assumption should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • constraint — a limit that affects validity or use. Example: Constraint should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • warning — a statement indicating a hazard or serious negative consequence. Example: Warning should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • caution — a statement indicating risk of damage, error, or undesired condition. Example: Caution should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • nominal — referring to the expected or standard operating condition. Example: Nominal should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • parameter — a measurable variable or defining characteristic. Example: Parameter should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • acceptance criteria — the conditions that must be satisfied for acceptance. Example: Acceptance criteria should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • appendix — supporting material placed outside the main body of a document. Example: Appendix should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • reference — a cited document, standard, or section used for support. Example: Reference should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• scope: typical collocation pattern — scope management, scope verification, or similar context-based use.
  • assumption: typical collocation pattern — assumption management, assumption verification, or similar context-based use.
  • constraint: typical collocation pattern — constraint management, constraint verification, or similar context-based use.
  • warning: typical collocation pattern — warning management, warning verification, or similar context-based use.
  • caution: typical collocation pattern — caution management, caution verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
The module emphasises scanning for data, recognising document signals, understanding cautionary language, and distinguishing requirement statements from explanatory notes. Learners also practise annotating texts for audience, action, and evidential status.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• locating critical data efficiently
  • recognising section function in manuals and reports
  • distinguishing description from instruction
  • annotating texts for later reuse in documentation

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — extracting data from a technical note
docs.text: The unit is designed for nominal operation between −20 °C and +55 °C. Short-duration storage outside this range is permitted only under non-operational conditions and shall not exceed the limits stated in Table 4. The connector interface shall remain protected during transport. Routine verification is required after any exposure to severe vibration or moisture ingress. From a reading perspective, the reader should immediately extract the operational range, the storage limitation, the protective condition, and the trigger for verification.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: Read the text and try to understand the important information.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: Before reading in detail, identify the operational range, the storage limitation, the protection requirement, and the condition that triggers additional verification.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A technician misreads an advisory note as a mandatory requirement, causing unnecessary delay. Review a short passage and mark which sentences are mandatory, advisory, descriptive, or conditional.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Prepare an annotated reading sheet for a one-page manual extract. Include purpose, audience, critical values, mandatory actions, and vocabulary notes.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task
docs.text: Summarise a dense paragraph orally in under sixty seconds, preserving only the information that is operationally relevant to the user.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create reading-strategy-checklist.md with a method for extracting limits, warnings, conditions, and references from technical documents.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.title: Quality checklist for revision
docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.title: Common weaknesses observed at B2 level
docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

docs.title: Cross-references
docs.text:
• Reading Exercises
  • Functional Language
  • Compliance And Standards Terms

docs.title: Recommended follow-up
docs.text: After completing this module, the learner should revise one earlier output from a previous section and improve it using the terminology, discourse patterns, and revision criteria introduced here. This repeated reworking is one of the fastest ways to build documentary maturity.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A learner must extract the relevant acceptance criteria from a dense technical page without copying unnecessary narrative detail.

docs.title: Documentary risk
docs.text: Reading tasks often fail because learners either translate every line or skim without identifying purpose, scope, and evidence-bearing sections.

docs.title: Premium response strategy
docs.text:
Before drafting the answer, the learner should stabilise the documentary function of the text. In other words, the writer must decide whether the page is primarily descriptive, explanatory, prescriptive, diagnostic, or evaluative. This step is essential because engineering English becomes weak when one paragraph tries to serve several functions at once without signalling the shift.

A strong response should also separate three layers of meaning:

  1. technical fact, meaning what was observed, measured, configured, installed, checked, or recorded;
  2. engineering interpretation, meaning what those facts appear to indicate under the current evidence boundary;
  3. documentary consequence, meaning what the reader should now understand, verify, approve, revise, or do.

docs.title: Model answer logic
docs.text: A strong answer identifies document purpose first, then extracts only the details that affect operation, compliance, configuration, risk, or decision-making.

docs.title: Premium model paragraph
docs.text:
In a premium-quality submission, the learner should normally produce a paragraph or section that can survive technical review with minimal rewriting. A useful model is the following:

The text should open by identifying the relevant object, process, document, or activity in unambiguous terms. It should then clarify the operational or documentary reason why the subject matters. Once the context is stable, the writer should move to controlled detail, using terms consistently and avoiding unnecessary synonym changes. The conclusion should not merely repeat the opening; instead, it should state status, implication, limitation, or recommended follow-up in a form that a reviewer can act on.

This model paragraph is deliberately generic so that it can be transferred across modules. The learner is encouraged to adapt it to the specific document type rather than memorise it mechanically.

docs.title: Teacher note and correction focus
docs.text: Insist on information hierarchy. A good learner does not merely understand the page; they can explain which details matter and why.

docs.title: Premium correction questions
docs.text:
When reviewing student work for this module, the teacher or reviewer should test the page against the following questions:

  • Does the learner understand the purpose of the text, or only its topic?
  • Are technical terms used consistently enough to support review and reuse?
  • Does the wording remain proportionate to the evidence available?
  • Is there a visible distinction between description, interpretation, and recommendation?
  • Could this text be inserted into the final repository with limited revision, or does it still read like classroom output?

docs.title: Repository integration task
docs.text: Add an annotated-reading page showing how to scan headings, tables, cautions, and constraints before writing a summary.

docs.title: Repository maturity note
docs.text: For V4-level work, the learner should not simply upload the page. The page should be reviewed as if it were part of a living engineering knowledge base. This means checking title precision, heading hierarchy, internal links, document scope, tone stability, and reuse value.

docs.title: Premium cross-reference and repository path
docs.text: This module should be studied together with Reading Exercises and the related template or exercise pages. Premium progression depends on repeated transfer, not on single-pass reading.

docs.title: Module 05 — Describing Components and Assemblies
docs.text:
Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

docs.text:
Attribute Value Module focus Describing Components and Assemblies Area Engineering Communication Operational purpose Component description requires the writer to combine physical, functional, and relational information. How to extract more value from this module

Complete the applied task first, then compare your draft against the related template, model answer, or playbook. This sequence produces better professional growth than reading every support page before writing.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Engineering Communication

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Component description requires the writer to combine physical, functional, and relational information. A good technical description identifies what the item is, where it is located, how it is connected, what it is made of, and why it matters within the assembly.

Weak part descriptions often sound acceptable in conversation but fail in documentation because they omit location, interface, dimensions, material relevance, or functional role. This module develops concise but sufficiently explicit component-level writing.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• fastener — a hardware item such as a bolt, screw, or rivet used to secure parts together. Example: Fastener should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • enclosure — a protective outer structure that houses internal elements. Example: Enclosure should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • connector — an interface element used to join electrical or signal paths. Example: Connector should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • mounting bracket — a structural support used to attach a component to a larger assembly. Example: Mounting bracket should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • bearing — a part that supports and guides motion while reducing friction. Example: Bearing should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • alignment — the correct positional relationship between components. Example: Alignment should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • tolerance — the allowable variation in dimension or fit. Example: Tolerance should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • fitment — the way a part is fitted or installed within an assembly. Example: Fitment should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • retention — the means by which a part is held in place. Example: Retention should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • sealing surface — a contact area designed to prevent leakage or contamination. Example: Sealing surface should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• fastener: typical collocation pattern — fastener management, fastener verification, or similar context-based use.
  • enclosure: typical collocation pattern — enclosure management, enclosure verification, or similar context-based use.
  • connector: typical collocation pattern — connector management, connector verification, or similar context-based use.
  • mounting bracket: typical collocation pattern — mounting bracket management, mounting bracket verification, or similar context-based use.
  • bearing: typical collocation pattern — bearing management, bearing verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
Language work focuses on spatial phrasing, material statements, dimensional reference language, and function-led description. Useful frames include is mounted on, is secured by, is positioned between, provides structural support for, and prevents relative movement between.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• describing geometry and position
  • explaining how parts are joined
  • distinguishing visible description from functional significance
  • moving from inventory-style lists to integrated prose

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — assembly description
docs.text: The interface module is housed within an aluminium enclosure mounted on the left-hand support frame. The enclosure is secured by four captive fasteners and includes a sealed front connector for maintenance access. Internally, the module is retained by guide rails that maintain alignment during installation and removal. The arrangement has been selected to simplify replacement while limiting exposure of the internal electronics to contamination.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: The module is inside a box on the frame and it has screws and a connector.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The module is housed in a sealed aluminium enclosure mounted on the left-hand support frame. Four captive fasteners secure the enclosure, while a front-access connector provides controlled maintenance interface without full removal of the assembly.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A learner describes a component only by naming its parts. The review comment states that the text is “list-like but not technically explanatory.” Expand the description so that physical arrangement, interface logic, and maintenance relevance become explicit.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Write a 250–350 word description of an assembly containing at least one enclosure, one connector, one support element, and one retention feature.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task
docs.text: Describe a component from a drawing or photograph, moving from identification to location, connection, function, and maintenance relevance.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create component-description-model.md with labelled sections for identity, position, materials, interfaces, and function.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

docs.title: Module 06 — Describing Systems and Architectures
docs.text:
Repository intent

This module is designed as a teaching page, a revision reference, and a repository asset. The learner should therefore read it not only for linguistic content, but also for documentary behaviour: how the page defines scope, stabilises terminology, and supports future reuse in a MkDocs environment.

docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Engineering Communication

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
System description moves beyond single objects and requires the writer to show hierarchy, interaction, and purpose. This module develops language for explaining how subsystems cooperate, how information or energy flows, and how architecture supports mission or operational requirements.

In project work and technical review, the ability to describe architecture clearly is essential. Reviewers need to understand boundaries, interfaces, dependencies, and the rationale behind system decomposition.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• architecture — the high-level organisation of a system and the relationships between its elements. Example: Architecture should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • subsystem — a major functional element within a larger system. Example: Subsystem should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • interface — the point at which information, energy, material, or control is exchanged. Example: Interface should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • data flow — the movement of information through a system. Example: Data flow should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • control logic — the rules or mechanisms by which the system responds to inputs and states. Example: Control logic should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • redundancy — the deliberate duplication of elements to increase availability or resilience. Example: Redundancy should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • integration — the combination of elements into a coordinated whole. Example: Integration should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • dependency — a relation in which one element relies on another. Example: Dependency should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • state — a defined system condition that influences behaviour. Example: State should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • operational mode — a specific configuration of system behaviour or use. Example: Operational mode should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• architecture: typical collocation pattern — architecture management, architecture verification, or similar context-based use.
  • subsystem: typical collocation pattern — subsystem management, subsystem verification, or similar context-based use.
  • interface: typical collocation pattern — interface management, interface verification, or similar context-based use.
  • data flow: typical collocation pattern — data flow management, data flow verification, or similar context-based use.
  • control logic: typical collocation pattern — control logic management, control logic verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
The module uses architecture verbs such as consists of, interfaces with, routes, provides, receives, supervises, and isolates. Learners also practise relational clauses that explain why an architectural feature exists, not only what it is.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• moving from subsystem inventory to system-level narrative
  • explaining interface logic
  • showing how architecture supports reliability or maintainability
  • summarising complex systems without overloading the reader

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — architecture summary
docs.text: The monitoring architecture is divided into three main subsystems: sensing, processing, and communication. The sensing subsystem acquires raw environmental and equipment-status data. The processing subsystem filters, validates, and stores the relevant values while generating threshold-based events. The communication subsystem routes selected information to the operator interface and to the maintenance port. A modular architecture has been adopted to simplify replacement, isolate faults, and support configuration growth.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: The system has sensors, software, and communication parts that work together.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The system is organised into sensing, processing, and communication subsystems, each of which performs a defined role and exchanges controlled information through documented interfaces.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A system overview slide lists subsystems but does not explain why the architecture has been divided that way. Rewrite the explanation so that functional boundaries and engineering rationale become visible to the reader.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Produce a one-page architecture description for a dual-use monitoring or control system. Include hierarchy, interfaces, data flow, and one maintainability consideration.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task
docs.text: Give a two-minute architecture briefing in which you explain the role of each subsystem and one key interface dependency.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create system-architecture-overview.md with a subsystem table and an explanatory narrative.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.title: Quality checklist for revision
docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.title: Common weaknesses observed at B2 level
docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.
