docs.title: Module identity
docs.text: Area: Defence, Dual-Use and Institutional Language

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
This module introduces the terminology and register typically found in defence-oriented engineering documentation while preserving a neutral, professional, and documentation-centred approach.

Dual-use and defence contexts often require language that is more formal, more traceable, and more institutionally controlled than ordinary commercial discourse. The writer must remain precise without becoming dramatic or obscure.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries
docs.text: This module addresses English as used in technical and documentary contexts. It does not attempt to replace domain-specific engineering expertise. Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• platform — the base vehicle, vessel, aircraft, or system that carries other capabilities. Example: Platform should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • payload — the functional equipment carried to perform a mission or task. Example: Payload should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • mission profile — the expected pattern of use or deployment for a system. Example: Mission profile should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • interoperability — the ability of systems or organisations to work together effectively. Example: Interoperability should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • readiness — the state of being prepared for required use or deployment. Example: Readiness should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • supportability — the extent to which a system can be maintained and supported in service. Example: Supportability should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • surveillance — the systematic observation or monitoring of an area, object, or condition. Example: Surveillance should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • deployment — the movement or assignment of systems for operational use. Example: Deployment should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • sustainment — the continuing support needed to keep a capability operational over time. Example: Sustainment should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • command and control — the organisational and technical framework for directing operations and resources. Example: Command and control should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Terminology note
docs.text:
A term becomes professionally useful only when the learner can do four things with it:

  1. define it with sufficient precision;
  2. place it in a realistic engineering sentence;
  3. connect it to the appropriate document type;
  4. distinguish it from near-synonyms or broader general-English wording.

docs.title: Collocation and stability reminder
docs.text:
• platform: typical collocation pattern — platform management, platform verification, or similar context-based use.
  • payload: typical collocation pattern — payload management, payload verification, or similar context-based use.
  • mission profile: typical collocation pattern — mission profile management, mission profile verification, or similar context-based use.
  • interoperability: typical collocation pattern — interoperability management, interoperability verification, or similar context-based use.
  • readiness: typical collocation pattern — readiness management, readiness verification, or similar context-based use.

The learner should record not only the term itself, but also the grammatical and documentary environment in which the term commonly appears.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
The module focuses on neutral institutional wording, capability description, mission-oriented context statements, and the difference between technical description and operational rhetoric.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.title: Engineering discourse patterns
docs.text:
• describing capabilities without promotional exaggeration
  • maintaining neutral tone in formal settings
  • distinguishing mission context from technical function
  • writing in a way that remains acceptable for both civil and defence-oriented readers

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — capability description
docs.text: The surveillance payload is integrated on a modular platform architecture that supports rapid replacement and configuration control. From a documentation perspective, the most relevant features are interface compatibility, supportability constraints, and environmental operating limits. The present description is limited to engineering and sustainment aspects and does not address operational doctrine.

docs.title: Why the example works
docs.text: The example is stronger than a typical classroom answer because it does more than name objects or actions. It gives the reader a controlled amount of context, identifies what is functionally important, and uses wording that can reasonably appear in professional documentation. It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: This platform is very advanced and gives excellent mission capability in many scenarios.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: The platform supports modular payload integration and is documented to emphasise interface compatibility, supportability, and environmental limits relevant to engineering and sustainment activities.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A draft text adopts a promotional tone inappropriate for an institutional review package. Rewrite the text so that capability, limitation, and supportability are described in neutral engineering language.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Prepare a neutral description of a dual-use platform or subsystem, emphasising interfaces, supportability, and operational constraints rather than marketing language.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task
docs.text: Explain why defence-oriented engineering language often requires stronger control over tone, traceability, and scope statements.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create defence-language-style-note.md with tone guidance and civil-versus-defence wording comparisons.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.title: Quality checklist for revision
docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.title: Common weaknesses observed at B2 level
docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

docs.title: Cross-references
docs.text:
• Terminology Comparison
  • Register And Tone
  • Defence And Dual Use Terms

docs.title: Recommended follow-up
docs.text: After completing this module, the learner should revise one earlier output from a previous section and improve it using the terminology, discourse patterns, and revision criteria introduced here. This repeated reworking is one of the fastest ways to build documentary maturity.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A dual-use systems team needs language that remains technically precise, operationally aware, and institutionally neutral across civil and defence-facing documents.

docs.title: Documentary risk
docs.text: Learners may drift into either over-militarised vocabulary or generic civilian wording that does not fit programme documentation.

docs.title: Premium response strategy
docs.text:
Before drafting the answer, the learner should stabilise the documentary function of the text. In other words, the writer must decide whether the page is primarily descriptive, explanatory, prescriptive, diagnostic, or evaluative. This step is essential because engineering English becomes weak when one paragraph tries to serve several functions at once without signalling the shift.

A strong response should also separate three layers of meaning:

  1. technical fact, meaning what was observed, measured, configured, installed, checked, or recorded;
  2. engineering interpretation, meaning what those facts appear to indicate under the current evidence boundary;
  3. documentary consequence, meaning what the reader should now understand, verify, approve, revise, or do.

docs.title: Model answer logic
docs.text: A premium answer shows how terms such as platform, mission profile, readiness, interoperability, and supportability change nuance depending on document purpose.

docs.title: Premium model paragraph
docs.text:
In a premium-quality submission, the learner should normally produce a paragraph or section that can survive technical review with minimal rewriting. A useful model is the following:

The text should open by identifying the relevant object, process, document, or activity in unambiguous terms. It should then clarify the operational or documentary reason why the subject matters. Once the context is stable, the writer should move to controlled detail, using terms consistently and avoiding unnecessary synonym changes. The conclusion should not merely repeat the opening; instead, it should state status, implication, limitation, or recommended follow-up in a form that a reviewer can act on.

This model paragraph is deliberately generic so that it can be transferred across modules. The learner is encouraged to adapt it to the specific document type rather than memorise it mechanically.

docs.title: Teacher note and correction focus
docs.text: Keep the focus on description, documentation, and communication quality. Reward controlled neutrality and contextual awareness more than dramatic terminology.

docs.title: Premium correction questions
docs.text:
When reviewing student work for this module, the teacher or reviewer should test the page against the following questions:

  • Does the learner understand the purpose of the text, or only its topic?
  • Are technical terms used consistently enough to support review and reuse?
  • Does the wording remain proportionate to the evidence available?
  • Is there a visible distinction between description, interpretation, and recommendation?
  • Could this text be inserted into the final repository with limited revision, or does it still read like classroom output?

docs.title: Repository integration task
docs.text: Develop a comparison page that maps civil, institutional, and defence-oriented wording without moving into sensitive operational instruction.

docs.title: Repository maturity note
docs.text: For V4-level work, the learner should not simply upload the page. The page should be reviewed as if it were part of a living engineering knowledge base. This means checking title precision, heading hierarchy, internal links, document scope, tone stability, and reuse value.

docs.title: Premium cross-reference and repository path
docs.text: This module should be studied together with Terminology Comparison and the related template or exercise pages. Premium progression depends on repeated transfer, not on single-pass reading.

docs.title: Module overview
docs.text:
Engineering communication at programme level must address deliverables, reviews, revisions, contractual expectations, and configuration discipline. This module introduces the associated language.

Technical teams often participate in procurement and programme communication even when they are not contract specialists. They therefore need a working command of terms related to deliverables, milestones, revision status, and compliance expectations.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, the learner should be able to:

  • understand and reuse the core terminology associated with the topic;
  • distinguish weak wording from professionally stronger engineering wording;
  • produce written and spoken output aligned with a clear documentary purpose;
  • revise initial drafts so that they become more precise, more consistent, and more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Professional significance
docs.text: This module has direct relevance to engineering work because it strengthens the language used to describe reality in a controlled form. Whether the learner is preparing a note, a report, a maintenance record, a review comment, or a presentation, the same recurring professional requirements apply: the wording must be understandable, proportional, review-friendly, and logically structured. The aim is not rhetorical sophistication. The aim is usable precision.

docs.title: Scope and boundaries

Instead, it gives the learner a language framework capable of carrying that expertise more effectively. In practical terms, this means the module focuses on language patterns, engineering logic, and document behaviour rather than on advanced specialist theory.

docs.title: Key vocabulary with usage orientation
docs.text:
• deliverable — a document, product, or output that must be provided under a plan or agreement. Example: Deliverable should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • milestone — a significant project point used to track progress or readiness. Example: Milestone should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • baseline — the approved reference configuration against which changes are controlled. Example: Baseline should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • revision status — the current version and maturity level of a document or item. Example: Revision status should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • statement of work — a formal description of tasks, outputs, and scope. Example: Statement of work should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • request for proposal — a document inviting suppliers to submit structured offers. Example: Request for proposal should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • configuration control — the discipline of managing approved changes to documented items. Example: Configuration control should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • stakeholder — a party with an interest in the outcome or progress of a project. Example: Stakeholder should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • acceptance review — a formal review used to determine whether an item can be accepted. Example: Acceptance review should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • compliance matrix — a structured mapping between requirements and evidence of compliance. Example: Compliance matrix should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

docs.title: Language and grammar focus
docs.text:
The module uses formal phrases such as subject to approval, for contractual review, to be delivered by, under the current baseline, and pending closure of open actions.

From a B2 perspective, the most important target is not merely grammatical correctness but functional control. A sentence can be grammatically possible and still fail as engineering communication if it hides the action, weakens the logic, or leaves the reader uncertain about what matters most.

docs.text:
• writing at programme rather than component level
  • describing document maturity and review status

A strong learner should gradually recognise these patterns not as isolated tricks, but as repeatable document functions that can be reused across multiple modules.

docs.title: Worked example — programme update note
docs.text: Revision 3 of the interface control document has been released for internal review under the current programme baseline. Closure of Action 07 remains a prerequisite for external issue because the connector allocation table still requires configuration-board confirmation. The associated compliance matrix will be updated once the dimensional review record is formally approved.

docs.title: Stronger version

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.text:
The final module turns language competence into structured digital documentation. Learners apply Markdown, navigation logic, version control principles, and editorial discipline to publish the course output as a coherent site.

In many organisations, good technical language has little impact if it remains buried in private notes or inconsistent files. A documentation system gives that language operational reach, review visibility, and future reuse.

  • publishable draft — a revised version acceptable for release to the intended audience. Example: Publishable draft should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • template — a reusable structure that standardises content creation. Example: Template should be recorded consistently across related documentation.
  • information architecture — the planned arrangement of content and navigation. Example: Information architecture should be recorded consistently across related documentation.

It also separates technical fact from interpretive comment, which is especially important in reports, procedures, and reviews.

docs.title: Weak version
docs.text: This page is about troubleshooting language and can help students.

docs.title: Stronger version
docs.text: This page provides a controlled reference for troubleshooting language used in reports, maintenance notes, and issue summaries. It consolidates reusable phrasing patterns and revision criteria for documentation purposes.

docs.title: Commentary
docs.text: The weak version is understandable but under-specified. It relies on general-language wording, compresses multiple ideas without hierarchy, and provides too little guidance about significance. The stronger version improves the result by stabilising terminology, clarifying the relationship between condition and action, and making the sentence more useful to a technical reader.

docs.title: Mini case study
docs.text: A repository contains many good pages, but navigation is inconsistent and the same term appears in different forms. Propose an editorial clean-up plan that improves findability, consistency, and publication quality.

docs.title: Suggested analysis path
docs.text:
When working on this case, the learner should ask the following questions:

  • What is the real documentary purpose of the text?
  • Which information is factual, and which part is interpretation or recommendation?
  • Which terms should remain stable throughout the paragraph or page?
  • Is the reader expected to understand, decide, verify, or act?
  • Which sentence currently carries too much meaning and should be split or reformulated?

docs.title: Applied writing task
docs.text: Prepare a documentation page with headings, links, one reusable table, and a short note explaining how it connects to the rest of the repository.

docs.title: Expected features of a strong answer
docs.text:
A strong answer should normally include:

  • an opening sentence that states the topic and function of the text;
  • stable terminology rather than unnecessary variation;
  • enough technical detail to be useful, but not so much that the paragraph loses control;
  • connectors that show sequence, logic, condition, comparison, or consequence where needed;
  • an ending that clarifies status, implication, or next step.

docs.title: Applied speaking task
docs.text: Explain the advantage of storing course outputs in MkDocs and GitHub rather than in disconnected word-processing files.

docs.title: Oral performance note
docs.text: In a technical speaking task, the learner does not need dramatic delivery. What matters more is controlled sequencing, correct terminology, explicit logic, and the ability to remain concise without becoming vague.

docs.title: Documentation output for the site
docs.text:
Create documentation-governance-note.md covering naming conventions, headings, links, glossary integration, and revision discipline.

This output should not be treated as disposable homework. It should be revised so that it can become part of the final documentation repository.

docs.title: Quality checklist for revision
docs.text:
Before publishing or submitting the page, verify the following:

  • Is the title specific and professionally useful?
  • Does the opening paragraph define scope and purpose clearly?
  • Are terms used consistently from beginning to end?
  • Are obligation, possibility, and recommendation distinguished correctly?
  • Are there any unsupported conclusions or overstatements?
  • Would a reader know what matters most after a quick scan?
  • Is the page structurally reusable in the final MkDocs project?

docs.title: Common weaknesses observed at B2 level
docs.text: Typical weaknesses at this stage include the transfer of Italian sentence rhythm into English, overuse of vague adjectives, insufficient distinction between observation and conclusion, and an understandable but weak tendency to translate directly rather than write according to document function. These issues are normal, but they must be actively corrected if the learner is expected to produce professional-quality output.

docs.title: Cross-references
docs.text:
• Project Guidelines
  • Revision Checklist
  • Publishing Guide

docs.title: Recommended follow-up
docs.text: After completing this module, the learner should revise one earlier output from a previous section and improve it using the terminology, discourse patterns, and revision criteria introduced here. This repeated reworking is one of the fastest ways to build documentary maturity.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A learner must convert dispersed course outputs into a coherent MkDocs repository with stable navigation, reusable pages, and revision discipline.

docs.title: Documentary risk
docs.text: The repository fails when pages are written as isolated assignments rather than as linked documentation assets with ownership, scope, and revision value.

docs.title: Premium response strategy
docs.text:
Before drafting the answer, the learner should stabilise the documentary function of the text. In other words, the writer must decide whether the page is primarily descriptive, explanatory, prescriptive, diagnostic, or evaluative. This step is essential because engineering English becomes weak when one paragraph tries to serve several functions at once without signalling the shift.

A strong response should also separate three layers of meaning:

  1. technical fact, meaning what was observed, measured, configured, installed, checked, or recorded;
  2. engineering interpretation, meaning what those facts appear to indicate under the current evidence boundary;
  3. documentary consequence, meaning what the reader should now understand, verify, approve, revise, or do.

docs.title: Model answer logic
docs.text: A premium answer treats the repository as an engineering information system. Page naming, navigation, internal linking, and version discipline become part of communication quality.

docs.title: Premium model paragraph
docs.text:
In a premium-quality submission, the learner should normally produce a paragraph or section that can survive technical review with minimal rewriting. A useful model is the following:

The text should open by identifying the relevant object, process, document, or activity in unambiguous terms. It should then clarify the operational or documentary reason why the subject matters. Once the context is stable, the writer should move to controlled detail, using terms consistently and avoiding unnecessary synonym changes. The conclusion should not merely repeat the opening; instead, it should state status, implication, limitation, or recommended follow-up in a form that a reviewer can act on.

This model paragraph is deliberately generic so that it can be transferred across modules. The learner is encouraged to adapt it to the specific document type rather than memorise it mechanically.

docs.title: Teacher note and correction focus
docs.text: Review the repository as a product, not only as language output. The learner should show information architecture judgement as well as writing control.

docs.title: Premium correction questions
docs.text:
When reviewing student work for this module, the teacher or reviewer should test the page against the following questions:

  • Does the learner understand the purpose of the text, or only its topic?
  • Are technical terms used consistently enough to support review and reuse?
  • Does the wording remain proportionate to the evidence available?
  • Is there a visible distinction between description, interpretation, and recommendation?
  • Could this text be inserted into the final repository with limited revision, or does it still read like classroom output?

docs.title: Repository integration task
docs.text: Build project guidance that covers structure, linking, naming conventions, revision discipline, and publication readiness.

docs.title: Repository maturity note
docs.text: For V4-level work, the learner should not simply upload the page. The page should be reviewed as if it were part of a living engineering knowledge base. This means checking title precision, heading hierarchy, internal links, document scope, tone stability, and reuse value.

docs.title: Premium cross-reference and repository path
docs.text: This module should be studied together with Publishing Guide and the related template or exercise pages. Premium progression depends on repeated transfer, not on single-pass reading.

docs.title: Purpose of This Section
docs.text:
The purpose of the Quality Assurance section is to define the editorial, linguistic, structural, and maintenance rules that govern the repository. In a documentation-driven course, quality assurance is not an optional final check carried out shortly before publication. It is a continuous discipline that influences how pages are planned, written, reviewed, linked, revised, and released.

This section therefore acts as the operational control layer of the project. It translates general intentions such as clarity, consistency, professional tone, and usability into concrete review criteria.

In practical terms, the pages in this section help the contributor answer questions such as the following:

  • Is this page written in a stable and professional register?
  • Does the terminology match the glossaries and modules already published?
  • Is the page architecturally coherent with the rest of the repository?
  • Are the internal links useful and intentional rather than decorative?
  • Is the page mature enough to be considered publication-ready?

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
The quality assurance framework applies to all content types in the repository, including:

  • course overview pages;
  • module pages;
  • glossaries;
  • language tools;
  • exercises;
  • templates;
  • civil vs defence comparison pages;
  • final project pages;
  • future appendices and release notes.

docs.title: Guiding Principle
docs.text:
The repository should be treated as a professional documentation environment rather than as a loose collection of teaching notes. As a consequence, every page should be evaluated not only for grammatical correctness, but also for:

  • information value;
  • readability;
  • structural integrity;
  • terminology governance;
  • documentation usefulness;
  • long-term maintainability.

docs.title: Pages in This Section
docs.text:
• Editorial Style Guide
  • Page Architecture Checklist
  • Cross-Linking Strategy
  • Glossary Governance
  • Release Readiness Checklist

docs.title: Recommended Use
docs.text:
A practical workflow is the following:

  1. Draft the page.
  2. Check structure against the page architecture checklist.
  3. Verify terminology against the glossary governance rules.
  4. Add and review links according to the cross-linking strategy.
  5. Align wording and tone with the editorial style guide.
  6. Perform a final publication check with the release readiness checklist.

docs.title: Quality Assurance as a Teaching Tool
docs.text:
This section also has a pedagogical role. Learners working at B2 level often believe that technical writing is mainly a matter of translating vocabulary from Italian into English. In reality, professional documentation quality depends on a broader set of abilities:

  • selecting relevant information;
  • reducing ambiguity;
  • distinguishing fact from interpretation;
  • structuring content in usable blocks;
  • using disciplined terminology;
  • maintaining consistency across multiple pages.

For that reason, the quality assurance section should be used not only during final review, but also during drafting, revision, and teacher feedback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Cross-linking is not a decorative feature. In a structured MkDocs repository, internal links perform three critical functions:

  • they improve navigation;
  • they reduce content isolation;
  • they help the reader move between concept, terminology, practice, and output.

A disciplined linking strategy turns the repository into a knowledge system rather than a collection of independent files.

docs.title: What Good Links Should Do
docs.text:
A useful internal link should help the reader do one of the following:

  • clarify terminology;
  • deepen understanding of a related concept;
  • move from theory to exercise;
  • move from exercise to model or template;
  • move from a page to the relevant quality or review guidance.

docs.title: Modules → Glossaries
docs.text: When a module introduces technical terminology, it should link to the relevant glossary page rather than redefining the same terms repeatedly.

docs.title: Modules → Exercises
docs.text: When a module develops a specific competence, it should point to the exercise page where that competence is practised.

docs.title: Exercises → Templates
docs.text: Where applicable, exercise pages should direct the learner toward a corresponding model output or reusable structure.

docs.title: Templates → Language Tools
docs.text: Template pages should link to phrasebanks and grammar/reference pages that help the learner produce better wording.

docs.title: Project Pages → Quality Assurance
docs.text: Pages related to final production, publication, and review should point to the quality assurance framework.

docs.title: Links to Avoid
docs.text:
Avoid adding links that do not support a real user need. In particular, avoid:

  • excessive linking of common words;
  • multiple links to the same target in a short span;
  • links added only to increase the number of references;
  • circular linking with no informational benefit.

docs.title: Anchor Text Guidance
docs.text: Use meaningful anchor text. The reader should understand what the target page is about before clicking.

docs.title: Good
docs.text:
• Glossary of maintenance terms
  • Phrasebank for reporting
  • Technical report template

docs.title: Weak
docs.text:
• click here
  • this page

docs.title: Reviewer Checklist
docs.text:
• Does each page contain at least a few high-value links where appropriate?
  • Are the links intentional and useful?
  • Do the links strengthen repository navigation?
  • Is there a healthy balance between self-contained writing and networked documentation?

docs.title: Strategic Principle
docs.text: A page should remain readable on its own, but it should never behave as if the rest of the repository did not exist.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This page defines the editorial conventions that should be applied across the repository. Its objective is not to impose unnecessary rigidity, but to ensure that the documentation remains readable, professional, coherent, and maintainable over time.

In an engineering-oriented knowledge base, inconsistent style quickly becomes a functional problem. It can weaken readability, blur distinctions between requirements and explanations, create terminological drift, and reduce the repository's credibility.

docs.title: 1. Clarity before ornament
docs.text: Language should be precise and functional. Prefer wording that helps the reader understand a system, process, requirement, or communication pattern without interpretive effort.

docs.title: 2. Consistency before variation
docs.text: A repository benefits more from stable formulation patterns than from stylistic variety. When a concept has already been defined and named, the same naming logic should be preserved unless there is a strong reason to revise it.

docs.title: 3. Professional neutrality
docs.text: The tone should remain professional, informative, and controlled. Avoid conversational exaggeration, emotional emphasis, and promotional wording.

docs.title: 4. Usability as an editorial criterion
docs.text: A technically correct paragraph is still weak if the reader cannot use it efficiently. Structure, headings, sequencing, scannability, and internal references are therefore part of editorial quality.

docs.title: Tone and Register
docs.text:
Use a register that is:

  • technical but accessible;
  • formal but not bureaucratic;
  • authoritative but not inflated;
  • instructional where needed;
  • descriptive where appropriate;
  • evaluative only when supported by explicit criteria.

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text:
• vague praise such as excellent, very good, highly impressive;
  • unsupported criticism such as poorly designed or weak without explanation;
  • colloquial transitions such as basically, kind of, a lot of;
  • rhetorical padding.

docs.title: Preferred features
docs.text:
• moderate sentence length;
  • explicit subject and action;
  • clear causal or logical relations;
  • visible distinction between fact, recommendation, and requirement.

docs.title: Good example
docs.text: The revised interface reduces cable routing complexity and improves inspection access during routine maintenance.

docs.title: Weak example
docs.text:
The new interface is much better and makes everything easier.

The weak version is unacceptable because it is vague, non-technical, and unsupported.

docs.title: Paragraph Design
docs.text:
Each paragraph should usually perform one primary function, such as:

  • introducing a concept;
  • defining a term;
  • explaining a process;
  • comparing two approaches;
  • describing a risk;
  • presenting a review criterion.

Avoid paragraphs that mix too many functions without clear sequencing.

docs.title: Terminology Rules
docs.text:
• Use the preferred term consistently once it has been established.
  • Avoid unnecessary synonyms for the same technical object.
  • Distinguish clearly between general-language approximations and technical terms.
  • Where a term may create confusion, define it explicitly on first use or link to the relevant glossary page.

docs.title: Requirement Language
docs.text:
When writing specifications or controlled instructions, use modal language deliberately:

  • shall for formal requirements;
  • must for strict obligation or constraint, especially where safety or compliance is involved;
  • should for recommendation;
  • may for permission or acceptable option;
  • can for capability.

These modal verbs should never be treated as interchangeable.

docs.title: Formatting Guidance
docs.text:
• Use informative headings.
  • Prefer short bulleted lists when listing criteria, steps, or comparisons.
  • Use tables only when they improve comparison or navigation.
  • Keep emphasis limited. Bold should support navigation, not decoration.
  • Use examples deliberately and label them clearly.

docs.title: Reviewer Questions
docs.text:
A reviewer applying this style guide should ask:

  • Is the page clear at first reading?
  • Does the wording remain professional throughout?
  • Are weak adjectives and vague claims avoided?
  • Does the page sound like part of the same repository as the other pages?
  • Would a technical learner or professional be able to reuse the wording?

docs.title: Final Rule
docs.text: The repository should read as if it were maintained by one disciplined editorial team, even when individual pages are drafted at different times or by different contributors.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Glossary governance ensures that terminology is controlled across the repository. In a technical English course with engineering orientation, glossaries are not supplementary vocabulary lists. They are the reference layer that stabilizes meaning, usage, register, and preferred phrasing.

Without glossary governance, the repository risks inconsistency, redundancy, weak paraphrase habits, and confusion between near-synonyms.

docs.title: Governance Objectives
docs.text:
The glossary system should:

  • provide stable definitions;
  • distinguish preferred terms from approximate or learner-generated alternatives;
  • capture usage notes relevant to engineering contexts;
  • support module pages and exercises;
  • reduce terminological drift across contributors and revisions.

docs.title: Preferred Entry Logic
docs.text:
A strong glossary entry should ideally include:

  • the term;
  • a concise definition;
  • one or more professional example sentences;
  • usage notes where confusion is likely;
  • related terms or contrasts where useful.

docs.title: Rule 1 — Use the glossary as reference, not as decoration
docs.text: If a term is important enough to appear repeatedly, it should either already exist in the glossary system or be added there with controlled wording.

docs.title: Rule 2 — Avoid unstable synonym cycling
docs.text: Do not rotate between multiple near-equivalent words merely for stylistic variation. If fault, issue, malfunction, and failure are used, each should retain its intended nuance.

docs.title: Rule 3 — Align modules with glossary language
docs.text: Where possible, modules should reuse the preferred definitions and usage patterns found in the glossary pages.

docs.title: Rule 4 — Revise rather than multiply
docs.text: If an existing entry is weak, improve it. Avoid creating duplicate or overlapping entries without strong reason.

docs.title: Review Questions
docs.text:
• Is the term defined clearly enough for B2 engineering learners?
  • Is the usage context explicit?
  • Is the term consistent with the rest of the repository?
  • Would a learner know when to use this term and when not to use it?
  • Does the entry reduce ambiguity rather than create it?

docs.title: Governance Outcome
docs.text: The glossary layer should function as the linguistic backbone of the repository. Pages may vary in subject matter, but the terminology system should remain disciplined, cumulative, and reusable.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This checklist is used to review the internal structure of a page before publication. A strong page architecture improves readability, supports navigation, and reduces the probability of content duplication or conceptual drift.

The goal is not to force every page into the same rigid template. Instead, the goal is to ensure that each page has a visible logic and performs its intended function clearly.

docs.title: Page identity
docs.text:
• Does the title clearly reflect the page topic?
  • Is the scope of the page immediately understandable?
  • Can the reader distinguish this page from related pages in the same section?

docs.title: Opening section
docs.text:
• Does the page explain its purpose near the beginning?
  • Does the opening define the value of the page for the reader?
  • Is the context clear enough for a first-time visitor?

docs.title: Internal sequencing
docs.text:
• Are sections arranged in a logical order?
  • Does the page move from overview to detail, or from principle to application, in a controlled way?
  • Are transitions clear?

docs.title: Functional completeness
docs.text:
• Does the page contain all expected elements for its type?
  • If it is a glossary page, does it define terms and usage notes?
  • If it is a module page, does it include outcomes, language focus, examples, and tasks?
  • If it is a template page, does it provide reusable structure and guidance?

docs.title: Reader usability
docs.text:
• Can the reader scan the page efficiently?
  • Are headings informative rather than generic?
  • Is excessive textual density avoided through segmentation?
  • Are lists, examples, and notes used where they improve comprehension?

docs.title: Repository integration
docs.text:
• Does the page link to relevant related pages?
  • Is the terminology aligned with the appropriate glossaries?
  • Does the page support the architecture of the repository instead of standing in isolation?

docs.title: Common Structural Failures
docs.text:
The following problems are common and should be corrected before release:

  • title too broad or too vague;
  • opening paragraph that does not define purpose;
  • duplicated concepts spread across different headings;
  • abrupt shifts from theory to exercises without transition;
  • dense blocks of text with no structural support;
  • a page that feels like notes rather than documentation.

docs.title: Minimum Viable Standard
docs.text:
Before a page is published, it should at least satisfy the following standard:

  1. clear title;
  2. clear purpose;
  3. visible section logic;
  4. coherent terminology;
  5. at least minimal internal or cross-referenced usability.

docs.title: Recommended Reviewer Comment Frame
docs.text:
When a page fails architectural review, feedback should be specific. For example:

  • "The page contains useful content, but the purpose is not defined early enough."
  • "Sections 2 and 4 overlap and should be merged or differentiated."
  • "The examples are relevant, but the reader lacks a clear progression from concept to application."
  • "Cross-links to the glossary and template pages would improve usability."

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This checklist is intended for use before publishing a page set, section update, or repository release. It is the final quality gate between drafting and public availability.

Release readiness is broader than proofreading. A page can be grammatically correct and still fail release readiness because of weak navigation, inconsistent terminology, incomplete structure, or insufficient documentation value.

docs.title: Content quality
docs.text:
• The content is complete enough for publication.
  • The page has a clear purpose and coherent scope.
  • The wording is professional, readable, and consistent.
  • Vague claims and unsupported evaluations have been removed.

docs.title: Terminology quality
docs.text:
• Key terms are aligned with the glossary pages.
  • Requirement language is used correctly.
  • No major terminology conflicts exist across related pages.

docs.title: Structural quality
docs.text:
• Headings are clear and informative.
  • The page architecture is coherent.
  • Dense sections have been segmented where needed.
  • Lists, examples, and notes support readability appropriately.

docs.title: Linking quality
docs.text:
• Relevant cross-links have been added.
  • Broken or missing internal references have been checked.
  • The page is integrated into the repository and not isolated.

docs.title: Publication quality
docs.text:
• File name and page title are appropriate.
  • Markdown formatting is stable.
  • The page works within the MkDocs navigation logic.
  • Any local conventions used in the repository have been respected.

docs.title: Section-Level Release Questions
docs.text:
If an entire section is being released or revised, ask the following:

  • Does the section have a visible internal logic?
  • Do the pages within the section complement each other without unnecessary duplication?
  • Is the section mature enough to represent the standard of the repository?
  • Are high-value entry pages, index pages, and cross-links in place?

docs.title: Recommended Final Step
docs.text: Before release, perform one last review from the perspective of a first-time visitor. Ask not only whether the content is correct, but whether it is genuinely usable, navigable, and worth consulting.

docs.title: Release Standard
docs.text:
A page should be considered release-ready only when it is:

  • technically credible;
  • linguistically stable;
  • structurally coherent;
  • terminologically governed;
  • integrated into the repository.

docs.title: Review Playbooks
docs.text:
Playbook use note

A playbook is most useful when applied in sequence. Follow the review order rather than correcting isolated sentences without a framework.

Section purpose

The playbooks in this section convert general quality expectations into repeatable review behaviour. They are especially useful during the move from draft-level classroom output to repository-level technical documentation.

docs.title: Why playbooks matter
docs.text: Many learners attempt to improve a document by making isolated sentence changes. This often produces surface-level improvement without increasing technical value. A playbook provides an ordered method: what to check first, what to standardise next, and how to decide whether the page is ready for reuse.

docs.title: Recommended review order
docs.text:
1. confirm scope and audience;
  2. test terminology consistency;
  3. verify sentence-level precision;
  4. inspect documentary structure;
  5. check internal links and repository fit;
  6. perform final style and formatting review.

docs.title: Internal links
docs.text:
• Writing Quality Playbook
  • Repository Quality Playbook
  • Release Readiness Checklist

docs.title: Repository Quality Playbook
docs.text:
Playbook use note

A playbook is most useful when applied in sequence. Follow the review order rather than correcting isolated sentences without a framework.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This playbook helps the learner and the reviewer assess whether the final MkDocs repository behaves like a professional technical knowledge base rather than a collection of disconnected assignments.

docs.title: 1. Page identity
docs.text: Each page should have a clear title, a stable function, and a visible scope.

docs.title: 2. Internal linking
docs.text: A page should not remain isolated if related glossaries, exercises, or templates exist elsewhere in the repository.

docs.title: 3. Reuse value
docs.text: The page should contain phrasing, structure, or reference logic that could be reused in later professional work.

docs.title: 4. Navigational logic
docs.text: Section names and hierarchy should help the reader predict where information lives.

docs.title: 5. Revision maturity
docs.text: A premium page usually shows evidence of at least one substantial revision pass.

docs.title: Final repository questions
docs.text:
• Can the user move from module to glossary to template without confusion?
  • Are repeated concepts explained consistently?
  • Does the site feel cumulative rather than repetitive?

docs.title: Writing Quality Playbook
docs.text:
Playbook use note

A playbook is most useful when applied in sequence. Follow the review order rather than correcting isolated sentences without a framework.

docs.title: Premium writing checks
docs.text:
A premium engineering-English page should normally satisfy the following criteria:

  • the first paragraph defines scope and purpose;
  • terminology remains stable;
  • sentence length is controlled;
  • lists are used where they improve skimmability;
  • uncertainty is marked honestly;
  • conclusions are narrower than evidence, not wider.

docs.title: Red flags
docs.text:
• decorative vocabulary with no technical gain;
  • excessive synonym variation;
  • “marketing tone” inside documentary content;
  • unsupported modal force;
  • no clear closing statement.

docs.title: Recommended workflow
docs.text: Draft quickly, then revise slowly for structure, terminology, evidence level, and repository integration.

docs.title: Teacher Notes and Correction Framework
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: Purpose of this section
docs.text:
This section is designed for course delivery, review, and quality control. It helps the instructor maintain a stable correction philosophy across modules, exercise types, and repository outputs.

In a premium course, correction should not be random. The learner should see the same principles applied consistently, especially in the following areas:

  • document purpose;
  • terminology stability;
  • evidence discipline;
  • requirement vs recommendation;
  • paragraph logic;
  • sentence economy;
  • reusable repository quality.

docs.title: Core teaching principle
docs.text: The main objective is not to make the student sound “advanced” in a decorative sense. The objective is to make the student professionally usable in written and spoken engineering English at B2 level.

docs.title: Correction stance
docs.text:
The teacher should respond to language as engineering communication. A sentence may be grammatically acceptable but still professionally weak because it:

  • mixes document functions;
  • hides responsibility or action;
  • overstates certainty;
  • uses vague adjectives;
  • fails to define scope;
  • cannot be reused in a documentation context.

docs.title: Linked resources
docs.text:
• Correction Principles
  • Common Error Patterns
  • Feedback Bank
  • Scoring Notes

docs.title: Common Error Patterns
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: Overlong sentence rhythm
docs.text: Students may place too much information into one sentence with delayed logic. This often makes the paragraph difficult to scan.

docs.title: Soft, vague adjectival style
docs.text: Examples include good, adequate, serious, important, particular, or specific without technical support.

docs.title: Literal translation of documentary verbs
docs.text: Examples include: - make a control instead of perform a check or carry out an inspection; - it is convenient to instead of it is advisable to or it is recommended to; - signalation instead of reporting or notification.

docs.title: Misuse of modal verbs
docs.text: Confusion between shall, should, must, and can is common and can seriously weaken technical documentation.

docs.title: Premature causal claims
docs.text: Students may jump from a symptom to a confirmed root cause with no evidence language in between.

docs.title: Teaching response
docs.text: These errors should be corrected systematically and linked back to document function rather than treated as isolated language faults.

docs.title: Correction Principles
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: 1. Correct for function before elegance
docs.text: A premium correction pass asks first whether the text does its job. If the sentence still fails to define scope, status, risk, action, or evidence, lexical refinement alone is not enough.

docs.title: 2. Separate observation from conclusion
docs.text:
This is one of the most important habits in engineering English. Students should learn to signal whether a sentence reports:

  • an observation;
  • a test or check;
  • an interpretation;
  • a conclusion;
  • a recommendation.

docs.title: 3. Reward stable terminology
docs.text: Students often try to vary vocabulary too much because they associate repetition with weak style. In technical writing, stable repetition is often a sign of maturity.

docs.title: 4. Penalise unsupported certainty
docs.text: Claims such as confirmed, fully compliant, resolved, or safe should be used only when the evidence boundary truly supports them.

docs.title: 5. Correct paragraph logic, not only local errors
docs.text: When a paragraph lacks purpose or sequence, fixing articles and prepositions will not solve the main documentary weakness.

docs.title: Practical correction sequence
docs.text:
1. Identify text function.
  2. Mark unstable or vague terminology.
  3. Mark evidence-level problems.
  4. Review sentence economy.
  5. Review formatting and heading logic.
  6. Suggest repository-ready revision.

docs.title: Documentation Review Notes
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: Reviewing the site as a product
docs.text:
The final repository should be reviewed as a documentation system. Premium review therefore asks:

  • Are page names consistent?
  • Is the navigation coherent?
  • Do pages link meaningfully to glossaries, templates, and exercises?
  • Can an external reader understand a page without teacher explanation?
  • Are repeated document patterns stable across modules?

docs.title: MkDocs-specific note
docs.text: Students often focus on writing individual pages but neglect information architecture. Premium-level review must assess both page quality and repository logic.

docs.title: Feedback Bank
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: Sentence-level feedback
docs.text:
• The sentence is understandable, but the documentary purpose is still unclear.
  • This statement needs a stronger distinction between observed fact and interpretation.
  • The terminology is mostly correct, but it is not yet stable across the paragraph.
  • The wording is too conversational for a reviewable engineering document.
  • The conclusion is stronger than the available evidence currently allows.
  • This paragraph would benefit from a clearer opening sentence and a more controlled closing statement.

docs.title: Paragraph-level feedback
docs.text:
• The paragraph contains useful information, but the logic is hidden.
  • The sequence should move from context to finding to implication.
  • Reduce synonym variation and keep the technical subject stable.
  • Clarify whether this is a requirement, a recommendation, or a descriptive note.

docs.title: Repository-level feedback
docs.text:
• This page can be improved before publication by tightening headings and adding internal links.
  • The content is valuable, but it still reads like coursework rather than reusable documentation.
  • Add a clearer scope note so the page can function independently within the MkDocs site.

docs.title: Oral Assessment Notes
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: What matters most in technical speaking
docs.text:
Technical speaking should be judged primarily on:

  • sequencing;
  • terminology;
  • clarity of logic;
  • evidence awareness;
  • control of scope.

The student does not need to sound theatrical. A calm, structured, engineering-like briefing is preferable to an energetic but vague presentation.

docs.title: Correction points
docs.text:
• Did the learner distinguish fact from working hypothesis?
  • Did the learner manage transitions clearly?
  • Were key terms repeated consistently?
  • Was the closing statement useful and action-oriented?

docs.title: Scoring Notes
docs.text:
Teaching use note

These notes are intended to stabilise correction behaviour across tasks, modules, and repository outputs. Use them to keep assessment principles explicit and repeatable.

docs.title: 1. Terminological control
docs.text: Can the learner select and maintain the correct engineering vocabulary?

docs.title: 2. Documentary function
docs.text: Does the text clearly behave as description, instruction, report, note, summary, or requirement set?

docs.title: 3. Evidence discipline
docs.text: Are claims proportionate to the facts presented?

docs.title: 4. Structural quality
docs.text: Are headings, paragraphs, and lists used effectively?

docs.title: 5. Professional tone
docs.text: Does the text avoid both casual language and inflated pseudo-technical style?

docs.title: 6. Repository readiness
docs.text: Could the output be inserted into the final MkDocs site after limited revision?

docs.title: Suggested interpretation scale
docs.text:
• Excellent: operationally usable with minor editorial refinement.
  • Strong: clear, disciplined, and mostly review-ready.
  • Adequate: understandable and broadly correct, but still classroom-like.
  • Developing: language control exists, but documentary quality remains inconsistent.
  • Weak: meaning is partially present, but structure and terminology are not yet reliable.
